headline: A Wasp Helped Explain What Intelligence Is Not
coreClaim: A digger wasp repeats a fixed pre-entry routine, showing intelligence requires adapting to change.
rationale: The payoff is an abstract philosophy-of-mind point, not a concrete surprise; average readers get an academic distinction, not a "wait really."
buriedGem: The wasp mechanically repeats the check-burrow routine when its food is moved.
proposedAngle: The generic "Artificial Intelligence" topic deserves a concrete flagship fact; the sphex loop is too inert to lead.

headline: An AI's Move Was So Strange Experts Called It a Mistake
coreClaim: AlphaGo's move 37 vs Lee Sedol looked like a blunder to every expert, then proved brilliant and won.
rationale: Concrete, dramatic, tellable; a machine finding a move humans missed in centuries is a genuine reversal.
buriedGem: Move 37; experts called it an error.

headline: A 1958 'Thinking Machine' Was Hyped to Soon Walk and Talk
coreClaim: Neural nets date to a 1958 machine (Perceptron) hyped to soon walk/talk/reproduce, then AI froze for decades.

buriedGem: Press predicted it would walk, talk, see, write, reproduce itself.

headline: A Robot That's 99% Human Is Creepier Than One That's 50%
coreClaim: Likability rises with human-likeness, then plunges when a face is almost-but-not-quite human (uncanny valley).
rationale: Reliably surprising to many, but a heavily circulated concept; keep/remove is a subjective call.
proposedAngle: Presentation is strong; decision is purely about over-circulation.

headline: Teaching Computers to See Was Once a Summer Homework Project
coreClaim: In 1966 an MIT professor assigned "make a computer see" as a summer project; it took 50+ years.

headline: A Translation AI Seemed to Invent Its Own Language

buriedGem: Zero-shot Japanese↔Korean via a self-made internal representation.

headline: The First Chatbot Ran on a Typewriter in 1966
coreClaim: The first chatbot, ELIZA (1966), ran via a mainframe-linked typewriter.

buriedGem: Typed on an electric typewriter wired to a mainframe.
proposedAngle: The real hook (the "ELIZA effect" — people confided in it, believing it understood) is not in the body; re-source rather than invent it.

headline: An AI Image Won an Art Contest and Outraged Artists
coreClaim: A 2022 state-fair art prize went to an AI-generated image, enraging artists.

headline: A Man in a Room Can Fake Chinese He Doesn't Speak

headline: Tiny Input Changes Can Fool AI Systems
coreClaim: Tiny, deliberately crafted input changes can make an AI misclassify an image.

proposedAngle: Topic keeps the strong lawyer fact; this row is a definition without a picture.

headline: Netflix Paid $1 Million to Improve Its Recommendations

headline: The First Internet Message Was 'Lo'

headline: A Flight Recorder's Memory Survives 3,400 G and 1,100°C

headline: The Plane With the Longest Wingspan Ever Flew Just Once

headline: Those Upturned Wingtips Were Inspired by Birds

headline: Drone Swarms Now Paint Pictures Across the Night Sky

headline: Drones Parachute Blood to Remote Clinics in Rwanda

headline: A Test Pilot's Death Changed Martin-Baker's Mission

headline: One Man Volunteered to Test the First Ejection Seat Himself

headline: Pilots Hand-Fly an Airliner Only a Few Minutes per Flight
coreClaim: Pilots hand-fly an airliner only a few minutes per flight; autopilot does most of it.

headline: Planes Can Land Themselves in Zero Visibility
coreClaim: Modern jets can land themselves ("autoland") in fog too thick for the pilot to see the runway.

headline: The First Speeding Ticket Was for Going 8 MPH

headline: That 'New Car Smell' Is Off-Gassing Chemicals

headline: Only a Quarter of Your Gas Actually Moves the Car

headline: Your Engine Sets Off Thousands of Explosions a Minute

headline: Electric Cars Were Competitive Before 1920

headline: The Self-Starter Helped Gas Cars Beat Electrics

headline: The First Hybrid Car Was Built in 1900

headline: Volvo Gave Away the Seat Belt That Saved Millions

headline: Your Seat Belt Is Built to Hold 6,000 Pounds

headline: Tires Are Black Because of a Hidden Ingredient

buriedGem: Color is the secret to strength.

headline: Cruise Control Was Invented by a Blind Engineer

headline: Formula 1 Brakes Glow Red-Hot While Stopping

headline: Some Transmissions Have No Gears at All

buriedGem: A gearbox with no gears to change.

headline: Windshield Wipers Came From a Snowy Trolley Ride

headline: A Wiper Inventor Took on Ford and Won

headline: Air Bags Inflate in Less Than 1/20th of a Second

headline: Cars Were Built Rigid Until Mercedes Made Them Fold in 1959
coreClaim: Cars were built rigid until Mercedes introduced crumple zones in 1959.

headline: Good Crash Design Sacrifices the Car to Save the People

headline: Car Horns Are Tuned to a Musical Note

buriedGem: Two horns for a richer chord.

headline: Crash Test Dummies Were Built Mostly for Men
coreClaim: Crash dummies were modeled on the "average male," leaving women more likely to be injured.

headline: Crash Test Dummies Were Invented to Replace Something Worse
coreClaim: Crash dummies replaced grimmer earlier stand-ins for crash research.

headline: Two Crash Dummies Got Into the Smithsonian
coreClaim: Crash dummies Vince and Larry became a famous safety campaign and entered the Smithsonian.
buriedGem: "You Could Learn a Lot From a Dummy."

headline: Blackbeard Set His Own Beard Smoking to Look Demonic
coreClaim: Blackbeard tucked lit slow-burning fuses into his beard to look like a smoking demon.

headline: Blackbeard's Whole Pirate Career Lasted About Two Years
coreClaim: Blackbeard was a pirate only ~2 years and may never have killed before his final fight.

headline: The Word 'Buccaneer' Comes From a Meat-Smoking Grill
coreClaim: "Buccaneer" comes from boucan, a meat-smoking grill — they began as hog hunters.

headline: A Pirate Was Knighted and Made a Colony's Governor

headline: Buccaneers Had a Price List for Lost Body Parts
coreClaim: Buccaneers agreed on a fixed "price list" of payouts for battle injuries.

buriedGem: Set rates for a lost arm, leg, eye, finger.

headline: Captain Kidd Was Hired to Catch Pirates, Then Condemned as One
coreClaim: Captain Kidd began as a state-backed pirate hunter, then was abandoned and condemned as a pirate.

headline: Captain Kidd Actually Buried Treasure, Helping Invent the Myth
coreClaim: Captain Kidd really did bury treasure — a rare true case that helped create the myth.

headline: A Red Pirate Flag Was Scarier Than the Black One

headline: Pirates Hid Behind Friendly Flags Until the Last Moment
coreClaim: Pirates flew false friendly flags, raising the Jolly Roger only once escape was impossible.

headline: Each Pirate Captain Had His Own Custom Flag Logo
coreClaim: Each captain flew a personalized flag "logo" (Blackbeard's heart-spearing skeleton, Calico Jack's cutlasses).

headline: A 'Letter of Marque' Was a License to Legally Rob Ships

headline: England's Greatest Hero Was Spain's Most Wanted Pirate

headline: Privateers, Not the Navy, Won Much of the Sea War

headline: A Pirate's Greatest Prize Was a Book of Secret Sea Charts

headline: Only One Pirate Treasure Ship Has Ever Been Authenticated

headline: A Novel Invented Almost Everything You Know About Pirates

headline: Pirate Crews Voted Like a Democracy at Sea

headline: Pirate Articles Banned Fighting and Ordered Lights Out

proposedAngle: Rewrite to marooning-as-abandonment and strip the self-harm line; if it can't survive without it, remove and trim the topic to two.

headline: The Most Powerful Pirate in History Was a Woman

headline: Nassau Was Briefly a Real 'Republic of Pirates'
coreClaim: Nassau was effectively a pirate-run "Republic of Pirates" until 1718.

headline: The 'Wickedest City on Earth' Sank Into the Sea

headline: Legend Tells of a Free Pirate Utopia Called Libertalia
coreClaim: Legend tells of "Libertalia," a free, equal pirate colony in Madagascar — likely invented but influential.

buriedGem: A pirate dream of liberty beyond kings.

headline: A Fast Pirate Ship Needed Constant Dirty Work

headline: Pirates Chose Speed Over Giant Warships

headline: Pirates Aimed to Cripple Ships, Not Sink Them

headline: Hibernating Bears Never Get Up to Use the Bathroom

headline: Some Frogs Freeze Solid and Come Back to Life

headline: One Animal Chills Its Blood Below Freezing
coreClaim: The arctic ground squirrel supercools its core below freezing without turning to ice.

headline: The Loudest Snores Rival Power Tools

headline: Playing the Didgeridoo Can Cure Snoring

headline: 'Touch Wood' May Come From a Playground Game

headline: A Rabbit's Foot Was Once Prescribed as Medicine

headline: Rabbits Seemed Magical Because of How They Live

headline: The Luckiest Foot Came With Grim Instructions

headline: One Superstition Is Really Just Good Advice

headline: Some Ants Have Farmed Fungus for 50 Million Years

headline: One Dragonfly Migrates Across an Ocean

headline: Fireflies Are Not Flies at All

coreClaim: Mosquitoes track you by exhaled CO2, body heat, and sweat chemistry — so some people get bitten more.
rationale: Concrete explanation of a real difference.

headline: One in Every Four Animal Species Is a Beetle
coreClaim: About one in four named animal species is a beetle.

headline: Dung Beetles Steer by the Milky Way
coreClaim: Dung beetles roll in a straight line by using the glow of the Milky Way as a compass.
buriedGem: Tiny hats blocked their view and lost them.

headline: One Beetle Fires a Boiling Chemical Spray
coreClaim: The bombardier beetle blasts attackers with a boiling chemical spray in machine-gun pulses.
buriedGem: Makes predators spit it back out.

headline: Detergent Beat Soap by Surviving Hard Water
coreClaim: Detergent replaced soap for laundry because it doesn't form scum in hard water.
rationale: A how-it-works explainer; weakest of three in the topic.
proposedAngle: Trim; the optical-brightener and fat-shortage facts are stronger.

headline: Detergent Makes Your Clothes Secretly Glow
coreClaim: Detergents add optical brighteners that absorb UV and glow blue, faking "whiter than white."
rationale: Concrete surprise; they literally fluoresce under blacklight.

headline: Detergent Was Invented Because of a Fat Shortage
coreClaim: Synthetic detergent was invented during WWI because soap-making fats grew scarce.
buriedGem: German chemists engineered a fat-free cleaner.

headline: Leaving the Fridge Open Actually Warms the Room
coreClaim: A fridge moves heat rather than making cold, so leaving the door open warms the room.
buriedGem: The motor adds even more heat.

headline: Einstein Co-Invented a Refrigerator With No Moving Parts
coreClaim: Einstein co-patented a refrigerator with no moving parts after a family died from a fridge gas leak.
buriedGem: Driven by heat, not a compressor.

headline: The Man Who Made Fridges Safe Also Wrecked the Ozone
coreClaim: Thomas Midgley made fridges safe with Freon (which wrecked the ozone) and also invented leaded gasoline.
buriedGem: One of history's most damaging inventing records.

headline: The Lather Is Not What Cleans Your Hands
coreClaim: Lather isn't what cleans — soap traps grime in microscopic micelles.
rationale: Concrete correction; low-foam soap cleans fine.
buriedGem: Bubbles are just a side effect.

headline: Soap Cleans Viruses by Dissolving Them Like Grease
coreClaim: Soap destroys many viruses by prying apart their fatty envelopes, like dissolving grease.

headline: The Light Switch's Genius Was Snapping Fast
coreClaim: The 1884 "quick-break" switch snaps contacts apart fast to kill a fire-starting spark — that's the satisfying click.
rationale: Concrete reframe of a familiar sensation.
buriedGem: The arc could erode metal and start fires.

headline: Two Switches Can Control One Light by a Wiring Trick
coreClaim: Two switches control one light via a "three-way" wiring trick.
rationale: Broadly familiar to anyone who's wired or noticed a hallway; mild surprise.
proposedAngle: Trim; weakest of three, and the concept is commonly understood.

headline: Light Switches Flip Opposite Ways Around the World
coreClaim: Light switches flip up for "on" in the US but down for "on" in the UK and much of the world.
buriedGem: Travelers flip exactly the wrong way.

headline: Air Conditioning Was Invented to Save Printing, Not People
coreClaim: Air conditioning was invented in 1902 to stop humidity smudging ink at a printing plant, not to cool people.
buriedGem: Cooling workers was an accidental bonus.

headline: The Big Idea Behind AC Came on a Foggy Platform
coreClaim: Carrier's humidity-control insight reportedly came on a foggy train platform.
rationale: An origin anecdote overlapping the printing-plant fact (same inventor/story).
buriedGem: Deliberately created fog to control humidity.
proposedAngle: Merge into the printing-plant fact or trim.

headline: Air Conditioning Built the Sun Belt and the Summer Blockbuster
coreClaim: Air conditioning enabled the Sun Belt population boom and the summer-blockbuster movie season.
buriedGem: Theaters were among the first to install it.

headline: A Socialite Built the First Real Dishwasher in Her Woodshed
coreClaim: A wealthy socialite, Josephine Cochran, built the first practical dishwasher in her woodshed.
rationale: Concrete, tellable; water jets, not scrubbing.
buriedGem: Fed up with servants chipping her china.

headline: The Dishwasher Was a Hit at Hotels Before Homes
coreClaim: Cochran's dishwasher sold to hotels first; homes didn't adopt it until the 1950s.
rationale: A mild adoption-history note; weakest of three.
buriedGem: Won a prize at the 1893 World's Fair.
proposedAngle: Trim; keep the invention and KitchenAid facts.

headline: The Dishwasher Company Grew Into KitchenAid
coreClaim: Cochran's company grew into the KitchenAid brand.
rationale: Concrete origin surprise for a household name.
buriedGem: A woodshed invention seeded a giant.

headline: The First Microwave Oven Was a Six-Foot Monster
coreClaim: The first microwave oven (1947 "Radarange") was ~6 feet tall, ~750 lbs, and cost ~$5,000.

headline: A Microwave Oven Was Discovered by a Melting Chocolate Bar
coreClaim: Percy Spencer discovered microwave cooking when radar melted the chocolate bar in his pocket.
buriedGem: He then exploded an egg testing it.

headline: Your Smoke Alarm Probably Contains a Radioactive Speck
coreClaim: Many smoke alarms contain a tiny, harmless speck of radioactive americium-241.
buriedGem: A nuclear ingredient on your ceiling.

headline: There Are Two Kinds of Smoke Alarm, and They Disagree
coreClaim: The two smoke-alarm types disagree: ionization catches flames fast, photoelectric catches smoldering smoke.

headline: A Cheap Smoke Alarm Roughly Halves Your Fire Risk
coreClaim: A working smoke alarm roughly halves the risk of dying in a home fire.
rationale: A worthy PSA but broadly assumed; the price-history adds little.
buriedGem: Cost hundreds of dollars in the 1960s.
proposedAngle: Trim; the radioactive-speck and two-types facts are stronger.

headline: An Asthmatic Janitor Gave Us the Word 'Hoover'
coreClaim: An asthmatic janitor invented the portable vacuum, and the buyer's name became a British verb.
buriedGem: Sold the patent to William Hoover.

headline: The Vacuum's Inventor Tested It by Sucking on a Chair
coreClaim: The vacuum's inventor tested suction by sucking dust through a handkerchief on a chair, nearly choking.

headline: People Threw 'Vacuum Parties' to Watch the Cleaning
coreClaim: Early vacuuming was a costly spectacle — society hosted "vacuum tea parties" to watch.

headline: Teflon Helped Build the Atom Bomb Before the Frying Pan
coreClaim: Teflon sealed corrosive uranium gases in the Manhattan Project before it ever coated a pan.

headline: Making Nonstick Stick to the Pan Was the Hard Part
coreClaim: Teflon is so slippery that bonding it to a pan (etching the metal) was the hard part.
buriedGem: A wife's fishing-gear suggestion cracked it.

headline: Nonstick Chemicals Became 'Forever Chemicals'
coreClaim: Nonstick chemicals (PFAS) are "forever chemicals" now in rainwater and nearly everyone's blood.
buriedGem: Prized for lasting forever, impossible to remove.

headline: Japan's 'Ama' Are Women Who Dive Into Old Age
coreClaim: Japan's "ama" women have free-dived for shellfish and pearls for ~2,000 years, often into old age.
buriedGem: Diving into their seventies and eighties.

headline: One Invention Nearly Wiped Out the Pearl-Diving Job
coreClaim: Mikimoto's cultured pearl let pearls be farmed, making dangerous natural-pearl diving nearly obsolete.
rationale: Concrete reversal of an old trade.
buriedGem: Farmed in calm water instead of hunted.

headline: A Pearling Town's Cemetery Holds Hundreds of Divers
coreClaim: Broome's pearling was so deadly its Japanese cemetery holds ~900 divers, many killed by the bends.
buriedGem: Buttons and jewelry paid for in lives.

headline: Antivenom Is Basically Purified Horse Blood
coreClaim: Antivenom is essentially purified horse blood — venom is injected into horses to harvest antibodies.
buriedGem: Refined plasma from a venom-trained horse.

headline: Snake Venom Can Be Worth More Than Gold
coreClaim: Milked snake venom can sell for thousands of dollars per gram — more than gold.

headline: One Snake Milker Survived 170 Bites and Lived to 100
coreClaim: Snake milker Bill Haast self-injected venom for decades, survived 170+ bites, and lived to 100.

headline: Almost Every Sound in a Movie Is Faked Later
coreClaim: Almost every everyday sound in a film — footsteps, clothes, doors — is performed and recorded later.
buriedGem: On-set mics catch dialogue, not much else.

headline: Jack Foley Never Won an Oscar, but the Whole Job Bears His Name
coreClaim: The job of "Foley artist" is named after Jack Foley, who never won an Oscar.
rationale: A "named after a person" history note; weakest of three.
proposedAngle: Trim; the sounds-faked-later and celery facts are stronger.

headline: Foley Artists Snap Celery to Fake Breaking Bones
coreClaim: Foley artists snap celery for breaking bones and squeeze cornstarch for footsteps in snow.
buriedGem: The most realistic sounds from groceries.

headline: Companies Pay People to Sniff Armpits All Day
coreClaim: Deodorant makers employ trained "odor judges" who sniff thousands of armpits a year.
buriedGem: Machines can't match the human nose.

headline: NASA Smells Everything Before It Flies to Space
coreClaim: NASA has a "master sniffer" who smell-tests every object before it flies, since odors are inescapable in a capsule.

headline: A Trained Nose Can Smell a Flaw in Parts Per Trillion
coreClaim: A trained nose can detect flaws like wine's cork taint at parts per trillion.
buriedGem: A few drops in an Olympic pool.

headline: NASA Pays People to Lie in Bed for 70 Days
coreClaim: NASA pays bed-rest volunteers to lie head-down for up to 70 days without ever standing.

headline: Weeks in Bed Age the Body Shockingly Fast
coreClaim: Weeks of bed rest cause rapid muscle and bone loss, like accelerated aging.
rationale: Overlaps the 70-day bed-rest fact (same NASA studies, same body effects).
proposedAngle: Merge the aging insight into the head-down fact; trim.

headline: Hotels and Bed Brands Hire Professional Sleepers
coreClaim: Mattress companies and hotels hire "professional sleepers" to test beds.
buriedGem: A real line on a resume.

headline: The Original Food Taster's Job Was to Risk Poisoning
coreClaim: The original food taster's job was to eat a ruler's meal first to check for poison.
rationale: Concrete, ancient, tellable; still used by some heads of state.
buriedGem: Falls ill or dies in the master's place.

headline: Professional Tasters Live by Strict Palate Rules
coreClaim: Professional tasters avoid perfume, smoking, and spicy food to keep their palate neutral.
rationale: Concrete but mild; weakest of three in the topic.
proposedAngle: Trim; the poison-taster and gold-spoon facts are stronger.

headline: Ice Cream Tasters Use a Gold Spoon and Insured Taste Buds
coreClaim: Ice-cream tasters use a flavorless gold spoon, and one insured his taste buds for ~$1M.
buriedGem: Gold imparts no taste and holds no cold.

headline: The Lighthouse Keeper Is Now an Almost Extinct Job
coreClaim: Automation made lighthouse keeping nearly extinct; only Boston Light still has a keeper, by act of Congress.
buriedGem: A special law preserves the one.

headline: A Lighthouse Keeper Became a Famous Lifesaver
coreClaim: Keeper Ida Lewis rowed into storms to rescue dozens; many keepers were women.
buriedGem: One of the most famous Americans of her day.

headline: Three Lighthouse Keepers Vanished Without a Trace
coreClaim: In 1900 all three keepers of Scotland's Flannan Isles lighthouse vanished without a trace.
buriedGem: A meal sat unfinished, the door shut.

headline: Tea Tasters Slurp Loudly and Spit It Out
coreClaim: Tea tasters loudly slurp and spit hundreds of cups a day to keep a brand tasting identical year-round.

headline: The US Had Official Tea Tasters for 99 Years
coreClaim: The US employed official federal tea tasters for 99 years, ending with the 1996 Repeal Act.

headline: Tea Experts Read the 'Agony of the Leaves'
coreClaim: Tea tasters read the "agony of the leaves" and can name a tea's region, altitude, and season from a sip.

headline: A Medieval Emperor Wrote a Science Book on Falconry
coreClaim: A 13th-century emperor wrote a falconry treatise so rigorous it counts as early scientific ornithology.
rationale: Concrete, surprising origin of bird science.

headline: Trained Falcons Still Work at Airports and Wimbledon
coreClaim: Trained falcons still work today — scaring birds off airport runways and pigeons off Wimbledon.
buriedGem: Vineyards and landfills hire them too.

headline: Falconry Gave English Some of Its Common Phrases
coreClaim: Falconry gave English "fed up," "hoodwinked," and "under your thumb."
buriedGem: Everyday phrases from a lost craft.

headline: Wildlife Now Thrives in the Chernobyl Exclusion Zone
coreClaim: With humans gone, the Chernobyl zone has become a haven for wolves, lynx, and wild horses.
buriedGem: Absence of people beat their presence for nature.

headline: Pripyat's New Ferris Wheel Never Officially Opened
coreClaim: Pripyat's new Ferris wheel was days from opening when the city was abandoned — it never officially ran.
buriedGem: A symbol of the ghost city.

headline: Workers in Sweden Discovered Chernobyl on Their Shoes
coreClaim: Sweden uncovered the hidden Chernobyl disaster when plant workers found radiation on their shoes.
buriedGem: Forced the Soviets to admit it.

headline: Fukushima's Seawall Was Built Too Low
coreClaim: Fukushima's ~5–6 m seawall was overwhelmed by a ~14 m tsunami that flooded its backup generators.
rationale: Concrete cause; the barrier, not the quake.
buriedGem: Generators sat low in the buildings.

headline: A Nearby Plant Survived the Same Tsunami Safely
coreClaim: A nearer plant, Onagawa, survived the same tsunami thanks to one engineer's taller seawall.

headline: Retirees Volunteered for Fukushima's Most Dangerous Work
coreClaim: Hundreds of Japanese retirees volunteered for the riskiest cleanup, reasoning radiation's cancers come too slowly to affect them.

headline: The Chicago Fire Helped Give Birth to the Skyscraper
coreClaim: Chicago's fireproof rebuild after 1871 helped spark the steel-framed skyscraper.
buriedGem: The first true skyscraper within a decade.

headline: Chicago Cleared Mrs. O'Leary's Cow After 126 Years
coreClaim: The O'Leary's-cow story was a reporter's invention; Chicago cleared her in 1997.
rationale: Concrete, tellable, overturns a famous myth.
buriedGem: 126 years of blame, then a resolution.

headline: A Deadlier Fire Burned the Same Night and Was Forgotten
coreClaim: A deadlier fire (Peshtigo) burned the same night and was overshadowed and forgotten.

headline: A 'Child of Krakatoa' Rose From the Sea Decades Later
coreClaim: Krakatoa destroyed itself in 1883, but "Anak Krakatau" (Child of Krakatoa) later rose from the sea and still erupts.
buriedGem: Its 2018 collapse triggered a deadly tsunami.

headline: Krakatoa's Tsunami Carried a Ship a Mile Inland
coreClaim: Krakatoa's ~120-foot tsunamis killed ~36,000 and carried a warship nearly a mile inland.
buriedGem: The Berouw came to rest in a jungle valley.

headline: Krakatoa's Skies May Glow in Munch's 'The Scream'
coreClaim: Krakatoa's 1883 ash turned skies blood-red worldwide, possibly inspiring the sky in Munch's "The Scream."
buriedGem: Munch described a blood-red Norway sky.

headline: About a Third of Pompeii Is Still Buried
coreClaim: About a third of Pompeii is still buried; recent digs found a preserved Roman "fast-food" counter.
buriedGem: Traces of last meals in the jars.

headline: Pompeii's Ash Preserved Perfect Hollow Molds
coreClaim: An archaeologist realized Pompeii's ash cavities were molds and poured plaster to make casts.
buriedGem: A dog still straining at its chain.

headline: Pompeii's Walls Are Covered in Ancient Graffiti
coreClaim: Pompeii's walls preserve thousands of ordinary Romans' graffiti — ads, insults, lovers' notes.

headline: A Man Confessed to the Great Fire — He Wasn't Even in London
coreClaim: A watchmaker confessed to starting the Great Fire — though his ship hadn't even reached London.

headline: The Great Fire of London Officially Killed Only Six People
coreClaim: The Great Fire leveled most of London yet officially killed only about six people.
buriedGem: The poor may have gone uncounted.

headline: Londoners Blew Up Houses to Stop the Great Fire
coreClaim: Londoners blew up houses to firebreak the Great Fire, which some believe helped end the plague.
rationale: Concrete, with a debated silver lining.

headline: Challenger Carried a Teacher, So Classrooms Watched It Live
coreClaim: Challenger carried teacher Christa McAuliffe, so schoolchildren nationwide watched the launch live.
buriedGem: A shared national moment for a generation.

headline: A Glass of Ice Water Explained the Challenger Disaster
coreClaim: A cold-stiffened O-ring doomed Challenger — Feynman showed it with a glass of ice water on live TV.

headline: Challenger Showed How Disasters Slowly Become 'Normal'
coreClaim: Challenger popularized "normalization of deviance" — treating repeated warning signs as acceptable.
rationale: Concrete concept taught far beyond NASA.

headline: A Wave of Molasses Raced Through Boston at 35 MPH
coreClaim: Boston's 1919 molasses flood sent a ~25-foot wave at ~35 mph, killing 21.

headline: Boston Smelled of Molasses for Decades Afterward
coreClaim: Locals said Boston smelled of molasses on hot days for decades afterward.
buriedGem: The harbor ran brown for months.

headline: A Molasses Tank Was Painted Brown to Hide Its Leaks
coreClaim: The molasses tank was so shoddy the company painted it brown to hide its leaks.
buriedGem: Blamed anarchists, but it was bad engineering.

headline: The Hydrogen-Filled Hindenburg Had a Smoking Room
coreClaim: The hydrogen-filled Hindenburg had a sealed, pressurized smoking room.
buriedGem: A single electric lighter, watched by a steward.

headline: Most People Aboard the Hindenburg Actually Survived
coreClaim: Despite the famous fireball, 62 of 97 people aboard the Hindenburg survived.

headline: The Hindenburg Burned Because the US Withheld Helium
coreClaim: The Hindenburg used flammable hydrogen because the US barred helium exports to Nazi Germany.
rationale: Concrete; a geopolitical cause of the fire.
buriedGem: The US controlled nearly all helium.

headline: One Man Drew the Infinity Symbol in 1655
coreClaim: The infinity symbol was one man's invention — John Wallis, 1655.
buriedGem: Maybe from a Roman numeral for "countless."

headline: The Infinity Symbol Is a Real Curve You Can Draw From an Equation
coreClaim: The infinity sign is a real curve (the lemniscate) traced by an equation.
rationale: Mildly technical but concrete; a proper name and math behind the doodle.

headline: The Peace Sign Is Two Hidden Flag Signals
coreClaim: The peace sign encodes the semaphore letters N and D — for nuclear disarmament.

headline: The Peace Sign Is a Person in Despair
coreClaim: Holtom designed the peace sign as a despairing human figure and never copyrighted it.
buriedGem: He wished he'd drawn arms raised in hope.

headline: Some Americans Called the Peace Sign Satanic
coreClaim: Cold War conservatives smeared the peace sign as a "broken cross" or Communist symbol.

headline: The Tiny Dots Are the Whole Point of Yin-Yang
coreClaim: The small dots are the real point of yin-yang — nothing is ever purely one force.
rationale: Concrete reframe of a familiar symbol.
buriedGem: A seed of the opposite in each side.

headline: Yin and Yang Started as a Hillside
coreClaim: Yin and yang originally meant the shady and sunny sides of a hill.

headline: The Yin-Yang Swirl Is Younger Than the Idea
coreClaim: The classic yin-yang swirl (taijitu) is ~1,000 years old — far younger than the concept.
buriedGem: Idea twice as old as its logo.

headline: A College Student Drew the Recycling Symbol in Days
coreClaim: A 23-year-old student drew the recycling symbol in days, and it was never trademarked.

headline: The Number Inside the Arrows Is Not a Promise
coreClaim: The number inside the arrows identifies plastic type, not recyclability.
buriedGem: The symbol you trust tells you the least.

headline: Recycling Has a Third Arrow Everyone Forgets
coreClaim: The third recycling arrow is "buy recycled" — the step everyone skips.
buriedGem: The loop never closes without it.

headline: The Heart Shape May Come From a Lost Plant
coreClaim: The heart shape may descend from silphium, a plant the Romans loved to extinction.

headline: Aristotle Thought the Heart Did the Thinking
coreClaim: Aristotle thought the heart did the thinking; our language still agrees.
rationale: Concrete; "learn by heart" is a 2,000-year-old mistake.

headline: A 1977 Logo Turned the Heart Into a Verb
coreClaim: Milton Glaser's 1977 I♥NY logo turned the heart shape into the verb "love."
rationale: Concrete origin of a global format.

headline: The Ampersand Is Just Two Glued Letters
coreClaim: The ampersand is a fused "e" and "t" — Latin "et," meaning "and."

headline: The Ampersand Was Once the 27th Letter
coreClaim: "Ampersand" comes from kids slurring "and per se and" at the alphabet's end.
buriedGem: A recitation worn into one word.

headline: Ireland Still Uses a 2,000-Year-Old 'And'
coreClaim: Ireland still uses the Tironian et (a Roman shorthand "and") on road signs.

headline: Poison Got a Friendlier Face Called Mr. Yuk
coreClaim: "Mr. Yuk" was invented in 1971 because kids found the skull symbol appealing.

headline: Old Poison Bottles Warned You in the Dark
coreClaim: Victorian poison came in ridged cobalt-blue bottles you could identify by feel in the dark.

headline: A Smiling Winged Skull Watched Over Early America
coreClaim: The winged "death's-head" skull was the most common carving in early American graveyards.
rationale: Concrete cultural surprise; death as historical theme.

headline: A Check Mark Means 'Wrong' in Some Countries
coreClaim: In Japan and other countries a check mark can mean an answer is wrong.
buriedGem: Correct is marked with a circle (maru).

headline: The Check Mark May Trace Back to the Latin Word for Truth
coreClaim: The check mark may trace to the Latin "veritas" (a V for verified).
rationale: A hedged etymology with no famous inventor; weakest of three.
proposedAngle: Trim; the "means wrong abroad" and X-for-agreement facts are stronger.

headline: People Signed With an X Long Before the Check
coreClaim: People signed an X to mean "I agree" — the root of X for a kiss.
rationale: Concrete; the affirmative X predates the check.
buriedGem: X still a binding signature and a kiss.

headline: The Greek Question Mark Is a Semicolon
coreClaim: Greek ends questions with a semicolon and uses a raised dot for a pause.
buriedGem: A string of questions can look like a list.

headline: Spanish Warns You a Question Is Coming
coreClaim: Spanish opens questions with an upside-down mark (official since 1754).
rationale: Concrete; punctuation that works at the start.
buriedGem: Brace for the rising tone from word one.

headline: The Question Mark May Be a Squished Word
coreClaim: The question mark may be the Latin "quaestio" shrunk to "qo" and stacked.
buriedGem: A word compressed into a squiggle.

headline: The Dollar Sign Is Older Than the Dollar
coreClaim: The $ is older than the dollar — it evolved from merchants' shorthand for the Spanish peso.

headline: The Word 'Dollar' Comes From a Czech Valley
coreClaim: "Dollar" descends from the "thaler," coins from a valley in today's Czech Republic.

headline: The Dollar Sign May Be Two Greek Pillars
coreClaim: A rival theory says the $ comes from the Pillars of Hercules on Spanish coins.

headline: A Cereal Ad Hid a Caesar Cipher in Every Box
coreClaim: Cereal-box "secret decoder" rings were Caesar ciphers, spoofed in A Christmas Story.

headline: Rome's Greatest General Used a Laughably Weak Code
coreClaim: Julius Caesar's cipher shifted letters by three — crackable today in seconds.
buriedGem: Augustus used an even flimsier shift of one.

headline: One Cipher Conveniently Unlocks Itself
coreClaim: ROT13 unlocks itself — applying the 13-shift twice restores the text.

headline: A Good Code Should Work Even If the Enemy Has It
coreClaim: Kerckhoffs's principle: a cipher must stay secure even if the enemy knows the whole method.
buriedGem: Strength lives in the key alone.

headline: Codebreaking Was Invented in a Baghdad Library
coreClaim: Codebreaking was invented ~9th century in Baghdad by al-Kindi via frequency analysis.

headline: Two Strangers Can Agree on a Secret in Public
coreClaim: Public-key crypto lets two strangers create a shared secret over an open channel.
rationale: Concrete; why browsers can trust sites.
buriedGem: Shout numbers, still share a secret.

headline: Enigma's Fatal Flaw Was What It Could Not Do
coreClaim: Enigma's fatal flaw: it could never encrypt a letter as itself.
buriedGem: Ruled out huge numbers of settings.

headline: Poland Cracked Enigma Years Before Bletchley
coreClaim: Polish mathematician Rejewski broke Enigma in the 1930s, years before Bletchley.
buriedGem: Handed the breakthrough to Britain in 1939.

headline: The Enigma Secret Was Kept for Almost 30 Years
coreClaim: Breaking Enigma may have shortened WWII by two years — kept secret until the 1970s.

headline: France Had a Working Internet in the 1790s
coreClaim: France built a working "internet" of signal towers in the 1790s.
buriedGem: A message crossed France in minutes.

headline: The First Data Hack Used a Telegraph Network
coreClaim: In the 1830s the Blanc brothers bribed semaphore operators to smuggle stock data — an early data hack.
buriedGem: Market news days ahead for two years.

headline: Nelson Rallied His Fleet With a Line of Flags
coreClaim: At Trafalgar (1805) Nelson flag-signaled "England expects that every man will do his duty."
buriedGem: A fleet turned into a sentence.

headline: An Ancient Secret Was Tattooed on a Slave's Head
coreClaim: Histiaeus tattooed a secret message on a slave's shaved scalp, hidden once hair regrew.
rationale: Concrete, vivid; the messenger was the secret.
buriedGem: Head shaved again to reveal it.

headline: Spies Shrank Whole Documents to a Tiny Dot
coreClaim: Spies shrank whole documents to a microdot the size of a printed period.

headline: Your Printer Hides Secret Dots on Every Page
coreClaim: Color printers secretly add near-invisible yellow dots encoding serial number, date, and time.

headline: The Pigpen Cipher Looks Secret but Barely Is
coreClaim: The Pigpen cipher looks cryptic but is broken in minutes by frequency analysis.
rationale: Concrete; appearance is its only defense.
buriedGem: A curious child can solve it.

headline: Freemasons Carved This Cipher on Their Graves
coreClaim: Freemasons used Pigpen so heavily that some Masonic gravestones are carved in it.

headline: Civil War Prisoners Passed Notes in Pigpen
coreClaim: Union prisoners used Pigpen in Confederate camps — just a pencil and a memorized grid.

headline: One Cipher Is Mathematically Proven Unbreakable
coreClaim: The one-time pad is the only cipher mathematically proven unbreakable — if its strict rules hold.
buriedGem: Key as long as the message, used once.

headline: Eerie Radio Voices Still Read Numbers to Spies
coreClaim: Shortwave "numbers stations" still read digit strings believed to be one-time-pad spy messages.

headline: The Soviets Got Lazy and America Read Their Mail
coreClaim: Soviet clerks reused one-time pads, and the Venona project exploited the slip to unmask spies.
rationale: Concrete; perfect cipher undone by human shortcuts.

headline: A Coded Message Promises Buried Treasure No One Can Find
coreClaim: The Beale ciphers point to buried treasure; one solved via the Declaration, two still unbroken.

headline: Benedict Arnold Betrayed America With a Book Code
coreClaim: Benedict Arnold hid his treason in a book cipher of page/line/word numbers.

headline: A Book Cipher's Power Is an Ordinary Book
coreClaim: A book cipher's innocent key is also its weakness — guess the book and it all unravels.
rationale: A how-it-works explainer overlapping the Arnold fact's mechanism.
buriedGem: A common novel as the key.
proposedAngle: Trim; the Beale and Arnold facts carry the topic.

headline: A Wooden Stick Was One of the First Codes
coreClaim: The Spartan scytale hid messages on a strip wrapped around a rod of a specific thickness.
buriedGem: The stick's size was the key.

headline: Historians Doubt the Famous Stick Cipher Worked
coreClaim: Some historians doubt the scytale worked as a cipher — it may have just verified messengers.
buriedGem: Too clumsy to keep real secrets.

headline: The Navajo Code Was Never Broken
coreClaim: The Navajo code used in WWII was never broken.
buriedGem: Unwritten, unlike anything Japan trained on.

headline: The Army Once Punished the Language That Won Battles
coreClaim: Many Navajo code talkers had been punished as children for speaking the language that later won battles.
buriedGem: The tongue the government tried to erase.

headline: Code Talkers Invented Words for Tanks and Subs
coreClaim: Code talkers coined terms like "iron fish" for submarine and beat coding machines' speed.
buriedGem: ~20 seconds vs a machine's half hour.

headline: The 'Real Smile' Is Named After a Doctor Who Shocked Faces
coreClaim: The genuine "Duchenne smile" is named after a doctor who mapped it by electrically shocking faces.
buriedGem: Eye-crinkle muscles are hard to fake.

headline: Babies Smile Before They Are Even Born
coreClaim: Fetuses smile in the womb and blind-born children smile normally — smiling is innate.

headline: A Smile Is Not Friendly Everywhere
coreClaim: In Russia and some cultures, smiling at strangers can seem insincere or foolish.
buriedGem: A proverb equates it with stupidity.

headline: Staring Into a Stranger's Eyes Can Spark Love
coreClaim: 36 personal questions plus four minutes of eye contact can spark real intimacy.
rationale: Concrete, tellable; a pair later married.
buriedGem: Short-circuits the slow build of closeness.

headline: You Look Away Because Eye Contact Is Hard Work
coreClaim: People break eye contact on hard questions because gazing competes with deep thinking.

headline: Your Pupils Give Away What You Like
coreClaim: Pupils secretly widen at what interests you; Renaissance women used belladonna to fake it.

headline: Your Sense of Personal Space Lives in One Brain Spot
coreClaim: A woman whose amygdala was destroyed felt no discomfort at any distance — where personal space lives.

headline: Conversations Can Turn Into a Slow Dance
coreClaim: When cultures with different space norms talk, one advances and the other retreats in a slow dance.

headline: The Handshake May Have Begun as a Weapon Check
coreClaim: The handshake may have begun as a weapon check — an empty hand, shaken to dislodge hidden blades.

headline: A Handshake Was Carved in Stone Nearly 3,000 Years Ago
coreClaim: A handshake appears on a 9th-century BCE Assyrian relief sealing an alliance.
buriedGem: Shalmaneser III and a Babylonian ruler.

headline: The Handshake Is a Germ Superhighway
coreClaim: A handshake spreads about twice the bacteria of a high-five.
buriedGem: Some doctors want them banned in hospitals.

headline: An Isolated Tribe Proved Some Faces Are Universal
coreClaim: Ekman found the isolated Fore people read core facial expressions the same as Westerners.
buriedGem: Same faces for happiness, anger, fear, disgust.

headline: A Blank Face Can Distress a Baby in Minutes
coreClaim: In the "still face" experiment, a mother going blank distresses her baby within minutes.
buriedGem: A blank face is not neutral — it alarms.

headline: Blind Athletes Make Faces They Have Never Seen
coreClaim: Congenitally blind athletes show the same triumph and shame body language as sighted ones.

headline: Standing Like a Superhero Sparked a Science War
coreClaim: "Power posing" claimed a superhero stance boosts confidence, then became a replication battle.
buriedGem: The hormone claims failed to reproduce.

headline: Your Phone Puts 60 Pounds on Your Neck
coreClaim: Tilting your head to a phone can load your neck with up to ~60 pounds of force.
buriedGem: Like a small child hanging from your neck.

headline: Fidgeting Can Quietly Burn Off a Meal
coreClaim: Constant fidgeting (NEAT) can burn hundreds of extra calories a day.

headline: The Same Hand Sign Can Be a Grave Insult Abroad
coreClaim: A thumbs-up or "OK" sign is a crude insult in some countries.

headline: Even Blind People Gesture With Their Hands While Talking
coreClaim: Even people blind from birth gesture while speaking — including to other blind people.
rationale: Concrete; gestures help the speaker think.
buriedGem: Your hands work for your own brain.

headline: Italian Hand Gestures Are Almost a Language
coreClaim: Italians share a documented vocabulary of hundreds of specific hand gestures.
buriedGem: A detailed exchange across a piazza, wordless.

headline: Most Laughter Has Nothing to Do With Jokes
coreClaim: Most real-life laughter follows ordinary remarks, not jokes — and speakers laugh most.

headline: Rats Giggle When You Tickle Them
coreClaim: Tickled rats chirp with laughter and chase the hand; apes laugh during play.
buriedGem: Chirps too high for us to hear.

headline: A Laughing Epidemic Once Shut Down Schools
coreClaim: A 1962 contagious-laughter outbreak in Tanganyika spread through schools and forced closures.
buriedGem: Fits lasted hours; schools shut for weeks.

headline: A Hit TV Show Was Built on Microexpressions
coreClaim: The show Lie to Me was built on microexpressions, but real lie-detection is far less reliable.
rationale: Lands the same "reading faces to catch liars fails" point as the $1B-program fact in this topic.
buriedGem: A twitch is not a confession.
proposedAngle: Keep the more concrete $1B-program fact; consider trimming this softer version.

headline: Some Expressions Vanish in a 25th of a Second
coreClaim: Microexpressions can flash in as little as 1/25th of a second — too fast to consciously notice.
buriedGem: Leak an emotion before composure returns.

headline: A Program to Read Faces Cost Nearly $1 Billion
coreClaim: The US spent nearly $1B training screeners to spot liars by their faces — auditors found it no better than chance.
buriedGem: Theory beat reality in a crowded airport.

headline: Crossing Your Arms Can Actually Reduce Pain
coreClaim: Crossing your arms can reduce felt pain by scrambling the brain's map of your hands.
buriedGem: Hands on the "wrong" sides weaken the signal.

headline: Folded Arms Can Make You More Stubborn
coreClaim: People who crossed their arms persisted longer on hard (secretly impossible) puzzles.

headline: You Always Fold Your Arms the Same Way
coreClaim: Everyone folds their arms the same way, and reversing it feels bizarrely wrong.

headline: Buttons Waited Thousands of Years for the Buttonhole
coreClaim: Buttons existed for millennia as ornaments until the 13th-century buttonhole made them fasteners.
buriedGem: Ancient Indus Valley buttons were just seals.

headline: Buttons Were Once Luxuries Restricted by Law
coreClaim: Buttons were once such luxuries that sumptuary laws restricted who could wear them.

headline: Men's and Women's Buttons Are on Opposite Sides
coreClaim: Men button on the right, women on the left — likely because wealthy women were dressed by servants.
buriedGem: Placed for the person facing the wearer.

headline: The Fork Was Once Condemned as Sinful
coreClaim: When forks reached Europe, clergy condemned them as sinful; they took ~600 years to accept.
buriedGem: A noblewoman's illness called divine punishment.

headline: Dinner Knives Are Round-Tipped Because of a Cardinal
coreClaim: Dinner knives are round-tipped because Cardinal Richelieu had the points filed off to stop tooth-picking.
buriedGem: Pointed knives doubled as toothpicks and weapons.

headline: The Victorians Had a Special Fork for Everything
coreClaim: Victorians had dozens of specialized forks — oyster, pickle, berry, pastry.
buriedGem: Which fork to use tested your class.

headline: Clear Mirrors May Have Changed How We See Ourselves
coreClaim: Clear glass mirrors may have sharpened the sense of self, linked to self-portraits and diaries.
buriedGem: Before, reflections were dim and distorted.

headline: Venice's Mirror Makers Were Forbidden to Leave
coreClaim: Venice forbade its mirror-makers to leave on pain of death, reputedly sending assassins after defectors.

headline: Almost No Animals Recognize Themselves in a Mirror
coreClaim: Almost no animals recognize themselves in a mirror — only apes, dolphins, elephants, magpies, one fish.

headline: There Has Never Been Any Lead in a Pencil
coreClaim: Pencil "lead" is graphite (carbon) — there's never been any lead in a pencil.
rationale: Reliably surprising; concrete history of the misnomer.

headline: England Guarded Its Pencil Graphite Like Treasure
coreClaim: England guarded its uniquely pure Borrowdale pencil graphite with armed guards and a flooded mine.

headline: A Single Pencil Can Draw a Line 35 Miles Long
coreClaim: One pencil can draw a ~35-mile line; Thoreau helped perfect the graphite-clay grade recipe.
rationale: Concrete scale plus a famous name.

headline: The Umbrella Began as a Sunshade for Royalty
coreClaim: For millennia the umbrella was a royal sunshade, not a rain tool.
buriedGem: Held over pharaohs and holy figures.

headline: A Man Was Mocked for 30 Years for Using an Umbrella
coreClaim: Jonas Hanway was mocked for ~30 years for carrying an umbrella in 1750s London.
buriedGem: Coach drivers jeered at lost fares.

headline: The Umbrella Still Hasn't Been Perfected
coreClaim: Despite an 1850s steel frame, umbrellas still flip inside-out and resist being perfected.
rationale: A soft "isn't it funny they still break" observation; weakest of three in the topic.
proposedAngle: Trim; the sunshade-origin and Hanway facts are stronger.

headline: One Company Makes About Half the World's Zippers
coreClaim: One Japanese firm (YKK) makes about half the world's zippers.
buriedGem: It even smelts its own brass.

headline: The Zipper Was a Flop for About 20 Years
coreClaim: The zipper flopped for ~20 years until the military, then fashion, adopted it.

headline: The Zipper Is Named After a Sound on Boots
coreClaim: "Zipper" came from B.F. Goodrich overshoes named for the zzzip sound.
buriedGem: An onomatopoeia became the object's name.

headline: An Ancient Chewing Stick Still Cleans Teeth Today
coreClaim: The ancient "miswak" chew stick still cleans teeth today and carries real antibacterial compounds.
rationale: Concrete; proven, still used by millions.

headline: Toothbrushes Used Hog Hair Until 1938
coreClaim: Toothbrushes used coarse hog-neck bristles until DuPont's nylon replaced them in 1938.

headline: People Voted the Toothbrush Their Most Essential Invention
coreClaim: In one survey Americans named the toothbrush the invention they could least live without — over the car and computer.

headline: Scientists Studied Why Shoelaces Come Untied
coreClaim: A 2017 study found footstep impact plus the leg's swing yanks shoelace knots loose.
rationale: Concrete; real physics behind a mystery.

headline: A Laced Shoe Survived 5,500 Years in a Cave
coreClaim: The 5,500-year-old Areni-1 shoe — older than the pyramids — was laced with a leather cord.
buriedGem: Stuffed with grass, preserved by dung.

headline: A Man Traded One Red Paperclip Up to a House
coreClaim: Starting from one red paperclip, Kyle MacDonald traded up to a house in ~14 swaps.

headline: The Most Famous Paper Clip Has No Known Inventor
coreClaim: The iconic Gem paper clip has no known inventor and has barely changed in 130+ years.

headline: Norwegians Wore Paper Clips to Defy the Nazis
coreClaim: Occupied Norwegians wore paper clips to defy the Nazis after patriotic pins were banned.

headline: An 'Unpickable' Lock Was Picked at an 1851 Fair
coreClaim: An "unpickable" Bramah lock was picked at the 1851 Great Exhibition, proving any lock can be opened.

headline: Your House Key Runs on 4,000-Year-Old Technology
coreClaim: Your house key runs on ~4,000-year-old Egyptian pin-tumbler technology.
buriedGem: Yale just miniaturized it into metal.

headline: Your Key Might Open a Stranger's Lock by Accident
coreClaim: Mass-produced keys share limited cuts, so a key can occasionally open a stranger's lock by chance.
buriedGem: Security as an illusion of mass production.

headline: In Italy, Battlement Shapes Showed Your Politics
coreClaim: In medieval Italy, merlon shape signaled politics — square for the Pope, swallowtail for the Emperor.

headline: Pouring Boiling Oil From Castles Is Mostly a Myth
coreClaim: Pouring boiling oil from castles is mostly a myth — defenders dropped rocks, hot water, and sand.
buriedGem: Oil was too expensive to waste.

headline: You Needed Royal Permission to Add Battlements
coreClaim: Adding battlements in England required a royal "licence to crenellate."

headline: The Castle Gate Became Its Strongest Point, Not Its Weakest
coreClaim: Castles fortified the gate so heavily the gatehouse became the strongest part — sometimes the lord's home.
buriedGem: The weakest point became the safest.

headline: A Counterweight Let One Person Raise a Drawbridge
coreClaim: Counterweighted drawbridges were so balanced one or two people could raise them in seconds.
buriedGem: Same principle as modern lift bridges.

headline: Himeji Castle's Path Is a Maze to Trap Attackers
coreClaim: Himeji Castle's approach is a deliberate maze, and a WWII bomb that hit it failed to explode.
rationale: Concrete design plus a striking survival.

headline: Japanese Castles Were Built to Ride Out Earthquakes
coreClaim: Japanese castles set flexible wooden keeps on mortarless sloping stone bases to survive quakes.
buriedGem: Built to move with the shaking.

headline: Some Castle Floors Were Built to Squeak on Purpose
coreClaim: "Nightingale floors" were built to squeak underfoot to betray intruders.
rationale: Concrete, vivid; the building as alarm.
buriedGem: Chirped no matter how lightly you tread.

headline: Normans Conquered England by Building Castles in Days
coreClaim: The Normans held England by throwing up motte-and-bailey castles in days.
buriedGem: Hundreds of quick forts, not one great one.

headline: Many Castles Were Deliberately Wrecked After Wars
coreClaim: Many castle ruins exist because victors deliberately "slighted" (wrecked) them.
rationale: Concrete; ruins by demolition, not time.

headline: A Legendary Crusader Castle Fell to a Forged Letter
coreClaim: The mighty Krak des Chevaliers reportedly surrendered in 1271 to a forged letter, not force.
buriedGem: A fake order from their own commander.

headline: Attackers Had to Fill the Moat Before Rolling the Tower
coreClaim: Before a siege tower could reach the wall, attackers had to fill the moat under fire.

headline: Siege Towers Were Huge Gambles That Often Burned
coreClaim: Wooden siege towers often burned, so crews covered them in wet animal hides.

headline: A Failed Siege Tower Helped Build the Colossus of Rhodes
coreClaim: Rhodes survived the giant "Helepolis" siege tower and sold the leftover gear to fund the Colossus of Rhodes.

headline: One Engineer Turned Sieges Into Predictable Math
coreClaim: Engineer Vauban made sieges so systematic he could predict the day a fort would fall.

headline: The Age of the Fortress Ended When Armies Drove Around It
coreClaim: France's modern Maginot Line was made useless in 1940 when Germany simply drove around it.
rationale: Concrete, ironic end of an era.

headline: Cannons Forced Forts Into Strange Star Shapes
coreClaim: Cannons forced forts into low, angled "star" shapes with no blind spots.
buriedGem: Still visible from the air today.

headline: The Word 'Dungeon' Once Meant the Grandest Tower
coreClaim: "Dungeon" comes from "donjon," which meant the grandest tower where the lord lived.

headline: Captured Nobles Were Ransomed, Not Thrown in a Cell
coreClaim: Medieval castles rarely jailed people long-term; captured nobles were held comfortably and ransomed.
buriedGem: A prisoner might dine with the lord.

headline: Many 'Oubliettes' Were Really Just Storage Pits
coreClaim: Many "oubliettes" shown to tourists were really cisterns or storerooms.

headline: Britain and Ireland Have Over 4,000 Hillforts
coreClaim: Britain and Ireland hold over 4,000 Iron Age hillforts — strongholds long before castles.

headline: Some Hillforts May Have Been Built to Show Off
coreClaim: Many hillforts seem impractical for defense — some may have been built for status or gatherings.

headline: Britain's 'Maiden Castle' Is No Castle at All
coreClaim: Britain's "Maiden Castle" is no castle but a hillfort enclosing ~50 football fields.

headline: Many Castle Keeps Had No Ground-Floor Door
coreClaim: Many castle keeps had no ground-floor door — entry was a second-floor door via a removable stair.
buriedGem: Destroy the stair, strand the entrance.

headline: Round Keeps Were Invented to Defeat Tunnelers
coreClaim: Castles switched to round keeps because square corners could be tunneled under and collapsed.
buriedGem: Round walls have no corner to undermine.

headline: Castle Keeps Had Rooms Built Inside Their Walls
coreClaim: Keep walls were so thick that stairs, passages, and rooms were built inside the stone itself.
buriedGem: Walls with their own miniature rooms.

headline: Castle Moats Were Often Stinking Sewers
coreClaim: Many castle moats were foul, waste-filled sewers, not clear water.
buriedGem: The stench was its own deterrent.

headline: A Moat's Real Job Was Often to Stop Tunneling
coreClaim: A water-filled moat's key job was often to flood and foil tunnelers, not just block attackers.
buriedGem: A shield against the unseen threat.

headline: Plenty of Castle Moats Held No Water at All
coreClaim: Many moats held no water — dry ditches, and the dug-out earth built the ramparts.
rationale: Concrete; excavating the defense built it.

headline: The Biggest Dinosaurs Hatched From Surprisingly Small Eggs
coreClaim: Even giant dinosaurs hatched from eggs no bigger than a football — shells can't thicken without suffocating the embryo.
buriedGem: A cradle-sized start to a 10-ton adult.

headline: A Dinosaur Named 'Egg Thief' Was Actually a Caring Parent
coreClaim: Oviraptor, named "egg thief," was later shown to be brooding its own eggs.
buriedGem: An embryo found inside "its" stolen egg.

headline: One Dinosaur's Name Means 'Good Mother Lizard'
coreClaim: A Montana nesting site gave us "Maiasaura" — "good mother lizard" — evidence of parental care.
buriedGem: Worn baby teeth suggest adults fed them.

headline: Ankylosaurus Swung a Tail Club That Could Break Bone
coreClaim: Ankylosaurus could swing its fused bony tail club hard enough to shatter a predator's leg bone.

headline: An Armored Dinosaur Was Found With Its Camouflage Intact
coreClaim: A superbly preserved armored dinosaur (Borealopelta) still shows reddish camouflage coloring.
rationale: Concrete; even a tank needed to hide.

headline: Even Ankylosaurus's Eyelids Were Armored
coreClaim: Ankylosaurus was armored even on its eyelids, yet ate plants with small leaf-shaped teeth.

headline: Brachiosaurus Needed Blood Pressure No Animal Alive Can Match
coreClaim: To pump blood up its huge neck, Brachiosaurus needed blood pressure beyond any living animal.
buriedGem: Debated whether it held its neck fully up.

headline: Brachiosaurus Swallowed Its Food Without Chewing
coreClaim: Brachiosaurus raked leaves and swallowed them whole, fermenting them in a huge gut.

headline: Brachiosaurus Had Hollow, Air-Filled Bones Like a Bird
coreClaim: Brachiosaurus saved weight with hollow, air-filled bones — the same trick birds use.

headline: A Thin Layer of Rare Metal Cracked the Dinosaur Mystery
coreClaim: A worldwide iridium layer (found 1980) pointed to an asteroid ending the dinosaurs.
rationale: Concrete; the clue that cracked it.
buriedGem: Iridium is rare on Earth, common in asteroids.

headline: The Dinosaurs Died Because the Asteroid Hit the Worst Spot
coreClaim: The dino-killing asteroid hit sulfur-rich rock at Chicxulub, worsening the sky-darkening.
buriedGem: Hitting elsewhere might have been survivable.

headline: One Fossil Site May Record the Very Day the Asteroid Hit
coreClaim: The Tanis site in North Dakota may capture the actual day of the asteroid impact.

headline: We've Probably Found Only a Fraction of All Dinosaurs
coreClaim: Fossilization is so rare that many dinosaurs are known from a single bone.
buriedGem: Each skeleton stands in for countless others.

headline: Scientists Found Soft Tissue Inside a T. rex Bone
coreClaim: Researchers found flexible, vessel-like soft tissue inside a 66-million-year-old T. rex bone.

headline: Two Rivals Waged a Dynamite-Fueled 'Bone War'
coreClaim: The 1800s "Bone Wars" saw rivals Cope and Marsh spy, bribe, and dynamite dig sites.

headline: Scientists Held a Dinner Party Inside a Dinosaur
coreClaim: In 1853 scientists held a New Year's Eve dinner party seated inside a hollow Iguanodon model.
buriedGem: Toasting from the belly of a dinosaur.

headline: Iguanodon's Thumb Spike Spent Decades on Its Nose
coreClaim: Iguanodon's thumb spike was mistaken for a nose horn and mounted on its snout for decades.
buriedGem: Walking museums with a thumb on its face.

headline: Iguanodon Was Named for a Lizard It Wasn't Related To
coreClaim: Iguanodon ("iguana tooth") helped Owen coin "Dinosauria," though dinosaurs aren't lizards.

headline: Stegosaurus Ran a Bus-Sized Body on a Walnut-Sized Brain
coreClaim: Stegosaurus ran a bus-sized body on a walnut-sized brain (spawning the debunked "second brain").
buriedGem: The hip space wasn't a brain.

headline: Stegosaurus's Tail Spikes Are Named After a Cartoon
coreClaim: Stegosaurus's tail spikes are officially called the "thagomizer" — from a 1982 Far Side cartoon.
rationale: Concrete, delightful; a gag became real anatomy.

headline: Nobody Is Sure What Those Stegosaurus Plates Were For
coreClaim: No one is sure what Stegosaurus's iconic back plates were for.
rationale: Concrete; its most famous feature is a mystery.

headline: A Triceratops Skull Was Nearly a Third of Its Whole Body
coreClaim: A Triceratops skull could reach ~8 feet — nearly a third of its whole body.
buriedGem: Among the largest heads of any land animal.

headline: Fossils Prove T. rex and Triceratops Really Fought
coreClaim: Fossils show real T. rex vs Triceratops combat — including bite marks a Triceratops survived.
rationale: Concrete; the movie showdown was real.
buriedGem: A healed, bitten frill and horn.

headline: A Triceratops May Have Just Been a Young 'Torosaurus'
coreClaim: Some scientists think "Torosaurus" is just a fully mature Triceratops.

headline: T. rex Lived Closer to Us Than to Stegosaurus
coreClaim: T. rex lived closer in time to us than to Stegosaurus.
buriedGem: Stegosaurus gone ~80M years before T. rex.

headline: T. rex Bit So Hard It Crushed and Digested Bone
coreClaim: T. rex bit hard enough to crush and digest bone — proven by bone in its fossil dung.

headline: The 'King' of Dinosaurs May Have Been Fluffy as a Baby
coreClaim: T. rex may have been fluffy as a baby — close relatives had feathers.
buriedGem: A downy chick that became a tyrant.

headline: The Real Velociraptor Was a Feathered Turkey-Sized Hunter
coreClaim: The real Velociraptor was a feathered, turkey-sized predator, far smaller than the movie version.

headline: A Fossil Forearm Proved Velociraptor Had Feathers
coreClaim: Quill knobs on a 2007 Velociraptor arm bone proved it had feathers.
buriedGem: The same anchors as bird feathers.

headline: One Fossil Caught a Velociraptor and Its Prey Mid-Fight
coreClaim: Mongolia's "Fighting Dinosaurs" fossil preserves a Velociraptor and Protoceratops buried mid-battle.
buriedGem: Sickle claw at the throat, jaws on the arm.

headline: Dunkleosteus Belonged to the Fish That Invented Jaws
coreClaim: Dunkleosteus was a placoderm — the group that pioneered jaws and among the earliest live birth.
rationale: A textbook group-membership note; weakest of three Dunkleosteus facts.
buriedGem: Earliest fossil evidence of internal fertilization.
proposedAngle: Trim; the self-sharpening-blades and 1/50-second-strike facts are stronger.

headline: Dunkleosteus Had No Teeth, Just Self-Sharpening Blades
coreClaim: Dunkleosteus had no teeth — it sheared prey with self-sharpening bony jaw blades.

headline: Dunkleosteus Could Inhale Prey in a Fraction of a Second
coreClaim: Dunkleosteus could open its jaws in ~1/50th of a second, inhaling prey before crushing it.

headline: Trilobites Ruled the Seas Far Longer Than the Dinosaurs
coreClaim: Trilobites ruled the seas for ~270 million years — far longer than the dinosaurs.

headline: Trilobites Saw the World Through Eyes Made of Crystal
coreClaim: Trilobites saw through eyes with lenses made of the crystal mineral calcite.

headline: Trilobites Could Curl Into a Ball Like a Pill Bug
coreClaim: Many trilobites could curl into a protective ball like a pill bug.

headline: Archaeopteryx Appeared Right After Darwin Predicted It
coreClaim: Archaeopteryx, the feathered dino-bird, was found in 1861 — two years after Darwin predicted such forms.

headline: Almost Every Archaeopteryx Comes From One German Quarry
coreClaim: Nearly all ~dozen Archaeopteryx fossils come from one Bavarian limestone region.

headline: Archaeopteryx's Lopsided Feathers Give Away How Flight Began
coreClaim: Archaeopteryx's asymmetrical flight feathers match flying birds', hinting how flight evolved.
buriedGem: Flight built up feather by feather.

headline: A California Tar Pit Holds Over 4,000 Dire Wolves
coreClaim: Over 4,000 dire wolves have been pulled from the La Brea Tar Pits.
rationale: Concrete; a predator trap that preserved them.

headline: Dire Wolves Were Not Really Wolves at All
coreClaim: DNA shows dire wolves weren't really wolves — they split off ~5.7 million years ago.
buriedGem: Too distant to interbreed with gray wolves.

headline: Dire Wolves Vanished With the Giants They Hunted
coreClaim: Dire wolves were big-prey specialists that died out with the Ice Age giants they hunted.
rationale: Concrete; specialization as a fatal trap.

headline: Thomas Jefferson Thought Giant Sloth Bones Were a Lion
coreClaim: Thomas Jefferson mistook giant ground-sloth bones for a huge lion and feared it still prowled the West.
buriedGem: Asked Lewis and Clark to watch for living ones.

headline: Avocados May Exist Because Giant Sloths Ate Them
coreClaim: Avocados' huge pits suggest they evolved to be swallowed whole by giant ground sloths.

headline: A Ground Sloth Could Rear Up as Tall as a Two-Story House
coreClaim: The ground sloth Megatherium could rear up two stories tall to feed.

headline: You Could Stand Inside a Megalodon's Open Jaws
coreClaim: Megalodon's jaws were big enough for several people to stand inside.
buriedGem: A great white would be a snack.

headline: People Once Thought Megalodon Teeth Were Dragon Tongues
coreClaim: Megalodon's hand-sized teeth were long thought to be petrified dragon tongues.

headline: A Smaller Shark May Have Helped Doom Megalodon
coreClaim: Megalodon may have been doomed partly by competition from smaller, nimbler great white sharks.
rationale: Concrete; being a giant has costs.

headline: A Mosasaur Skull Helped Prove Extinction Is Real
coreClaim: A Dutch mosasaur skull became famous proof that extinction is real — and was looted by French troops.
buriedGem: Carried to Paris as a war trophy.

headline: Mosasaurs Gave Birth at Sea Like Whales
coreClaim: Mosasaurs gave birth to live young in the open ocean, never returning to land.
rationale: Concrete; committed to the sea like whales.

headline: Mosasaurs Were Lizards That Went Back to the Sea
coreClaim: Mosasaurs were land lizards that returned to the sea — kin to monitor lizards and snakes.
buriedGem: Reclaimed the sea their ancestors left.

headline: Nature Invented Saber Teeth Over and Over
coreClaim: Saber teeth evolved repeatedly — even a marsupial "saber-tooth" in South America.

headline: A Saber-Tooth's Famous Fangs Were Surprisingly Fragile
coreClaim: A saber-tooth's famous fangs were fragile, so Smilodon pinned prey before a careful killing bite.

headline: Healed Wounds Hint Saber-Tooths Cared for Each Other
coreClaim: Many La Brea saber-tooths have healed injuries, hinting they lived socially and fed the wounded.
buriedGem: Like lions, caring for their own.

headline: Scientists Are Editing Elephant DNA to Rebuild the Mammoth
coreClaim: Scientists are editing Asian-elephant DNA to rebuild the woolly mammoth.
rationale: Concrete; de-extinction from fiction to lab.

headline: Frozen Baby Mammoths Still Have Their Last Meal Inside
coreClaim: Permafrost preserved baby mammoths so well their last meal remains in their stomachs.

headline: Woolly Mammoths Were Still Alive When the Pyramids Stood
coreClaim: The last woolly mammoths survived on Wrangel Island until ~4,000 years ago — after the pyramids.

headline: Terror Birds Were Apex Predators With Axe-Like Beaks
coreClaim: Terror birds were 10-foot flightless apex predators with axe-like beaks.

headline: South America Was an Island Ruled by Giant Birds
coreClaim: Isolated South America was ruled by giant terror birds until a land bridge let mammal predators invade.

headline: Terror Birds Likely Killed by Striking Like a Pickaxe
coreClaim: Terror birds likely killed by driving their hooked beak down like a pickaxe.
buriedGem: Rigid skull built for downward blows.

headline: Gold Leaf Is Thin Enough to See Through
coreClaim: Gold leaf is so thin that light passes through it, looking greenish-blue.

headline: A Roman Cup Changes Color, Thanks to Gold
coreClaim: The Roman Lycurgus Cup shifts green to red because it holds gold nanoparticles.

headline: Gold Is Yellow Because of Einstein's Physics
coreClaim: Gold is yellow because its electrons move fast enough for Einstein's relativity to matter.

headline: The Blackest Black Started an Artists' War
coreClaim: Vantablack absorbs 99.96% of light and sparked a running feud between two artists.

headline: A True Black Was Once a Sign of Wealth
coreClaim: A deep, colorfast black was once so hard to make that it signaled wealth and power.
buriedGem: Why judges and clergy wear black.

headline: The Ink of History Slowly Eats Its Own Pages
coreClaim: Iron gall ink wrote much of history — and slowly corrodes the paper it's on.

headline: Blue Is the Rarest Color in Nature
coreClaim: Blue pigment barely exists in nature; animals fake blue with light-scattering structures.
rationale: Concrete, eerie; crush a blue feather and the blue vanishes.

headline: Ancient People May Not Have Noticed Blue
coreClaim: Homer called the sea "wine-dark," and many old languages had no word for blue.
buriedGem: Blue is usually the last color named.

headline: The Best Blue Was Ground-Up Gemstone
coreClaim: The finest blue (ultramarine) was crushed lapis lazuli, sometimes costlier than gold.

headline: A Beautiful Green Quietly Poisoned the Victorians
coreClaim: Victorian "Scheele's green" got its color from arsenic and may have helped kill Napoleon.
buriedGem: Toxic dust from wallpaper and dresses.

headline: Your Eyes Are Tuned to See Green Best
coreClaim: Human vision peaks in green — we distinguish more green shades than any other color.
rationale: Concrete; why night-vision and scrubs are green.

headline: Green Screens Are Green for a Real Reason
coreClaim: Green screens are green because it's farthest from skin tone and sensors capture it best.
buriedGem: Erase the background, not the person.

headline: Indigo Is the Rainbow Color Nobody Needed
coreClaim: Newton added indigo to the rainbow mainly to make the colors total a tidy seven.
rationale: Concrete; a color "talked into" the rainbow.
buriedGem: To match the seven musical notes.

headline: Your Jeans Are Blue Thanks to Air
coreClaim: Jeans turn blue as indigo meets air, and fade because the dye only coats the thread's surface.

headline: Fake Indigo Wiped Out a Global Crop
coreClaim: Synthetic indigo from the lab collapsed the world's natural-indigo plantations within decades.
rationale: Concrete; a test tube beat the fields.

headline: Orange the Color Was Named After Orange the Fruit
coreClaim: English had no word for "orange" until the fruit arrived — before that it was "yellow-red."
buriedGem: The color borrowed the fruit's name.

headline: Carrots Used to Be Purple
coreClaim: Carrots used to be purple, white, and yellow until Dutch growers made orange standard.
buriedGem: Possibly for the House of Orange.

headline: The Most Expensive Orange Is a Spice
coreClaim: Saffron, the orange-gold spice, needs ~150,000 hand-picked flowers per kilogram.
rationale: Concrete scale; sometimes worth more than gold.

headline: Pink Does Not Exist in the Rainbow
coreClaim: Pink has no wavelength — your brain invents it by mixing red and violet.

headline: Pink Was Once a Color for Boys
coreClaim: A century ago pink was often considered the manlier color, blue the delicate one for girls.

headline: A Certain Pink May Calm You Down
coreClaim: "Baker-Miller pink" was tested in jails and locker rooms to reduce aggression.
buriedGem: Teams paint the visitors' locker room pink.

headline: One Robe's Worth of Purple Cost a Fortune in Snails
coreClaim: One ounce of Tyrian purple took ~250,000 murex snails in reeking workshops.

headline: The First Synthetic Dye Was a Teenage Blunder
coreClaim: The first synthetic dye (mauve) was a teenager's failed 1856 malaria-cure experiment.

headline: Cardinals Wear Red Because Purple Ran Out
coreClaim: Cardinals wear red because the fall of Constantinople (1453) cut off true purple dye.

headline: The Red in Your Food Is Made of Bugs
coreClaim: Carmine red food dye is crushed cochineal insects — ~70,000 bugs per pound.

headline: Red Was Humanity's First True Color
coreClaim: Red is the first color most languages name, and red ochre is the oldest pigment humans used.
buriedGem: Sprinkled on the dead 100,000+ years ago.

headline: Bulls Cannot Actually See Red
coreClaim: Bulls can't see red — it's the cape's motion that provokes them.
buriedGem: Red hides the blood, for the crowd.

headline: Fashionable White Makeup Slowly Poisoned People
coreClaim: Fashionable white "ceruse" face paint was toxic lead that corroded the skin it whitened.
buriedGem: Users caked on more to hide the damage.

headline: The Whitest White Is Hiding in Your Food
coreClaim: Titanium dioxide, the whitest pigment, brightens paint — and toothpaste and candy.
buriedGem: Same stuff in wall paint and doughnuts.

headline: In Much of the World, White Means Death
coreClaim: In much of Asia, white — not black — is the color of mourning.
buriedGem: The wedding-white and funeral-white are the same color.

headline: A Flat Pattern Can Hide a Hidden 3D Image
coreClaim: Magic Eye images fool your two eyes into seeing 3D depth in a flat pattern.
buriedGem: Your brain builds a shape from nothing.

headline: Some People Cannot See in 3D at All
coreClaim: Some people are "stereoblind" and see no 3D depth — a few gain it later in life.

headline: The Moon Looks Huge on the Horizon for No Real Reason
coreClaim: The horizon Moon looks huge but is identical to the high Moon — science still debates why.
buriedGem: A coin proves they're the same size.

headline: A Mirage Can Conjure Floating Cities and Ghost Ships
coreClaim: The Fata Morgana mirage stacks distant objects into floating cities — likely behind ghost-ship tales.

headline: That Desert Oasis Is Really a Piece of the Sky
coreClaim: The "water" on a hot road is really a bent-up image of the sky.

headline: A Mirage Can Show You a Sun That Has Already Set
coreClaim: Bending Arctic air can show the Sun while it's still below the horizon.
buriedGem: Dutch explorers saw sunrise ~2 weeks early.

headline: A Face on Mars Turned Out to Be a Hill
coreClaim: The famous "Face on Mars" was an ordinary hill sculpted by shadow and low resolution.

headline: A Grilled Cheese Sold for $28,000 Because of a Face
coreClaim: A grilled cheese said to show the Virgin Mary sold for ~$28,000.

headline: Cars and Outlets Look Like Faces on Purpose
coreClaim: Your trigger-happy face detector is why cars and outlets seem to have faces.
buriedGem: Missing a predator cost more than a false face.

headline: Stare at the Wrong Colors and a Hidden Flag Appears
coreClaim: Stare at a color-reversed flag, then a blank wall, and a correctly colored flag appears.
buriedGem: Your eyes flip each color to its opposite.

headline: An Afterimage Is Your Own Worn-Out Eye Cells
coreClaim: An afterimage isn't on the wall — it's fatigued cone cells in your eye.

headline: Hold Your Eyes Still and Things Start to Vanish
coreClaim: Hold your gaze perfectly still and steady edges of your vision fade out (Troxler fading).
buriedGem: Your brain tunes out the unchanging.

headline: Your Blind Spot Could Swallow Nine Full Moons
coreClaim: Each eye's blind spot is big enough to hide nine full Moons; the other eye covers the gap.
rationale: Concrete scale surprise; overlaps the human-body blind-spot fact (different angle).
buriedGem: The offset eyes cover for each other.
proposedAngle: Overlaps human-body/eyes-two-eyes-3d-vision; keep the more concrete of the two.

headline: You Go Blind Every Time Your Eyes Move
coreClaim: Vision briefly switches off during each eye movement — you can't catch your own eyes moving in a mirror.
buriedGem: Blind hundreds of times an hour.

headline: Astronomers See Faint Stars by Looking Away From Them
coreClaim: The center of your gaze is nearly blind in dim light, so astronomers look beside faint stars.

headline: Two Squares Can Be Identical Yet Look Totally Different
coreClaim: In the checker-shadow illusion, two squares are the identical grey but look completely different.
buriedGem: Believable only when connected by a grey bar.

headline: A Photo With No Red Can Still Look Full of Red
coreClaim: A cyan-tinted photo with zero red pixels still looks full of red strawberries.

headline: The Same Gray Looks Different Depending on Its Neighbors
coreClaim: An identical grey looks lighter or darker depending on its surroundings (simultaneous contrast).
rationale: Concrete; color has no fixed appearance.
buriedGem: Change its neighbors, change the hue.

headline: The Impossible Trident Cheats at the Joints
coreClaim: The impossible trident works because each end looks valid alone but the two can't connect.
buriedGem: Cover one end and it looks buildable.

headline: An Artist and a Physicist Traded Impossible Ideas
coreClaim: Escher's impossible staircases grew from the Penrose triangle, in a loop of art and math.

headline: You Can Actually Build a Penrose Triangle
coreClaim: A Perth sculpture looks like a solid Penrose triangle — but only from one exact spot.
buriedGem: Walk a few steps and it falls apart.

headline: Stare at a Waterfall and the Rocks Drift Upward
coreClaim: After watching downward motion, still objects seem to drift upward (the waterfall illusion).

headline: This Still Image Only Moves When You Don't Look at It
coreClaim: "Rotating Snakes" seems to spin in your peripheral vision but freezes when you stare at it.
buriedGem: Phantom motion from tiny eye movements.

headline: Spinning Wheels Can Appear to Turn Backward on Film
coreClaim: Filmed wheels can appear to spin backward because film captures motion as still frames.
rationale: Concrete explanation of a familiar effect.
buriedGem: Each frame catches the wheel slightly "behind."

headline: Hobbits Were Shrunk Mostly Without Special Effects
coreClaim: Lord of the Rings shrank hobbits mainly with camera distance and sliding sets, not CGI.
buriedGem: A bench slid during one shot.

headline: A Room Can Make People Grow and Shrink
coreClaim: The lopsided Ames Room makes a person appear to grow or shrink as they cross it.
buriedGem: Built to look normal through a peephole.

headline: Disney Castles Are Shorter Than They Look
coreClaim: Disney shrinks the upper floors of its castles so they look far taller than they are.
buriedGem: Smaller windows and bricks up top.

headline: The Dress That Broke the Internet Was Really Blue and Black
coreClaim: The viral dress was really blue and black, yet many people physically couldn't see it that way.

headline: Whether You Saw Gold May Depend on When You Wake Up
coreClaim: Seeing the dress as white-gold or blue-black tracked with being an early bird or a night owl.

headline: The Dress Had a Sound-Based Twin
coreClaim: "Yanny or Laurel" split people's hearing just as The Dress split their vision.
rationale: Concrete parallel; perception is always guessing.

headline: Shackleton Lost His Ship but Not a Single Man
coreClaim: After Endurance sank, Shackleton sailed 800 miles in a lifeboat and saved all 28 men.

headline: Shackleton Quit 97 Miles From Glory to Stay Alive
coreClaim: Shackleton turned back just 97 miles from the South Pole to keep his men alive.
buriedGem: "A live donkey beats a dead lion."

headline: Endurance Was Found Almost Perfectly Preserved
coreClaim: Endurance was found in 2022 almost perfectly preserved — Antarctic water lacks wood-eating shipworms.
buriedGem: The ice that killed it preserved it.

headline: Ibn Battuta Out-Traveled Marco Polo Three Times Over
coreClaim: Ibn Battuta traveled ~75,000 miles — about three times as far as Marco Polo.

headline: A Judge and Serial Husband Wandered for Decades
coreClaim: Ibn Battuta married repeatedly and served as a judge in distant courts like the Maldives and Delhi.
buriedGem: A restless life, not one expedition.

headline: A Trip to Mecca Became a 24-Year Journey
coreClaim: Ibn Battuta left at 21 for a pilgrimage to Mecca and didn't return home for ~24 years.

headline: Cousteau Co-Invented the Gear That Freed Divers
coreClaim: Cousteau co-invented the 1943 Aqua-Lung that freed divers and launched modern scuba.

headline: Cousteau Had People Live on the Sea Floor
coreClaim: Cousteau had "oceanauts" live and work on the sea floor for weeks in the 1960s.
buriedGem: Inhabiting the ocean, not visiting it.

headline: Cousteau's Ocean Film Won Cannes and an Oscar
coreClaim: Cousteau's The Silent World won both the Palme d'Or and an Academy Award.

headline: Lewis and Clark Crossed 8,000 Miles and Lost Only One Man
coreClaim: Lewis and Clark crossed ~8,000 miles and lost only one man, likely to a burst appendix.
