buriedGem: No doctor of the era could have saved him.

headline: Lewis and Clark Described 300 Species New to Western Science
coreClaim: Lewis and Clark documented ~300 species new to Western science.

headline: An Enslaved Man Made the Journey and Got a Vote
coreClaim: York, enslaved by Clark, made the whole journey and even got a vote — then was denied freedom for years.
buriedGem: Voted decades before he legally could.

headline: Magellan's Enslaved Interpreter May Have Circled Earth First
coreClaim: Magellan's enslaved interpreter Enrique may have been the first person to fully circle the globe.

headline: Magellan Named the Calm Ocean That Nearly Killed Him
coreClaim: Magellan named the calm Pacific, then his starving crew crossed it eating rats and leather.

headline: The Survivors Came Home a Full Day Behind
coreClaim: Magellan's survivors returned a full day behind Europe — the first brush with the date line.

headline: Marco Polo Dictated His Book From a Prison Cell
coreClaim: Marco Polo dictated his famous book from a Genoese prison cell to a fellow prisoner.

headline: Some Doubt Marco Polo Ever Reached China
coreClaim: Some scholars doubt Marco Polo reached China — he never mentions tea, chopsticks, or the Great Wall.

headline: Marco Polo's Last Words Doubled Down on His Tales
coreClaim: Told to admit he exaggerated, the dying Marco Polo said "I did not tell half of what I saw."

headline: Nellie Bly Raced Around the World Against a Rival
coreClaim: Nellie Bly circled the world in 72 days while a rival reporter secretly raced her the opposite way.

headline: Nellie Bly Faked Madness to Expose an Asylum
coreClaim: Nellie Bly faked madness to get committed and expose an asylum from the inside.

headline: Nellie Bly Became a Factory Owner and War Reporter
coreClaim: After her stunts, Bly ran a factory, held patents, and reported from the WWI front.

headline: Amundsen Beat His Rival to the Pole, Who Then Died
coreClaim: Amundsen beat Scott to the South Pole by five weeks; Scott's team then died on the way back.

headline: Amundsen Cracked a Route Sought for 400 Years
coreClaim: Amundsen's six-man crew was first to sail the Northwest Passage, sought for 400 years.

headline: Amundsen Vanished Trying to Rescue a Rival
coreClaim: First to both poles, Amundsen vanished in 1928 flying to rescue a rival he disliked.

headline: China's Greatest Admiral Was an Enslaved Eunuch
coreClaim: China's greatest admiral, Zheng He, was captured and castrated as a boy, then rose to command.

headline: Zheng He's Fleet Made Columbus Look Tiny
coreClaim: Zheng He's treasure fleet had hundreds of ships and 28,000 men, decades before Columbus.

headline: A Giraffe Gift Preceded China Abandoning the Seas
coreClaim: Zheng He brought home a giraffe hailed as a mythical qilin — then China scrapped its fleets and turned inward.
buriedGem: The leading navy simply sailed home.

headline: Sacagawea Crossed a Continent With a Newborn
coreClaim: At ~16, Sacagawea crossed the continent and back with her newborn on her back.
buriedGem: Gave birth weeks before setting out.

headline: Sacagawea's Horse Trade Reunited Her With Her Lost Brother
coreClaim: The Shoshone chief Sacagawea negotiated horses from was her own long-lost brother.
buriedGem: Separated when she was kidnapped as a child.

headline: Sacagawea Was a Walking Signal of Peace
coreClaim: Sacagawea's presence marked the Corps as peaceful; she now has more statues than any other American woman.
buriedGem: A woman and baby signaled no war party.

headline: This Grass Is Strong Enough to Build Skyscrapers
coreClaim: Bamboo is a grass, yet strong enough to serve as skyscraper scaffolding.

headline: Some Bamboo Grows More Than an Inch an Hour
coreClaim: Some bamboo grows more than an inch an hour — a shoot inflates segments it already has.

headline: Bamboo Flowers Worldwide at Once, Then Famine Follows
coreClaim: A bamboo species flowers worldwide in sync, then dies — and in India the seed glut triggers rat famines.
buriedGem: Even clones bloom the same year.

headline: People Have Turned Baobabs Into Pubs and Jails
coreClaim: Baobab trunks grow so huge and hollow that people have used them as a pub, a jail, and a bus stop.
buriedGem: A tree that doubles as a building.

headline: Some of Africa's Oldest Baobabs Suddenly Died
coreClaim: Baobabs can top 2,000 years old, yet several of Africa's oldest abruptly died in the 2010s.

headline: Legend Says the Baobab Was Planted Upside Down
coreClaim: The baobab's root-like branches inspired a legend that a god replanted it upside down.

headline: Almost Every Cactus on Earth Is From the Americas
coreClaim: With one wild exception, all cacti are native only to the Americas.
buriedGem: Old World cacti were brought by people.

headline: A Cactus Spine Is Really a Modified Leaf
coreClaim: A cactus spine is really a modified leaf; the stem does the photosynthesis.
buriedGem: Spines shield, shade, and drip dew.

headline: A Saguaro Grows So Slowly It Can Get Revenge
coreClaim: A saguaro can take 75 years to grow one arm — and one crushed a man who shot it.

headline: A Pinhead of Poison Ivy Oil Could Rash Hundreds
coreClaim: A pinhead of poison-ivy oil could rash hundreds, and it stays active for years.

headline: Whatever You Do, Never Burn Poison Ivy
coreClaim: Never burn poison ivy — urushiol rides the smoke and can injure the lungs.

headline: Climate Change Is Making Poison Ivy Worse
coreClaim: Higher CO2 makes poison ivy grow bigger and produce a more potent, itchier oil.

headline: Wild Venus Flytraps Grow in Only One Small Area
coreClaim: Wild Venus flytraps grow only within ~75 miles of Wilmington, NC — poaching is a felony.

headline: A Venus Flytrap Doesn't Eat Bugs for Food
coreClaim: A Venus flytrap lives on sunlight; it catches bugs mainly for nitrogen its poor soil lacks.

headline: A Venus Flytrap Can Actually Count
coreClaim: A Venus flytrap counts touches — snapping only after two, digesting only after ~five.

headline: Washington's First Cherry Trees Had to Be Burned
coreClaim: Japan's first 2,000 cherry trees to Washington arrived diseased and were burned; Japan sent 3,000 more.

headline: Cherry Records Span 1,200 Years and Reveal Warming
coreClaim: Kyoto's 1,200-year record of cherry blooms now shows the earliest peaks ever — evidence of warming.
buriedGem: One of the longest seasonal datasets.

headline: Cherry Blossoms Are Loved Because They Barely Last
coreClaim: Cherry blossoms are loved partly because they last only about a week.
buriedGem: The heart of hanami and mono no aware.

headline: The Biggest Living Thing Grew From an Oat-Sized Seed
coreClaim: The biggest living thing (General Sherman) outweighs ten blue whales but grew from an oat-flake seed.

headline: Giant Sequoias Actually Need Fire to Survive
coreClaim: Giant sequoias actually need fire — its heat opens their cones and clears the ground.

headline: People Once Drove Cars Through a Living Sequoia
coreClaim: People drove cars through a tunnel cut in a living Yosemite sequoia until it fell in 1969.

headline: The 'World's Biggest Flower' Is Not Really a Flower
coreClaim: The "world's biggest flower" is really a spike of thousands of tiny flowers.
rationale: Concrete correction; the real record is Rafflesia.

headline: The Corpse Flower Heats Itself to Spread Its Stink
coreClaim: A blooming corpse flower heats its spike to near body temperature to waft its stench half a mile.

headline: A Corpse Flower Bloom Draws Crowds and Livestreams
coreClaim: Corpse flowers bloom only every 7–10 years for barely a day, so crowds line up and gardens livestream it.

headline: Mangroves Give Birth to Live Seedlings
coreClaim: Many mangroves "give birth" to live spear-shaped seedlings that drop into the mud or float away.

headline: Some Mangroves Breathe Through Snorkel Roots
coreClaim: Many mangroves breathe through vertical "snorkel" roots poking out of airless mud.

headline: A Wall of Mangroves Can Blunt a Tsunami
coreClaim: Mangroves' dense roots blunt waves — villages behind them fared far better in the 2004 tsunami.

headline: A Bonsai Is Not a Special Dwarf Tree
coreClaim: A bonsai isn't a dwarf species — it's an ordinary tree kept tiny by pruning roots and branches.

headline: Some Bonsai Are Centuries Old and Worth Millions
coreClaim: Some bonsai are centuries old and have sold for over a million dollars.
buriedGem: Shaped by a lineage of caretakers.

headline: A Bonsai in Washington Survived Hiroshima
coreClaim: A 400-year-old bonsai gifted to the US survived Hiroshima two miles away — unknown until 2001.
buriedGem: Its keepers said nothing for 25 years.

headline: The Shrinking Dead Sea Is Swallowing the Land
coreClaim: As the Dead Sea recedes ~3 feet a year, thousands of sinkholes are collapsing and swallowing land.

headline: The Dead Sea Was One of History's First Spas
coreClaim: The Dead Sea was arguably history's first spa — Herod built a retreat, and its mud is still sold worldwide.
buriedGem: Cleopatra reputedly sought it for cosmetics.

headline: The Dead Sea Coughs Up Its Own Asphalt
coreClaim: The Dead Sea periodically floats up blocks of natural asphalt, once used to embalm Egyptian mummies.
buriedGem: The Romans called it the Asphalt Lake.

headline: This Salt Flat Becomes the World's Largest Mirror
coreClaim: When flooded, Salar de Uyuni becomes a mirror so flat satellites use it for calibration.
buriedGem: The horizon vanishes; you walk on clouds.

headline: This Salt Flat Stays Level to Within a Meter for Miles
coreClaim: Across ~4,000 square miles, Salar de Uyuni varies in elevation by less than a meter.
rationale: Concrete; one of Earth's flattest places.

headline: The Salt Flat's Photos Hide the World's Battery Supply
coreClaim: Beneath the salt flat lies one of the world's largest lithium reserves — the metal in your batteries.
buriedGem: Mirror photos over the battery supply.

headline: Nevada's Most Surreal Geyser Was an Accident
coreClaim: Nevada's surreal Fly Geyser was created by accident from a leaking 1960s geothermal well.
rationale: Concrete; a plumbing error gone wild.

headline: Fly Geyser's Wild Colors Are Actually Alive
coreClaim: Fly Geyser's wild red and green colors are living heat-loving algae, not minerals.

headline: Fly Geyser Keeps Growing, and Burning Man Owns It
coreClaim: Fly Geyser is still growing, and its land was bought by the Burning Man organization.

headline: A Fire Meant to Last Weeks Has Burned 50 Years
coreClaim: Soviet geologists lit a Turkmenistan gas crater in 1971 expecting weeks — it still burns 50+ years on.
buriedGem: A glowing gateway to the underworld.

headline: Turkmenistan Keeps Trying to Put the Fire Out
coreClaim: Turkmenistan's leaders keep ordering the burning crater put out, but no one has managed it.
buriedGem: Easier to order than to accomplish.

headline: An Explorer Rappelled Into the Burning Crater
coreClaim: George Kourounis rappelled into the fiery crater in 2013 and found bacteria living on its floor.

headline: 40,000 Stone Columns Cracked Into Perfect Hexagons
coreClaim: The Giant's Causeway's 40,000 columns crack into hexagons because that relieves cooling stress most efficiently.

headline: The Giant's Bridge to Scotland Is Partly Real
coreClaim: Scotland's Fingal's Cave is from the same 60-million-year-old lava flow as the Giant's Causeway.
rationale: Concrete; the legend is partly real.
buriedGem: Two ends of one volcanic event.

headline: A Giant Disguised as a Baby Won the Day
coreClaim: In the legend, Finn McCool's wife disguises him as a giant baby to scare off a bigger Scottish giant.

headline: The Paris Catacombs Hold Around Six Million People
coreClaim: The Paris Catacombs hold the bones of ~6 million people — more than the living city above.

headline: A Secret Cinema Was Found in the Catacomb Maze
coreClaim: Beneath Paris run ~200 miles of tunnels where police once found a secret cinema and bar.

headline: The Catacomb Bones Were Arranged Into Walls
coreClaim: The catacomb bones were arranged into decorative walls of skulls and thighbones.
buriedGem: A doorkeeper lost in 1793, found 11 years later.

headline: For a Century, No One Could Explain the Bleeding Glacier
coreClaim: Blood Falls' red baffled explorers for a century — it's really iron rusting on contact with air.
buriedGem: The glacier isn't bleeding, it's rusting.

headline: Blood Falls Flows From a 2-Million-Year-Old Lake
coreClaim: Blood Falls flows from a briny lake sealed beneath the ice for ~2 million years.

headline: Life Thrives Where There Is No Sun or Oxygen
coreClaim: Microbes in Blood Falls live with no sun or oxygen, eating iron and sulfur — a model for alien life.
buriedGem: A glimpse of life on other worlds.

headline: This 'Cotton Castle' Is Actually Solid Rock
coreClaim: Pamukkale's "Cotton Castle" looks like cascading snow but is solid travertine rock.

headline: Next to Pamukkale Sits an Ancient 'Gate to Hell'
coreClaim: Beside Pamukkale, an ancient "Gate to Hell" killed animals with invisible CO2 while priests survived.
buriedGem: Lethal gas pooled near the ground.

headline: You Can Swim Among Sunken Roman Columns
coreClaim: At Pamukkale you can swim among Roman marble columns toppled into the spring by an ancient earthquake.

headline: Socotra's Dragon's Blood Trees Look Alien
coreClaim: Socotra's dragon's blood trees look like umbrellas and bleed red sap used for millennia.

headline: Socotra Is a Galapagos Marooned in the Ocean
coreClaim: Isolated for millions of years, Socotra evolved plants and animals found nowhere else — a marooned Galapagos.

headline: Monsoon Winds Seal Socotra Off for Months
coreClaim: Monsoon winds seal Socotra off from the world for months each year.

headline: Lake Hillier Stays Pink Even in a Glass
coreClaim: Lake Hillier's pink is in the water itself — scoop it into a glass and it stays pink.
buriedGem: Not a reflection of the sky.

headline: Flamingo Pigment Is Why the Lake Is Pink
coreClaim: Lake Hillier's pink comes from salt-loving microbes making carotenoids — the pigment that colors flamingos.
buriedGem: Dyed from within by its brine.

headline: A Pink Lake Sits Right Beside a Deep-Blue Sea
coreClaim: Lake Hillier's bubblegum-pink water sits a thin strip of trees from a deep-blue sea.
rationale: Concrete visual contrast; softest of the three.

headline: Farming May Have Been a Health Disaster at First
coreClaim: Early farmers were shorter and sicker than the foragers they replaced.

headline: Humans Invented Farming From Scratch Many Times
coreClaim: Farming was invented independently in as many as eleven separate regions.

headline: We Bred Corn So Much It Cannot Survive Alone
coreClaim: We bred corn so much it can no longer drop its own seeds to reproduce.
rationale: Concrete; a crop dependent on us.
buriedGem: Wild teosinte looks nothing like a cob.

headline: Switching Calendars Erased Days From History
coreClaim: Adopting the Gregorian calendar deleted days — Oct 1582 jumped 4th to 15th; Britain skipped 11 in 1752.

headline: The Last Four Months Are Named for the Wrong Numbers
coreClaim: September–December mean "months 7–10" — a fossil from when the Roman year began in March.

headline: The French Revolution Tried to Decimalize Time
coreClaim: Revolutionary France built a 10-day week and a 10-hour decimal day — abandoned within a decade.
buriedGem: Only one rest day in ten.

headline: One of the First Cities Had No Streets at All
coreClaim: One of the first cities (Catalhoyuk) had no streets — people walked the rooftops and entered through the ceiling.

headline: Rome Was So Big No City Matched It for 1,800 Years
coreClaim: Ancient Rome hit ~1 million people, a size no city matched again until London ~1800.

headline: Humans Only Recently Became a City Species
coreClaim: Humanity only became majority-urban around 2007 — for the first time ever.

headline: The Word 'Money' Comes From a Roman Temple
coreClaim: "Money" and "mint" both come from Rome's temple of Juno Moneta, where coins were struck.
buriedGem: A goddess stamped her name on cash.

headline: The First Coins Came From a King So Rich He's a Byword
coreClaim: The first coins came from Lydia, home of King Croesus — "rich as Croesus."
rationale: Concrete origin plus a living phrase.

headline: Brutus Put the Ides of March on a Coin
coreClaim: Brutus minted an "EID MAR" coin to advertise assassinating Caesar.
buriedGem: A liberty cap over the Ides of March.

headline: Ancient Persia Moved Water Underground for Miles
coreClaim: Ancient Persian qanats carried water underground for dozens of miles by gravity — some still used.

headline: Egypt Taxed People by Measuring the Nile Flood
coreClaim: Egyptian officials set each year's taxes by measuring the Nile flood with "nilometers."
buriedGem: The river set the tax rate.

headline: Irrigation Drained One of the World's Biggest Lakes
coreClaim: Soviet cotton irrigation drained the Aral Sea, once the world's 4th-largest lake, into a desert.

headline: Hammurabi's Famous Code Was Not the First
coreClaim: Hammurabi's "eye for an eye" code wasn't the first — Ur-Nammu's predates it by ~300 years and favored fines.

headline: One Lawmaker Was So Harsh He Named a Word
coreClaim: Athenian lawgiver Draco punished even minor crimes with death, giving us "draconian."
rationale: Concrete; a word from a person.

headline: Medieval Europe Put Animals on Trial
coreClaim: Medieval European courts put animals on trial — with defense lawyers and sentences.

headline: All Roads Really Did Lead to Rome
coreClaim: Rome's 50,000 miles of roads were all measured from one Golden Milestone in the Forum.
rationale: Concrete; "all roads lead to Rome" made literal.
buriedGem: The true center of the Roman world.

headline: Modern Highways Still Follow Roman Roads
coreClaim: Many modern European highways still follow the exact routes of 2,000-year-old Roman roads.

headline: Rome Ran a Pony Express Two Millennia Ago
coreClaim: Rome ran a "pony express" (cursus publicus) two millennia ago, swapping horses to move ~50 miles a day.

headline: Rome's Great Sewer Still Works After 2,500 Years
coreClaim: Rome's Cloaca Maxima still partly drains the city after ~2,500 years — and had its own goddess.

headline: A 'Great Stink' Forced London to Build Sewers
coreClaim: London's 1858 "Great Stink" forced Parliament to build sewers that also helped end cholera.

headline: Doctors Voted Sanitation the Greatest Advance
coreClaim: Readers of a medical journal voted sanitation the greatest medical advance since 1840 — over antibiotics and vaccines.
buriedGem: Plumbing saved more lives than medicine.

headline: An 'Hour' Used to Change Length With the Seasons
coreClaim: An "hour" used to change length with the seasons — daylight split into 12, whatever the day's length.
buriedGem: Summer hours longer than winter ones.

headline: Railways Are the Reason We Have Time Zones
coreClaim: Time zones exist because railways forced standardized "railway time."
buriedGem: Local noon varied town to town.

headline: A Clock Problem Kept Sailors Lost at Sea
coreClaim: Sailors couldn't find their longitude until Harrison built a clock accurate at sea.

headline: The World Map You Know Gets Sizes Very Wrong
coreClaim: The Mercator map makes Greenland look as big as Africa — which is really ~14 times larger.

headline: North Was Not Always at the Top of Maps
coreClaim: Many medieval maps put east, not north, on top — the origin of "orient."

headline: The Oldest World Map Is a Babylonian Clay Tablet
coreClaim: The oldest world map is a 2,600-year-old Babylonian tablet ringed by a "bitter river."
buriedGem: Babylon at the center of creation.

headline: Near a Black Hole, You'd Watch the Universe Age
coreClaim: Hover near a black hole and you'd watch the outside universe race through its whole future.

headline: Spaghettification Is a Real Scientific Term
coreClaim: "Spaghettification" is a real term — tidal gravity stretches you into a strand of pasta.
rationale: Concrete, memorable; a real Einstein prediction.
buriedGem: Harder pull on the near side.

headline: Black Holes Aren't Cosmic Vacuum Cleaners
coreClaim: Black holes aren't cosmic vacuum cleaners — from a distance one pulls like any equal-mass star.

headline: Our Galaxy Has a Black Hole Weighing Four Million Suns
coreClaim: The Milky Way hides a black hole weighing four million Suns, found by clocking stars whipping around it.

headline: There May Be One Septillion Stars in the Universe
coreClaim: The visible universe may hold ~1 septillion stars (a 1 with 24 zeros).
rationale: A large number without a relatable anchor (no grains-of-sand comparison in the body).
proposedAngle: Add a concrete comparison (e.g., grains of sand on Earth) or it stays an inert big number.

headline: Some Stars Can Live for Trillions of Years
coreClaim: Red dwarf stars can live for trillions of years — the universe is too young for any to have died yet.

headline: The Stars in a Constellation Aren't Actually Connected
coreClaim: The stars in a constellation aren't connected — they can be trillions of miles apart.
buriedGem: They just line up from Earth.

headline: NASA Astronauts Still Train to Navigate by the Stars
coreClaim: NASA astronauts still train to navigate by the stars as a backup.
buriedGem: Ancient sky knowledge in the space age.

headline: One Nebula Looks Like a Giant Eyeball
coreClaim: The Helix Nebula looks like a giant eye ("The Eye of God").
rationale: A "looks like X" fact with thin payoff; the sole fact in its topic.
proposedAngle: Re-source a stronger nebula fact (e.g., the Pillars of Creation scale, or a nebula less dense than a vacuum).

headline: Some Supernovas Outshine Entire Galaxies
coreClaim: A supernova can briefly outshine every other star in its galaxy combined.

headline: The Gold in Your Jewelry Was Forged in Dying Stars
coreClaim: The gold in your jewelry was forged in dying stars — older than the Sun.

headline: Ancient People Saw Supernovas in Daylight
coreClaim: Some supernovas were bright enough for ancient people to see in daylight.

headline: Nobody Has Ever Photographed the Milky Way From Outside
coreClaim: No one has ever photographed the Milky Way from outside — every "photo" is a reconstruction.
buriedGem: Like drawing a city from inside it.

headline: The Milky Way Will Collide With Andromeda
coreClaim: The Milky Way will merge with Andromeda — yet almost no stars will actually collide.
buriedGem: Stars are too far apart to hit.

headline: Some Galaxies Are Seen Near the Dawn of Time
coreClaim: Telescopes catch light that has traveled 13+ billion years, showing the universe as a baby.
buriedGem: Galaxies from just after the Big Bang.

headline: Astronauts Repaired a Telescope in Space
coreClaim: Hubble was built to be fixed in orbit — astronauts flew up five times to repair and upgrade it.
buriedGem: Some of spaceflight's most daring missions.

headline: Hubble Found the Universe Is Speeding Up, Not Slowing Down
coreClaim: Hubble helped reveal the universe is speeding up, implying "dark energy."
buriedGem: Most of the universe is a mystery force.

headline: Webb Had to Fold Like Origami
coreClaim: The James Webb telescope was too big for its rocket, so it unfolded like origami in space.

headline: Webb's Sunshield Is Like SPF One Million
coreClaim: Webb's five-layer sunshield blocks the Sun like "SPF one million."
buriedGem: Sides differ by hundreds of degrees.

headline: Webb Can See More Than 13 Billion Years Into the Past
coreClaim: Webb can see light from more than 13 billion years ago, near the Big Bang.
rationale: Nearly identical revelation to light-years-early-universe in the same category.
proposedAngle: Merge with light-years-early-universe or trim; keep the origami and sunshield Webb facts.

headline: Some Galaxy Jets Move Almost at Light Speed
coreClaim: Some galaxies fire particle jets outward at nearly the speed of light.

headline: One Feeding Black Hole Can Outshine Its Whole Galaxy
coreClaim: A single feeding black hole (quasar) can outshine its entire galaxy of hundreds of billions of stars.
rationale: Concrete scale; how we see them so far.

headline: A Sugar Cube of Neutron Star Stuff Weighs Like a Mountain
coreClaim: A sugar cube of neutron-star material would weigh about as much as a mountain.

headline: One Magnetar Burst Beat 100,000 Years of Sunlight
coreClaim: One 0.1-second magnetar burst released more energy than the Sun made in 100,000 years.

headline: A White Dwarf Packs a Sun Into Earth's Size
coreClaim: A white dwarf packs the Sun's mass into something only a little bigger than Earth.

headline: The Universe May Be Too Young for Black Dwarfs
coreClaim: Dead stars should cool into "black dwarfs," but the universe may be too young for any to exist yet.

headline: Sirius Wobbled Because of a Hidden Dead Star
coreClaim: The first white dwarf was found because Sirius, the brightest star, was wobbling.

headline: The Sun Will Swallow the Inner Planets
coreClaim: When the Sun becomes a red giant it will swallow Mercury and Venus — and maybe Earth.
buriedGem: The star that gives us life consumes its worlds.

headline: A Red Giant Is Cooler Because It Gets Bigger
coreClaim: A red giant is cooler on its surface precisely because it swelled so large.
buriedGem: Heat spread over a bigger surface.

headline: The Oldest Light in the Universe Is Still Around
coreClaim: The oldest light in the universe, from ~375,000 years after it began, is still traveling today.

headline: Tiny Temperature Differences Grew Into Galaxies
coreClaim: Tiny temperature differences in the ancient sky grew into all stars and galaxies.

headline: Ordinary Atoms Are Only About 5% of the Universe
coreClaim: Ordinary atoms — stars, planets, people — are only about 5% of the universe.

headline: Some Coral Reefs Can Be Seen From Space
coreClaim: Some coral reefs, built by tiny polyps, are big enough to see from space.

headline: Corals Can Turn White Without Dying
coreClaim: Stressed corals expel their algae and turn ghostly white — but aren't necessarily dead.
buriedGem: They can recover if conditions improve.

headline: Sharks Can Detect Electricity
coreClaim: Sharks sense the electric fields of hidden prey via the ampullae of Lorenzini.

headline: Sharks Are Older Than Dinosaurs
coreClaim: Sharks appeared ~455 million years ago — long before dinosaurs — and outlasted five mass extinctions.
buriedGem: One of the oldest surviving groups.

headline: Most Deep-Sea Animals Can Make Their Own Light
coreClaim: About 80% of animals in the deep sea can make their own light.
rationale: Concrete; glowing is the norm down there.

headline: Some Shrimp Can Spit Out Light
coreClaim: Some deep-sea shrimp spit out glowing material like a "smoke bomb" to blind predators.
buriedGem: A burst of light to flee.

headline: The Deepest Ocean Is Deeper Than Mount Everest Is Tall
coreClaim: The deepest ocean is deeper than Everest is tall — the peak still wouldn't reach the surface.

headline: The Largest Daily Migration on Earth Happens Underwater
coreClaim: The largest daily migration on Earth is underwater — countless animals rise hundreds of meters each night.

headline: There Are Lakes at the Bottom of the Ocean
coreClaim: There are super-salty "brine lakes" on the ocean floor, complete with shorelines and ripples.
buriedGem: Too dense to mix with seawater.

headline: Some Jellyfish Trail Tentacles Longer Than a Bus
coreClaim: A sea nettle jellyfish trails 12–15-foot tentacles.
rationale: The headline claims "Longer Than a Bus," but 12–15 feet is shorter than a bus — headline/body mismatch.
buriedGem: Stinging cells with no brain or bones.
proposedAngle: Fix the headline to match the body, or re-hook the lion's mane jellyfish (100+ ft) which truly beats a bus.

headline: Some Young Crabs Hitchhike on Jellyfish
coreClaim: Baby crabs hitchhike on jellyfish mouth-arms — and may snack on their host.
buriedGem: A drifting jellyfish as a home.

headline: Some Jellyfish Travel Thousands of Feet Every Day
coreClaim: Some jellyfish rise and sink 3,600+ feet a day — with no real muscles.
buriedGem: Trips that would challenge bigger animals.

headline: A Blue Whale's Tongue Weighs as Much as an Elephant
coreClaim: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, and its heart is the size of a small car.

headline: Whales' Closest Living Relative Is the Hippo
coreClaim: Whales evolved from land animals — their closest living relative is the hippo.

headline: Male Seahorses Give Birth
coreClaim: Male seahorses give birth — the female deposits eggs in his pouch.

headline: Seahorses Don't Have Stomachs
coreClaim: Seahorses have no stomach, so food rushes through and they must eat almost constantly.

headline: A Sea Turtle's Sex Depends on the Sand
coreClaim: A sea turtle's sex is set by the sand temperature — warm makes females, cool makes males.
rationale: Concrete; "hot chicks, cool dudes"; warming skews it.

headline: Leatherbacks Travel More Than 10,000 Miles a Year
coreClaim: Leatherback turtles swim more than 10,000 miles a year, crossing whole oceans.

headline: Sea Stars Can Regrow Entire Bodies
coreClaim: A single severed sea-star arm (with part of the disc) can grow into a whole new animal.

headline: Sea Stars Can Push Their Stomachs Outside Their Bodies
coreClaim: Sea stars push their stomach out of their body and into a clam's shell to digest it.
buriedGem: Then pulls the stomach back in.

headline: Some Tidal Currents Can Move Faster Than a Sprinter
coreClaim: Some tidal currents top eight knots — faster than a person can sprint.

headline: Norway Has Milder Winters Than Expected Because of Ocean Currents
coreClaim: The Gulf Stream gives Bergen, Norway milder winters than New York City, far to the south.
buriedGem: An ocean shapes weather thousands of miles off.

headline: Female Anglerfish Carry a Built-In Fishing Rod
coreClaim: A female anglerfish hunts with a glowing lure on a "fishing rod" attached to her head.
buriedGem: Bait, then a mouth of glassy teeth.

headline: Anglerfish Can Swallow Prey Twice Their Size
coreClaim: An anglerfish's flexible body lets it swallow prey up to twice its own size.
rationale: Concrete; vital where meals are rare.

headline: Male Anglerfish Fuse to Females
coreClaim: A male anglerfish bites onto a female and permanently fuses to her body, becoming a parasitic mate.
buriedGem: Loses eyes and organs but the testes.

headline: Giant Squid Have Dinner-Plate Eyes
coreClaim: Giant squid have eyes about the size of dinner plates.
buriedGem: ~1 foot across, to gather faint light.

headline: Scientists First Filmed a Giant Squid in Its Home in 2012
coreClaim: Giant squid stayed almost mythical until one was filmed in its natural habitat in 2012.
buriedGem: Known mostly from dead bodies before.

headline: Sperm Whales Carry Giant Squid Battle Scars
coreClaim: Sperm whales carry round sucker scars from deep-sea battles with giant squid.
buriedGem: Squid beaks found in their stomachs.

headline: Giant Kelp Can Grow 18 Inches in a Day
coreClaim: Giant kelp can grow 18 inches in a single day, building underwater "forests."

headline: Kelp Isn't a Plant — and It Has No Roots
coreClaim: Kelp isn't a plant — it's a rootless giant algae gripping rock with a "holdfast."
buriedGem: Closer to pond scum than a tree.

headline: Orcas Are Actually Dolphins
coreClaim: Orcas ("killer whales") are actually the largest member of the dolphin family.

headline: Orcas Are One of the Few Species With Menopause
coreClaim: Orcas are one of only a handful of species (with humans) that go through menopause.

headline: Sea Otters Have the World's Densest Fur
coreClaim: A sea otter can have more hairs in one square inch than you have on your whole head.
buriedGem: Over a million hairs per square inch.

headline: Sea Otters Have Built-In Snack Pockets
coreClaim: Sea otters have built-in "pockets" of loose skin to stash food during dives.

headline: Travel Near Light Speed and You'd Age Slower
coreClaim: Travel near light speed and you return younger than the twin who stayed on Earth.

headline: You Can Never Catch Up to a Beam of Light
coreClaim: Chase light at 99% of its speed and it still races away at full speed.
buriedGem: Speeds don't add up as intuition says.

headline: GPS Needs Relativity to Stay Accurate
coreClaim: GPS needs relativity — satellite clocks tick differently, or locations would drift.
buriedGem: Weaker gravity, higher speed up there.

headline: A Fridge Magnet Beats the Entire Planet's Gravity
coreClaim: A cheap fridge magnet beats the entire planet's gravitational pull.
rationale: Concrete, vivid; gravity is the weakest force.
buriedGem: Six trillion trillion kg loses to a magnet.

headline: Light Could Circle the Earth 7 Times in One Second
coreClaim: Light could circle the Earth more than seven times in one second.

headline: A Particle Can Be in Two Places at Once
coreClaim: Until measured, a single particle can be in many places at once.

headline: Two Particles Can Stay Linked Across the Galaxy
coreClaim: Two entangled particles respond to each other instantly, even galaxies apart.

headline: Particles Can Pass Straight Through Solid Walls
coreClaim: Quantum particles can tunnel through solid barriers — which is why the Sun shines.

headline: Lightning Is Five Times Hotter Than the Sun
coreClaim: A lightning channel is briefly about five times hotter than the Sun's surface.

headline: That Doorknob Shock Is a Tiny Lightning Bolt
coreClaim: That doorknob shock is a tiny lightning bolt — thousands of volts jumping the gap.
rationale: Concrete reframe of a familiar thing.

headline: The Loudest Sound Ever Was Heard 3,000 Miles Away
coreClaim: Krakatoa's 1883 blast may be the loudest sound in history — heard ~3,000 miles away.
rationale: Concrete, vivid (distinct from the disaster category's Krakatoa facts).

headline: Some Sounds Only Teenagers Can Hear
coreClaim: Some high-pitched tones only teenagers can hear — used to repel loiterers.

headline: The Matter in a Paperclip Could Level a City
coreClaim: The matter in a single paperclip, fully converted, could level a city.
rationale: Cited in the writing guide as a model fact; concrete E=mc^2 hook.
buriedGem: A blast like a large bomb.

headline: The Energy in Your Body Came From the Sun
coreClaim: Every move you make is powered by sunlight, passed up the food chain.
rationale: Concrete; even gasoline is ancient sunlight.

headline: Your Resting Body Glows Like a Light Bulb
coreClaim: At rest, your body radiates about as much heat as a 100-watt bulb.
buriedGem: A crowded room warms up fast.

headline: Earth's Magnetic Poles Have Flipped Many Times
coreClaim: Earth's magnetic poles have completely flipped hundreds of times.
buriedGem: A compass would have pointed the other way.

headline: Animals Navigate Using Earth's Magnetism
coreClaim: Birds and sea turtles cross thousands of miles by sensing Earth's magnetic field.
buriedGem: Turtles read the coastline's magnetic signature.

headline: Time Runs Faster at Your Head Than Your Feet
coreClaim: Gravity makes time run faster at your head than your feet — and it's been measured.
rationale: Concrete, mind-bending; measured over a foot.

headline: Days Are Slowly Getting Longer
coreClaim: Days are slowly getting longer — a dinosaur's day was about 23 hours.
buriedGem: The Moon drags on Earth's spin.

headline: The Best Clocks Won't Lose a Second in Billions of Years
coreClaim: The best atomic clocks wouldn't lose a second over the 14-billion-year life of the universe.

headline: In Space, a Thrown Object Coasts Forever
coreClaim: Throw something in deep space and, with nothing to slow it, it coasts forever.
rationale: Concrete; why probes drift on little fuel.

headline: You're Hurtling Through Space Right Now
coreClaim: Sitting still, you're racing around the Sun at ~67,000 mph.
buriedGem: The galaxy sweeps you faster still.

headline: Astronauts Float Because They're Constantly Falling
coreClaim: Astronauts float not because gravity is gone but because they're endlessly falling.
buriedGem: Moving sideways fast enough to keep missing Earth.

headline: The Tablecloth Trick Works Because of Inertia
coreClaim: The tablecloth trick works because the dishes' inertia keeps them still.
rationale: Concrete explanation of a familiar trick.

headline: A Supertanker Can Take Miles to Stop
coreClaim: A loaded supertanker can take miles to stop, even in full reverse.

headline: In Space, Metal Parts Can Weld Themselves Together
coreClaim: In the vacuum of space, clean bare-metal parts can spontaneously weld together.

headline: We Fire Lasers at the Moon to Measure Its Distance
coreClaim: We fire lasers at Moon mirrors and found it's drifting ~1.5 inches farther each year.
rationale: Concrete; a laser as a tape measure.

headline: The Strongest Lasers Briefly Out-Power the Sun
coreClaim: The strongest lasers briefly out-power all the sunlight hitting Earth.

headline: Ocean Waves Barely Move the Water Forward
coreClaim: Ocean waves carry energy, not water — the sea mostly bobs in place.
buriedGem: A gull rises and falls but hardly drifts.

headline: Ripples in Space Itself Were Detected in 2015
coreClaim: In 2015 we detected a ripple in space itself from two black holes colliding.
buriedGem: Stretched space by less than a proton.

headline: Noise-Canceling Headphones Fight Sound With Sound
coreClaim: Noise-canceling headphones erase sound by blasting a mirror-image anti-noise wave.

headline: A Current in a Superconductor Could Flow Forever
coreClaim: A current started in a superconductor could keep looping for billions of years, no battery.

headline: Superconductors Can Make Magnets Float in Midair
coreClaim: Chill a superconductor and a magnet hovers above it — the science behind maglev trains.

headline: The Material That Would End Wasted Power Doesn't Exist Yet
coreClaim: A room-temperature superconductor would end wasted power — and doesn't exist yet.
buriedGem: Today's work hundreds of degrees below zero.

headline: If an Atom Were a Stadium, the Nucleus Would Be a Marble
coreClaim: If an atom were a stadium, the nucleus would be a marble — the solid world is mostly empty.

headline: Steal One Proton From Gold and It Becomes Platinum
coreClaim: Knock one proton off gold and it literally becomes platinum.
rationale: Concrete; the alchemists' dream, made real.

headline: Why Helium Refuses to React With Anything
coreClaim: Helium ignores everything because it's born with a full outer electron shell.
buriedGem: Noble gases have nothing to gain.

headline: A Single Glass of Water Outnumbers the Oceans
coreClaim: One glass of water holds more molecules than there are glasses of water in all the oceans.
buriedGem: Split H2O and it stops being water.

headline: Every Calorie You Burn Is Atoms Rolling Downhill
coreClaim: Every calorie you burn is atoms "rolling downhill" to a lower-energy state.
buriedGem: The leftover energy is your body heat.

headline: The Same Molecule, Mirror-Flipped, Can Be a Poison
coreClaim: The same atoms, mirror-flipped, can be a medicine or a poison (isomers).

headline: A Whole Column of the Table Explodes in Water
coreClaim: A whole left-hand column of the table reacts violently with water.

headline: He Left Blanks in the Table for Elements Nobody Had Found
coreClaim: Mendeleev left blanks for undiscovered elements — and corrected the man who found one.
buriedGem: Told the discoverer to re-measure; he was right.

headline: You Are Mostly Just Six Elements
coreClaim: About 99% of your body is just six elements.
rationale: Concrete; variety from a tiny set.
buriedGem: Oxygen, carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, calcium, phosphorus.

headline: Some Elements Vanish Almost the Instant They're Made
coreClaim: Some elements exist for only a sliver of a second before falling apart.
buriedGem: Less than an ounce of francium on Earth.

headline: A Poison Gas Plus a Metal That Explodes Equals Table Salt
coreClaim: A poison gas plus an explosive metal make harmless table salt.
rationale: Concrete, striking; new compound erases both.
buriedGem: Chlorine + sodium = what's on your fries.

headline: Water's Lopsided Bonds Are Why Ice Floats
coreClaim: Water's lopsided electron sharing is why ice floats and lakes don't freeze solid.

headline: A Diamond Is One Single Giant Molecule
coreClaim: A diamond is technically one single giant molecule of carbon — same element as pencil lead.
buriedGem: Each carbon locked to four neighbors.

headline: A Burning Log Doesn't Vanish, It Floats Away
coreClaim: A burning log doesn't vanish — its mass floats away as invisible gas.
buriedGem: Weigh the smoke and it's all there.

headline: There Are Far More Than Three States of Matter
coreClaim: Beyond solid/liquid/gas lie exotic states — including a superfluid that climbs out of its container.

headline: The Most Common State of Matter Is One You Rarely See
coreClaim: Plasma feels rare on Earth, yet it's the most common state in the visible universe.
buriedGem: You live in a rare cool pocket.

headline: Your Stomach Acid Is 100,000 Times More Acidic Than Water
coreClaim: Stomach acid is about 100,000 times more acidic than water — the pH scale is a cliff, not a ruler.
rationale: Concrete; each step multiplies by ten.

headline: Factory Workers Licked Radium Paint to Tip Their Brushes
coreClaim: Alpha radiation a sheet of paper blocks is the one you must never swallow — the radium-dial painters learned it.
buriedGem: They pointed brushes with their lips.

headline: Some Atoms Decay in a Blink, Others Outlast the Universe
coreClaim: Half-lives range from a trillionth of a second to longer than the universe has existed.
buriedGem: The steady ticking enables carbon dating.

headline: A Metal That Melts in Your Hand
coreClaim: Gallium is a metal that melts in your warm hand.
buriedGem: Mercury is liquid at room temperature.

headline: Some Metals Float on Water and Catch Fire in Air
coreClaim: About three of every four elements are metals — some soft enough to cut with a butter knife.
buriedGem: Metals that float and catch fire in air.

headline: Aluminum Was Once More Precious Than Gold
coreClaim: Aluminum was once more precious than gold — an emperor saved his aluminum cutlery for top guests.
buriedGem: Then a process made it foil.

headline: Rust, a Browning Apple, and a Green Statue Are the Same Reaction
coreClaim: Rust, a browning apple, and the Statue of Liberty's green are all the same electron-swapping reaction.

headline: Oxidation Doesn't Actually Need Oxygen
coreClaim: Despite its name, oxidation is really about losing electrons — and it powers every battery.
rationale: Concrete; oxygen is beside the point.

headline: Breathing Is Just Slow-Motion Burning
coreClaim: Breathing is slow-motion burning — your cells oxidize sugar like a fire, just gently.
buriedGem: Gently burning your breakfast all day.

headline: In Space, a Flame Is a Tiny Blue Sphere
coreClaim: In space a flame is a dim blue sphere — without gravity, no updraft feeds it.
buriedGem: Fire needs gravity to look like fire.

headline: Fire Is a Chain Reaction That Powers Itself
coreClaim: Fire is a self-feeding chain reaction — which is exactly why breaking one link puts it out.
buriedGem: A single match can burn a forest.

headline: Solving Fire Launched Modern Chemistry
coreClaim: Lavoisier solved what fire is and turned chemistry into a measured science — then was executed in the Revolution.
buriedGem: "The Republic has no need of scientists."

headline: Your Enzymes Do in a Second What Would Take Centuries
coreClaim: Enzymes finish in a heartbeat reactions that would otherwise take millions of years.
buriedGem: Life would be too slow without them.

headline: Your Car Turns Poison Into Harmless Gas as You Drive
coreClaim: Your catalytic converter quietly turns poisonous exhaust into harmless gas as you drive.
buriedGem: Platinum and rhodium do it over and over.

headline: Half the Nitrogen in Your Body Was Made in a Factory
coreClaim: About half the nitrogen atoms in your body came from a fertilizer factory (Haber-Bosch).

headline: Two Meters of DNA Are Coiled Inside Every Cell
coreClaim: The DNA in a single cell, unwound, stretches about two meters.
buriedGem: All your cells' DNA reaches the Sun and back many times.

headline: Engineers Still Can't Copy What a Spider Spins in Seconds
coreClaim: Spider silk beats steel weight-for-weight, and labs still can't copy what a spider spins in seconds.
buriedGem: Tougher than steel, stretchier than rubber.

headline: Teflon and Vulcanized Rubber Were Both Lucky Accidents
coreClaim: Teflon and vulcanized rubber were both lucky accidents.
buriedGem: A vanished gas; a spill on a hot stove.

headline: A Crystal's Shape Is Its Atoms Showing Their Work
coreClaim: A crystal's outer shape is a scaled-up readout of its hidden atomic pattern.
buriedGem: Salt grains are cubes because the atoms are.

headline: Every Snowflake Has Six Sides for the Same Reason
coreClaim: Every snowflake has six sides because one six-sided ice pattern repeats throughout.
buriedGem: Branches vary; the six-fold rule never breaks.

headline: Cold Can Turn Solid Tin Into Crumbling Powder
coreClaim: In extreme cold, solid tin's crystals rearrange and it crumbles to powder ("tin pest").
buriedGem: Same metal, different crystal, total collapse.

headline: Cleopatra Lived Closer to the Moon Landing Than to the Great Pyramid
coreClaim: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the building of the Great Pyramid.

headline: King Tut's Tomb Was Found Intact
coreClaim: Nearly every royal tomb was emptied by robbers, but King Tut's was found intact.
rationale: Concrete contrast; a minor king made famous.

headline: Killing a Cat in Egypt Could Be a Death Sentence
coreClaim: Egyptians revered cats so much that killing one could be a death sentence — and they shaved their eyebrows in grief.

headline: The Maya Invented Zero on Their Own, an Ocean Away
coreClaim: The Maya invented zero independently, an ocean away from the Old World.
buriedGem: Powered detailed calendars and star charts.

headline: The Maya Predicted Eclipses Centuries Ahead
coreClaim: The Maya predicted eclipses centuries ahead with no telescopes.
rationale: Cited in the writing guide as a model rewrite; concrete.

headline: Millions of Maya Descendants Still Live Today
coreClaim: The "lost" Maya never vanished — millions of descendants live today.

headline: The Aztecs Built a City on a Lake
coreClaim: The Aztecs built one of the grandest cities on a marshy island in the middle of a lake.

headline: Mexico's Flag Comes From an Aztec Legend
coreClaim: Mexico's flag (eagle, cactus, snake) comes straight from an Aztec founding prophecy.
buriedGem: Where they saw the sign, they built the capital.

headline: The Aztecs Enjoyed Chocolate Before Europe
coreClaim: The Aztecs drank bitter chocolate and used cacao beans as money.

headline: The Inca Built Thousands of Miles of Roads Without the Wheel
coreClaim: The Inca built thousands of miles of mountain roads without the wheel or iron tools.
buriedGem: Rest stops with food and shelter.

headline: The Inca Recorded Information With Knots
coreClaim: The Inca recorded taxes and populations in knotted, colored strings (quipu) — no alphabet.

headline: People in the Andes Still Build Inca Rope Bridges by Hand
coreClaim: Andean communities still hand-weave Inca grass rope bridges, rebuilt because the grass rots.

headline: Your Clock Uses Babylonian Math
coreClaim: Your clock uses Babylonian base-60 math — 60 minutes, 60 seconds, 360 degrees.
rationale: Concrete; an ancient system still ticking.

headline: The First Writing Was Invented to Track Goods, Not Tell Stories
coreClaim: The first writing (cuneiform) was invented to track goods, not tell stories.
buriedGem: Pressed into clay with a reed.

headline: The First Wheels Shaped Pottery, Not Carts
coreClaim: The first wheels shaped pottery, not carts.

headline: Hammurabi Carved 300 Laws on a Pillar for All to See
coreClaim: Hammurabi carved ~300 laws on a public stone pillar for all to see.
rationale: Concrete; law made public and permanent.
buriedGem: Famous for "an eye for an eye."

headline: A Wonder of the World May Never Have Existed
coreClaim: The Hanging Gardens are the only ancient Wonder with no ruins — some think they never existed.

headline: Babylonians Predicted Eclipses Without Telescopes
coreClaim: Babylonians predicted eclipses with no telescopes by recording the sky on clay tablets.
rationale: Nearly identical revelation to maya-astronomy in the same category.
proposedAngle: Trim or differentiate; the Maya version is the guide exemplar. Keep one "ancient eclipse prediction" fact.

headline: Phoenician Sailors May Have Slipped Into the Atlantic
coreClaim: Phoenician sailors may have ventured out of the Mediterranean into the Atlantic.
rationale: Hedged and vague ("may have"); no concrete payoff or destination.
proposedAngle: Re-hook a concrete Phoenician voyage (e.g., the reported circumnavigation of Africa) or trim; the purple-dye and alphabet facts carry the topic.

headline: Royal Purple Came From the Phoenicians
coreClaim: Royal purple came from Phoenician sea-snail dye so costly only royals could afford it.
rationale: Concrete; "Phoenician" may derive from the color.
buriedGem: Squeezed from snails in tiny amounts.

headline: Your Alphabet Traces Back to Phoenician Traders
coreClaim: Our alphabet traces back to a simple Phoenician letter-set traders spread across the Mediterranean.

headline: Cyrus Held His Empire Together by Letting People Keep Their Gods
coreClaim: Cyrus the Great held his vast empire together by letting conquered peoples keep their own gods.
buriedGem: Famous for it, not just conquest.

headline: The Persians Built an Ancient Superhighway
coreClaim: Persia's 1,500-mile Royal Road cut a months-long trip to about a week.

headline: Alexander the Great Burned Persia's Ceremonial Capital
coreClaim: Alexander the Great burned Persia's ceremonial capital, Persepolis.

headline: Indus Cities Ran on Grid Streets and Standardized Bricks
coreClaim: Indus cities ran on grid streets and standardized bricks, millennia ahead of the norm.

headline: Nobody Has Deciphered the Indus Script
coreClaim: No one has deciphered the Indus script after a century of trying.

headline: Nobody Knows Exactly Why the Indus Civilization Disappeared
coreClaim: No one knows exactly why the thriving Indus civilization disappeared.

headline: A Roman Senator Ended Every Speech by Demanding Carthage Be Destroyed
coreClaim: A Roman senator ended every speech — on any topic — demanding Carthage be destroyed.

headline: The Romans Flooded the Colosseum for Sea Battles
coreClaim: The Romans could flood the Colosseum to stage mock naval battles with real ships.

headline: Rome Offered Citizenship to the People It Conquered
coreClaim: Rome grew partly by offering conquered peoples a path to Roman citizenship.
rationale: Concrete; conquest into a shared system.

headline: Rome's First Emperor Called Himself Just 'First Citizen'
coreClaim: Rome's first emperor styled himself merely "first citizen" while wielding real power.
buriedGem: The principate dressed in republican language.

headline: Olmec Sculptors Carved Ruler Portraits Taller Than a Person
coreClaim: Olmec sculptors carved 17 known ruler-portrait heads, some up to ~11 feet tall.

headline: The Oldest Writing in the Americas May Be Olmec
coreClaim: The oldest writing in the Americas may be Olmec, on a ~900 BCE stone slab.

headline: Minoan Youths Leaped Over Bulls as a Religious Ritual
coreClaim: Minoan youths appear to have leaped over live bulls as a religious ritual.
rationale: Concrete, vivid; "someone had to do it."

headline: A Hittite King Raided Babylon
coreClaim: A Hittite king raided down the Euphrates all the way to Babylon, helping end a dynasty.

headline: Both Sides Claimed Victory at Kadesh, Then Signed a Peace Treaty
coreClaim: Both sides claimed victory at Kadesh, then signed history's famous early peace treaty.
rationale: Concrete; boast vs the surviving treaty.

headline: An Anatolian Empire's Metalwork Kicked Off the Iron Age
coreClaim: Hittite metalworking helped kick off the Iron Age.
rationale: A vague, textbook "helped start an age" claim with no concrete hook.
proposedAngle: Re-hook a concrete Hittite iron detail or trim; the Babylon-raid and Kadesh facts are stronger.

headline: A Head Injury Can Give You a Foreign Accent
coreClaim: A brain injury can leave someone speaking their native language in a "foreign" accent.

headline: Shakespeare Sounded Nothing Like a Posh Englishman
coreClaim: Reconstructed "original pronunciation" suggests Shakespeare sounded earthy, not posh.

headline: Even the Queen's Accent Changed Over Her Lifetime
coreClaim: Even Queen Elizabeth II's famously posh accent drifted over her lifetime.
rationale: Concrete; if she drifted, everyone does.

headline: The First Emojis Now Hang in a Museum
coreClaim: The original 1999 set of 176 pixelated emoji is now in MoMA's permanent collection.

headline: Almost Everyone Quietly Replaced the Gun Emoji
coreClaim: Phone makers quietly turned the realistic pistol emoji into a green water gun.
buriedGem: A threat on one phone, a pool toy on another.

headline: A Thumbs-Up Emoji Counted as a Signed Contract
coreClaim: A Canadian court ruled a thumbs-up emoji was a valid contract acceptance, costing the sender tens of thousands.
buriedGem: A cartoon thumb did a signature's work.

headline: One Stone Cracked a Language Dead for 1,400 Years
coreClaim: Hieroglyphs were unreadable for 1,400 years until the Rosetta Stone's Greek text gave the key.

headline: Almost No One in Ancient Egypt Could Read
coreClaim: Only about 1% of ancient Egyptians could read — scribes were a tax-exempt elite.

headline: Sacred-Looking Hieroglyphs Labeled Wine Jars
coreClaim: Egyptians used hieroglyphic jar seals as shipping labels for wine — vineyard and destination.
buriedGem: The sacred script also tracked groceries.

headline: English Doesn't Borrow Words, It Mugs for Them
coreClaim: Over half of English vocabulary is borrowed — English "mugs" other languages for words.
buriedGem: Blunt words native, fancy ones imported.

headline: Ketchup, Sugar, and Alcohol Are All Loanwords
coreClaim: Ketchup, sugar, alcohol, and shampoo are all loanwords tracing old trade routes.
rationale: Concrete; each a fossil of contact.
buriedGem: Ketchup from a Chinese fish sauce.

headline: Some 'English' Words Were Never English at All
coreClaim: Some "English" words (German "Handy" for cellphone) were invented abroad, not by English.

headline: Morse Code Was Designed Using a Box of Printing Type
coreClaim: Morse's letter lengths were set by counting type pieces in a printer's case.
rationale: Concrete; why E is one dot.

headline: A Prisoner Sent a Secret Message by Blinking on TV
coreClaim: A captured pilot blinked a secret Morse warning during a 1966 propaganda interview.

headline: SOS Doesn't Actually Stand for Anything
coreClaim: SOS doesn't stand for anything — it was chosen for its clear Morse pattern.

headline: A 2,000-Year-Old Word Puzzle No One Can Explain
coreClaim: The 2,000-year-old Sator square reads the same four ways — and no one is sure what it meant.

headline: People Have Written Entire Novels as Palindromes
coreClaim: Writers have composed 17,000+ word novels that read identically backward, letter for letter.

headline: Deaf Babies Babble With Their Hands
coreClaim: Deaf babies "babble" with rhythmic hand movements at the same age hearing babies babble vocally.
rationale: Concrete; sign is a true language.

headline: American Signers Can't Understand British Ones
coreClaim: American and British Sign Language are mutually unintelligible — ASL descends from French signing.

headline: A Whole Island Once Spoke Sign Language
coreClaim: On Martha's Vineyard, so many were deaf that for 200 years nearly everyone could sign.
buriedGem: You couldn't recall who was deaf.

headline: Scientists Found a Twister So Hard People Stopped Talking
coreClaim: MIT found a tongue twister so hard some people just stopped talking.

headline: 'She Sells Seashells' Was About a Real Fossil Hunter
coreClaim: "She sells seashells" may honor Mary Anning, a pioneering fossil hunter.
buriedGem: A word game as a quiet monument.

headline: Tongue Twisters Have Been Used to Catch Spies
coreClaim: Hard-to-say words have served as wartime passwords ("shibboleths") to expose spies.

headline: Writing Was Invented From Scratch Only a Few Times
coreClaim: Writing was invented from scratch only a few times ever; almost all scripts descend from those.

headline: One Man Invented a Whole Writing System Alone
coreClaim: Sequoyah, who could read no language, single-handedly invented the Cherokee syllabary.
buriedGem: The nation became literate within years.

headline: Some Ancient Writing Has Never Been Cracked
coreClaim: Scripts like Linear A and rongorongo remain undeciphered to this day.
rationale: Concrete; silent messages in plain sight.
buriedGem: No bilingual key, no living speaker.

headline: The Letter A Is an Upside-Down Ox
coreClaim: The letter A began as an ox's head — flip it and the horns reappear.

headline: Many Written Languages Skip the Vowels
coreClaim: Hebrew and Arabic are written largely without vowels — readers fill them in.

headline: Alphabets Range From 12 Letters to Over 70
coreClaim: Alphabets range from ~12 letters (Rotokas) to over 70 (Khmer).

headline: The Oxford Dictionary Was Crowdsourced by Mail
coreClaim: The OED was crowdsourced by mail — its most prolific volunteer wrote from an asylum cell.

headline: Dictionaries Hide Fake Words to Catch Copycats
coreClaim: Dictionaries plant fake words ("mountweazels") as copyright traps.

headline: An 18th-Century Dictionary Was Full of Sass
coreClaim: Samuel Johnson's 1755 dictionary smuggled in jokes — "oats" feed horses, or Scots.

headline: A Librarian Invented Punctuation
coreClaim: Punctuation began ~200 BCE when an Alexandria librarian invented dots to mark pauses.

headline: Ancient Writing Had No Spaces Between Words
coreClaim: Ancient text ran together with no spaces, so reading was usually aloud.
rationale: Concrete; silent reading once amazed people.

headline: Typewriters Had No Exclamation Point Key
coreClaim: Typewriters long had no exclamation-point key — you built one from a period and apostrophe.

headline: A Translation Slip Convinced the World Mars Had Canals
coreClaim: A mistranslation of "canali" as "canals" convinced the world Mars had built waterways.
rationale: Concrete; one word invented the Martians.

headline: The Most Translated Document Isn't a Holy Book
coreClaim: The most translated document isn't a holy book — it's the UN's 1948 human-rights declaration (500+ languages).

headline: Early Machine Translation Turned Spirit Into Vodka
coreClaim: Early machine translation reputedly turned "the spirit is willing" into "the vodka is good."

headline: Octopuses Are Color-Blind Masters of Camouflage
coreClaim: Octopuses appear color-blind yet produce astonishingly accurate color camouflage.

headline: Mother Octopuses Sacrifice Themselves for Their Eggs
coreClaim: A mother octopus guards her eggs for months, starving, then dies.
buriedGem: Gives her life for the hatchlings.

headline: An Elephant Is Pregnant for Nearly Two Years
coreClaim: An elephant is pregnant for about 22 months — the longest of any mammal.
buriedGem: Calf born at ~200 lbs, stands within hours.

headline: An Elephant Footprint Can Become an Ecosystem
coreClaim: A single deep elephant footprint fills with water and becomes a tiny ecosystem.

headline: Every Dolphin Has Its Own Name
coreClaim: Every dolphin has its own "name" — a signature whistle others copy to call it.

headline: Dolphins Can Hear the Shape of Objects
coreClaim: Dolphins can "hear the shape" of objects, telling fish apart without seeing them.
rationale: Concrete; sensing we can barely imagine.

headline: Dolphins Hunt Using Mud Traps
coreClaim: Some dolphins hunt by stirring a ring of mud to scare fish into the air.

headline: Penguin Fathers Balance Eggs on Their Feet
coreClaim: Emperor penguin fathers balance the egg on their feet for two months without eating.
buriedGem: Under a warm fold of skin.

headline: Emperor Penguins Raise Their Young During Winter
coreClaim: Emperor penguins breed in the depths of the Antarctic winter, when most animals flee.
buriedGem: Chicks ready to feed by summer.

headline: Penguin Chicks Form Daycare Groups
coreClaim: Penguin chicks form "daycare" huddles, and parents find their own by its unique call.

headline: A Giraffe's Neck Has the Same Bones as Yours
coreClaim: A giraffe's six-foot neck has exactly seven bones — the same as yours.
buriedGem: Each vertebra up to 10 inches long.

headline: Giraffes Must Spread Their Legs to Drink
coreClaim: A giraffe's neck is too short to reach the ground, so it must splay its legs to drink.

headline: Other Animals Use Giraffes as Lookouts
coreClaim: Zebras and antelope watch giraffes as tall lookouts for danger.

headline: A Tiger's Stripes Are in Its Skin, Not Just Its Fur
coreClaim: A tiger's stripes are in its skin, not just its fur — unique as a fingerprint.

headline: A Tiger Can Eat 80 Pounds of Meat in One Meal
coreClaim: A tiger can eat 80 pounds of meat in one meal, then not eat for days.
buriedGem: Making the most of each kill.

headline: Every Wolf Pack Has Its Own Howl
coreClaim: Every wolf pack has its own distinctive howl, letting members recognize family from afar.

headline: Older Wolf Siblings Help Raise Pups
coreClaim: Older wolf siblings help raise pups — guarding them and bringing food.
buriedGem: Coughing up meat for the young.

headline: A Sprinting Cheetah Is Airborne Half the Time
coreClaim: A sprinting cheetah is airborne more than half of every stride.
buriedGem: The spine coils like a spring.

headline: The Black Lines on a Cheetah's Face Act Like Sunglasses
coreClaim: A cheetah's black facial "tear marks" cut sun glare like an athlete's eye black.

headline: Cheetahs Are Big Cats That Can't Roar
coreClaim: The cheetah is a big cat that can't roar — it chirps almost like a bird.

headline: Being Slow May Help Sloths Stay Alive
coreClaim: A sloth's slowness is a survival strategy — predators track motion, so it barely moves.

headline: Sloths Wear Living Camouflage
coreClaim: Green algae grow right in a sloth's fur, giving it living camouflage.

headline: Sloths Visit the Bathroom Only About Once a Week
coreClaim: Sloths defecate only about once a week — and the climb down is the most dangerous moment of their lives.
buriedGem: Exposed to predators on the ground.

headline: A Platypus Bill Is Soft and Sensor-Packed
coreClaim: A platypus bill is soft and packed with thousands of receptors to find prey underwater.
rationale: Concrete; the bill is a hunting tool.

headline: Male Platypuses Are Venomous
coreClaim: Male platypuses carry venomous stingers on their back feet.

headline: Platypuses Chew With Gravel
coreClaim: Toothless platypuses use swallowed gravel to mash their food.

headline: Axolotls Stay Like Forever Tadpoles
coreClaim: Axolotls stay like "forever tadpoles," keeping juvenile gills and underwater life for life.
buriedGem: Never make the switch to land.

headline: Axolotls Can Regrow a Leg in About a Month
coreClaim: An axolotl can regrow a whole lost leg in about a month.

headline: Axolotls Are Named After an Aztec God
coreClaim: The axolotl is named after Xolotl, an Aztec god who turned into the creature to escape enemies.

headline: Chameleons Don't Only Change Color to Hide
coreClaim: Chameleons change color not just to hide but to warm up, court, and signal.

headline: A Chameleon's Skin Has See-Through Layers
coreClaim: A chameleon's color change happens as pigment cells shift beneath a clear outer skin.
buriedGem: Red and blue mix to purple.

headline: Chameleon Eyes Can Look Two Ways at Once
coreClaim: A chameleon's eyes can swivel and look in two different directions at once.

headline: Raven Nests Can Be Five Feet Wide
coreClaim: Raven nests can be about five feet across.
rationale: A mild size fact that undersells one of the smartest birds; weakest of three.
proposedAngle: Trim, or replace with a stronger raven-intelligence fact (e.g., planning for the future, holding grudges).

headline: Ravens Play Catch in Midair
coreClaim: Ravens play catch in midair — dropping objects in flight to swoop and grab them.

headline: Ravens Can Work Together to Solve Problems
coreClaim: Ravens cooperate to solve new problems, investigate objects, and cache food for later.
rationale: Concrete intelligence, if a touch general.

headline: Mantis Shrimp Punch Faster Than a Blink
coreClaim: A mantis shrimp throws a punch ~50 times faster than a human blink.

headline: A Mantis Shrimp's Punch Lands Twice, Thanks to Bubbles
coreClaim: A mantis shrimp's punch lands twice — collapsing bubbles add a second burst of force.

headline: Mantis Shrimp Eyes See Light Humans Cannot
coreClaim: Mantis shrimp eyes see ultraviolet and circularly polarized light humans can't.
rationale: Concrete; the most complex eyes known.

headline: Banks Create Most Money Out of Thin Air
coreClaim: Banks create most new money out of thin air when they make a loan.

headline: Your Bank Doesn't Actually Have Your Money
coreClaim: Your bank doesn't hold your money — it lends most of it out, so all savers can't withdraw at once.

headline: The World's Oldest Bank Has Operated Since 1472
coreClaim: The oldest surviving bank (Monte dei Paschi, 1472) opened before Columbus sailed.
buriedGem: Central banks as lender of last resort.

headline: The 'Barter Before Money' Story Is Probably a Myth
coreClaim: Anthropologists have never found a real barter economy — the "money replaced barter" story is likely a myth.
buriedGem: Early trade ran on credit and IOUs.

headline: In POW Camps, Cigarettes Became Real Money
coreClaim: In WWII POW camps, cigarettes became a full currency — even among non-smokers.

headline: Some Cultures Gained Status by Giving Wealth Away
coreClaim: In the potlatch, leaders raised status by giving away or destroying wealth — governments banned it.
buriedGem: The more you gave up, the higher you rose.

headline: Your Dinner Depends on the Baker's Self-Interest, Not Kindness
coreClaim: Adam Smith's "invisible hand": your dinner comes from the baker's self-interest, not kindness.

headline: Two Koreas Look Like Night and Day From Space
coreClaim: From space at night, prosperous South Korea glows while North Korea is dark.

headline: There Are More $100 Bills Than $1 Bills
coreClaim: There are more $100 bills in circulation than $1 bills — most held overseas.
buriedGem: The bill you see least is the most common.

headline: This Island Used Giant Stone Coins Too Heavy to Move
coreClaim: The island of Yap used car-sized stone coins too heavy to move — one is on the sea floor.
rationale: Cited in the writing guide as a model; concrete, tellable.
buriedGem: Ownership changes without the stone moving.

headline: Adding '.com' to Your Name Could Pump Your Stock
coreClaim: In the dot-com mania, just adding ".com" to a company's name could pump its stock.

headline: The World Believed House Prices Could Never Fall
coreClaim: The 2008 crash grew from one shared belief: US house prices could never fall nationwide.
rationale: Concrete; a single assumption nearly broke the economy.

headline: Isaac Newton Lost a Fortune in a Stock Bubble
coreClaim: Isaac Newton lost a fortune in the 1720 South Sea Bubble.
rationale: Concrete, tellable; even a genius wasn't immune.
buriedGem: "I can calculate the heavens, not the madness of people."

headline: The World Was Already 'Globalized' Over a Century Ago
coreClaim: The world economy was nearly as connected in 1913 as today — until wars reversed it.

headline: Your Valentine's Roses Probably Flew In From the Equator
coreClaim: Your Valentine's roses probably flew in from near the equator within a day or two.
buriedGem: Kenya, Colombia, Ecuador by refrigerated plane.

headline: The 'Victorian Internet' Connected Continents in 1866
coreClaim: The 1866 transatlantic telegraph cable cut messages between continents from ten days to minutes.
buriedGem: Markets on two continents moved together.

headline: Disasters and Car Crashes Can Make GDP Go Up
coreClaim: Disasters and car crashes can make GDP go up, because it just counts spending.

headline: GDP's Inventor Warned Us Not to Use It This Way
coreClaim: GDP's inventor, Simon Kuznets, warned that a nation's welfare "can hardly be inferred" from it.
rationale: Concrete; we do exactly what he warned against.

headline: One Country Measures Happiness Instead of GDP
coreClaim: Bhutan officially measures "Gross National Happiness" instead of GDP alone.

headline: Germans Once Burned Cash to Stay Warm
coreClaim: In Germany's 1923 hyperinflation, burning cash for heat was cheaper than buying firewood.

headline: One Country's Prices Doubled Every 15 Hours
coreClaim: Hungary's 1946 prices doubled every ~15 hours — the fastest inflation ever.

headline: Central Banks Actually Want a Little Inflation
coreClaim: Central banks deliberately aim for ~2% inflation because deflation is more dangerous.

headline: Charging Interest Was Once a Sin
coreClaim: Charging interest was condemned as the sin of "usury" for centuries; Islamic finance still forbids it.
buriedGem: A moneylender could be denied burial.

headline: That $24 for Manhattan Could've Beaten Owning It
coreClaim: The legendary $24 for Manhattan, invested since 1626, could now top the island's value.

headline: Some Banks Paid People to Borrow Money
coreClaim: In the 2010s, negative interest rates meant some banks paid people to borrow.

headline: A Famous Study Found Higher Wages Didn't Kill Jobs
coreClaim: A famous fast-food study (Card & Krueger) found raising the minimum wage didn't cut jobs.

headline: Some Rich Countries Have No Minimum Wage at All
coreClaim: Sweden, Denmark, and Switzerland have no legal minimum wage — unions set pay instead.

headline: The 'Two Bad Quarters' Recession Rule Is a Myth
coreClaim: The "two negative quarters" recession rule is a myth — a committee decides, often a year late.
buriedGem: The NBER weighs jobs and income.

headline: Everyone Saving Money Can Make a Recession Worse
coreClaim: If everyone sensibly saves in a downturn, falling spending makes the recession worse (paradox of thrift).
buriedGem: Wise for one household, harmful for all.

headline: 'Depression' Was Picked Because It Sounded Gentle
coreClaim: "Depression" was chosen because it sounded gentler than "panic" — then became the byword for disaster.

headline: One Stuck Ship Jammed $10 Billion of Trade a Day
coreClaim: When the Ever Given wedged across the Suez Canal in 2021, it stalled ~$10 billion in trade a day.
buriedGem: ~12% of global trade through one waterway.

headline: A Missing Chip Can Halt an Entire Car Factory
coreClaim: A shortage of one cheap chip idled car factories worldwide in 2021 ("just-in-time" fragility).
rationale: Concrete; a modern car needs thousands of chips.
buriedGem: No stockpiles to fall back on.

headline: Why a Small Demand Blip Causes Huge Shortages
coreClaim: The "bullwhip effect" magnified a small demand blip into 2020's toilet-paper shortages.

headline: England Once Taxed You by Your Windows
coreClaim: England once taxed houses by their windows, so people bricked them up — maybe the root of "daylight robbery."

headline: The Income Tax Was Sold as Temporary
coreClaim: The US income tax began as a temporary Civil War measure and, made permanent in 1913, hit only the wealthy.

headline: A Napkin Sketch Reshaped Tax Policy
coreClaim: The Laffer curve, reputedly sketched on a napkin in 1974, argued lower rates can raise revenue.

headline: College's Biggest Cost Isn't Tuition
coreClaim: College's biggest cost isn't tuition — it's the wages you give up while studying.

headline: Bill Gates Shouldn't Stop to Pick Up a $100 Bill
coreClaim: Bill Gates earns so fast that stopping to grab a $100 bill could "cost" him more in time.
buriedGem: Every minute has an opportunity cost.

headline: Why 'We've Come This Far' Is a Costly Trap
coreClaim: The sunk-cost fallacy kept Britain and France funding the doomed Concorde.
rationale: Concrete; "we've come this far" is a trap.

headline: Why Useless Diamonds Cost More Than Vital Water
coreClaim: Vital water is cheap and useless diamonds are costly because price follows scarcity, not importance.
buriedGem: Price tracks the next unit's scarcity.

headline: Companies Invent Scarcity on Purpose
coreClaim: Companies engineer artificial scarcity — the Disney "vault," limited sneaker drops — because rarity raises value.

headline: Being Short on Money Can Tax Your Brain
coreClaim: The mental strain of scarcity (especially money) can drain brainpower like a sleepless night.
rationale: Concrete; poverty as a hidden tax on attention.

headline: Early Blood Transfusions Got Banned for 150 Years
coreClaim: Early animal-to-human blood transfusions were so deadly that transfusion was banned for ~150 years.

headline: Doctors Once Tried Transfusing Milk Into Patients
coreClaim: Before blood transfusion was understood, some doctors tried transfusing milk.
buriedGem: The right fluid, not just any fluid.

headline: One Chemical Turned Blood Into Something You Can Bank
coreClaim: Adding citrate to stop clotting let blood be stored, creating the modern blood bank.
rationale: Concrete; chemistry made a supply chain.

headline: Nobody Fully Knows How Anesthesia Switches You Off
coreClaim: Anesthesia reliably erases consciousness, yet no one fully knows how.

headline: Surgery's Most Famous Room Is Named After a Gas
coreClaim: The 1846 operation that proved painless surgery happened in a room still called the Ether Dome.

headline: Surgical Anesthesia Started as a Party Trick
coreClaim: Ether and laughing gas were party drugs for years before anyone used them to stop surgical pain.

headline: The First Penicillin Patient Was Saved by His Own Urine
coreClaim: Penicillin was so scarce doctors recovered it from a patient's urine to keep dosing him.
rationale: The headline says the patient was "saved," but the body says he died — a headline/body contradiction.
proposedAngle: Fix the headline to match the body (he died despite the effort); the revelation itself is strong.

headline: Most Antibiotics Are Stolen Microbial Weapons
coreClaim: Most antibiotics are chemical weapons microbes evolved to fight each other — we just borrowed them.
rationale: Concrete; why they do nothing to viruses.

headline: Bacteria Trade Resistance Like Swapping Cards
coreClaim: Bacteria trade antibiotic-resistance genes sideways, even across species, like swapping cards.

headline: Fewer Than 50 People Have 'Golden Blood'
coreClaim: Fewer than 50 people have "golden blood" (Rh-null), which lacks every Rh antigen.
buriedGem: Precious, but can receive only from each other.

headline: He Turned Transfusion From a Deadly Lottery Into Science
coreClaim: Landsteiner's discovery of blood groups turned transfusion from a deadly lottery into science.
buriedGem: His birthday is World Blood Donor Day.

headline: The Tech Behind Ultrasound Started by Hunting Submarines
coreClaim: Medical ultrasound grew out of naval SONAR built to hunt submarines.
buriedGem: War tech became the gentlest way to see inside.

headline: Beatles Records Helped Pay for the First CT Scanner
coreClaim: The first CT scanner was built at EMI, funded partly by Beatles record sales.

headline: A PET Scan Lights Up Cancer Because Tumors Love Sugar
coreClaim: A PET scan lights up cancer because tumors consume radioactive sugar fastest.

headline: He Proved Handwashing Saved Lives and Was Locked Away
coreClaim: Semmelweis proved handwashing saved mothers' lives, was ridiculed, and died in an asylum.

headline: Surgeons Once Bragged About Their Blood-Caked Coats
coreClaim: Before germ theory, surgeons wore blood-caked coats as a badge of how busy they were.
buriedGem: The dirtier the coat, the busier the doctor.

headline: Pasteur Tested His Rabies Vaccine on a Dying Boy
coreClaim: Pasteur gambled an unproven rabies vaccine on a dying boy in 1885 — and it saved him.
buriedGem: No license, tested only on animals.

headline: Without Insulin, You Starve in a Sea of Sugar
coreClaim: Without insulin, cells can't absorb blood sugar, so the body starves amid plenty.

headline: Insulin Woke Children From Diabetic Comas Overnight
coreClaim: In 1922, doctors injecting new insulin woke a whole ward of comatose diabetic children.
buriedGem: Kids waking before they finished the row.

headline: The Discoverers Sold the Insulin Patent for $1
coreClaim: Insulin's discoverers sold the patent for $1, saying it belonged to the world.

headline: An MRI Magnet Can Turn a Gas Tank Into a Missile
coreClaim: An MRI's always-on magnet can hurl a steel oxygen tank across the room like a missile.
rationale: Concrete, vivid; why metal is banned.

headline: A Dead Salmon 'Lit Up' in a Brain Scanner
coreClaim: Researchers found fake "brain activity" in a dead salmon, exposing a flaw in scan analysis.

headline: An MRI Basically Listens to the Water Inside You
coreClaim: An MRI basically listens to the water in your body, reading faint radio signals it sends back.

headline: Placebos Can Work Even When You Know They're Fake
coreClaim: Placebos can work even when patients are told outright the pills are fake.

headline: The Placebo Has an Evil Twin: the Nocebo
coreClaim: The placebo has an evil twin — the nocebo — where expecting harm produces real side effects.

headline: A Pill's Color and Price Change How Well It Works
coreClaim: A placebo's color, size, and even price change how well it seems to work.
buriedGem: Red feels like a stimulant, blue calming.

headline: The Oldest Known Prosthetic Is a 3,000-Year-Old Toe
coreClaim: The oldest known working prosthetic is a 3,000-year-old wooden toe on an Egyptian mummy.

headline: Some Bionic Hands Now Move and Feel by Thought
coreClaim: Advanced bionic hands connect to nerves so users move — and sometimes feel — by thought.

headline: Running Blades Work Like Springs, Not Legs
coreClaim: Carbon-fiber running blades store and release energy like springs, not legs.
rationale: Concrete; doesn't copy nature to outperform.

headline: A Skilled Ear Can Hear a Collapsed Lung
coreClaim: A trained ear with a stethoscope can detect pneumonia or a collapsed lung by sound alone.
rationale: Concrete; careful listening as a diagnostic window.

headline: Embarrassment Invented the Stethoscope
coreClaim: Laennec invented the stethoscope because he was too embarrassed to press his ear to a patient's chest.

headline: Medicine's Most Famous Tool Started as a Rolled Paper Tube
coreClaim: The 200-year-old stethoscope endures as medicine's symbol despite pocket-ultrasound rivals.
rationale: Repeats the rolled-paper-tube origin already told (better) in stethoscope-distance-invention.
proposedAngle: Trim, or re-hook a distinct angle; the embarrassment-origin and lung-listening facts are stronger.

headline: The First Successful Transplant Was Between Twins
coreClaim: The first successful organ transplant (1954) worked because it was between identical twins.
rationale: Concrete; the body didn't see the kidney as foreign.
buriedGem: Pushed the search for anti-rejection drugs.

headline: Corneas Rarely Reject Because the Body Can't See Them
coreClaim: Corneas rarely reject because they have no blood supply, hiding them from the immune system.

headline: The First Heart Transplant Made a Surgeon World-Famous Overnight
