coreClaim: The first heart transplant (1967) made the surgeon an overnight global celebrity.

headline: Smallpox Is the Only Human Disease Wiped From the Wild
coreClaim: Smallpox is the only human disease wiped from the wild — samples survive in two guarded freezers.

headline: The Word 'Vaccine' Literally Means 'From a Cow'
coreClaim: The word "vaccine" literally means "from a cow" (Latin vacca), after Jenner's cowpox breakthrough.

headline: Shoe Stores Used to X-Ray Your Feet for Fun
coreClaim: Shoe stores once let customers X-ray their own feet for fun.

headline: An X-Ray Is a Shadow, Not a Photo
coreClaim: An X-ray is a shadow, not a photo — dense bone blocks the rays to leave a pale outline.
buriedGem: Casting a shadow of your insides.

headline: The First X-Ray Made a Woman Say She'd Seen Her Death
coreClaim: Roentgen's first X-ray showed his wife's hand bones; she said "I have seen my own death."

headline: A Surgical Robot Can Peel and Stitch a Grape
coreClaim: A surgical robot can peel and stitch a grape — it scales motion and cancels hand tremor.

headline: The Surgeon Never Touches the Patient
coreClaim: In robotic surgery the surgeon works from a nearby console and never touches the patient.
rationale: Overlaps the NY-operates-on-France fact's "surgeon at a distant console" point.
proposedAngle: Trim, or fold the setup detail into another robot fact; the grape and transatlantic facts are stronger.

headline: A Surgeon in New York Operated on a Patient in France
coreClaim: In 2001 a surgeon in New York removed a gallbladder from a patient in France by robot.

headline: Fishermen Named El Nino After the Christ Child
coreClaim: Peruvian fishermen named El Nino after the Christ child because the warm water arrives near Christmas.
buriedGem: Weak winds shut down the fishery.

headline: One Patch of Warm Sea Can Flood Peru and Parch Australia at Once
coreClaim: One warm patch of Pacific can flood Peru and parch Australia at the same time.

headline: El Nino Has a Cold Twin Named 'the Girl'
coreClaim: El Nino has a cold twin, La Nina ("the girl"), swinging every 2–7 years.
rationale: Concrete; the biggest driver of year-to-year weather.

headline: Whole Trade Empires Were Built on Monsoon Winds
coreClaim: A monsoon is a seasonal wind reversal — whole trade empires were timed to it.
buriedGem: Sailors rode it like a conveyor belt.

headline: More Than a Billion People Live on the Monsoon's Schedule
coreClaim: Over a billion people live on the Indian monsoon's schedule; a weak one moves global food prices.
buriedGem: A whole economy waits for it.

headline: The Monsoon Makes the Wettest Places on Earth
coreClaim: Monsoon rains make Mawsynram and Cherrapunji the wettest places on Earth — ~11–12 m a year.
buriedGem: The same system has a dry winter.

headline: Cut a Hailstone Open and You'll See Tree-Like Rings
coreClaim: Cut a hailstone open and you'll see tree-like rings from riding a storm's updraft up and down.
buriedGem: Big stones rode the elevator dozens of times.

headline: The Ice That Dents Your Car Falls on Hot Summer Days
coreClaim: The big damaging hail forms in hot summer thunderstorms, while sleet is a quiet winter event.

headline: Record Hailstones Are Bigger Than Softballs
coreClaim: The largest US hailstone (~20 cm, nearly a kilogram) fell in 2010.

headline: The Foggiest Places Are Where Warm and Cold Water Meet
coreClaim: The foggiest places are where warm and cold ocean currents meet, like Newfoundland's Grand Banks.

headline: Some Towns Harvest Drinking Water From Fog
coreClaim: Some dry towns harvest drinking water from fog using mesh nets.

headline: London's Killer Fog of 1952 Took Thousands of Lives
coreClaim: London's 1952 Great Smog trapped coal smoke and is blamed for ~12,000 deaths.
buriedGem: A "pea souper" so dense you couldn't see your feet.

headline: Rainbows Can Glow at Night by Moonlight
coreClaim: Rainbows can glow at night as faint "moonbows," and a double rainbow's second arc runs in reverse.

headline: No Two People Ever See the Same Rainbow
coreClaim: No two people ever see the same rainbow — and your two eyes don't either.
rationale: Concrete; why you can never reach it.

headline: From an Airplane, a Rainbow Becomes a Full Circle
coreClaim: A rainbow is actually a full circle — from a plane you can see the whole ring.
buriedGem: The horizon hides the lower half.

headline: A Solar Storm Once Made Telegraphs Spark and Skies Glow at the Equator
coreClaim: The 1859 Carrington solar storm made auroras glow near the equator and telegraphs spark.
buriedGem: Lines ran on the air's own charge.

headline: Every Northern Aurora Has a Secret Twin in the South
coreClaim: Every northern aurora has a near-mirror twin glowing over Antarctica at the same moment.
buriedGem: Same physics on Jupiter and Saturn.

headline: The Aurora's Colors Are a Map of Which Gas Is Glowing
coreClaim: An aurora's color is a chemical fingerprint — oxygen glows green or red, nitrogen blue or purple.
buriedGem: All-red auroras once read as omens.

headline: Earth Is Closest to the Sun in the Dead of Northern Winter
coreClaim: Earth is closest to the Sun in early January — seasons come from axial tilt, not distance.

headline: Near the Poles, the Sun Doesn't Set for Months
coreClaim: Near the poles the Sun doesn't set for months (the "midnight sun"), then months of darkness.
buriedGem: One very long day and night.

headline: London Once Held Winter Fairs on the Frozen Thames
coreClaim: During the Little Ice Age the Thames froze so hard London held "Frost Fairs" on it.
buriedGem: An elephant once led across the ice.

headline: Ice Ages Come and Go With a Wobble in Earth's Orbit
coreClaim: Ice ages are paced by slow wobbles in Earth's orbit and tilt (Milankovitch cycles).
buriedGem: We live in a warm gap.

headline: Earth Has Been a Frozen Snowball and a Crocodile Arctic
coreClaim: Earth's climate has swung from a near-frozen "Snowball" to a crocodile-filled warm Arctic.
buriedGem: One setting on a wide dial.

headline: Thunder Is the Sound of Air Being Blown Apart
coreClaim: Thunder is the shockwave of air violently exploding outward as lightning superheats it.
buriedGem: Air hotter than the Sun's surface.

headline: 'Heat Lightning' Is Just a Storm Too Far Away to Hear
coreClaim: "Heat lightning" is a myth — it's ordinary lightning from a storm too far away to hear.

headline: Snow Isn't Actually White
coreClaim: Snow isn't actually white — its clear ice crystals scatter all light; deep snow can look blue.

headline: It Can Be Too Cold to Snow, and Snow Can Turn Pink
coreClaim: It can be "too cold to snow," and algae can turn mountain snow pink ("watermelon snow").

headline: A Man Spent His Life Proving No Two Snowflakes Match
coreClaim: Wilson "Snowflake" Bentley photographed 5,000+ snowflakes to show no two are alike.

headline: Every Cloud Droplet Forms on a Speck of Something
coreClaim: Every cloud droplet forms by condensing onto a tiny speck — dust, salt, even bacteria.
rationale: Concrete; a cloud is water on debris.

headline: An Amateur Forecaster Gave Clouds Their Latin Names
coreClaim: An amateur, Luke Howard, gave clouds their Latin names in 1802 — still used today.

headline: Storm Clouds Can Grow Taller Than Mount Everest
coreClaim: Storm clouds (cumulonimbus) can grow taller than Mount Everest, into the stratosphere.
buriedGem: The flat "anvil" warns of severe weather.

headline: There's a Layer of Air That Is 2,000 Degrees but Feels Freezing
coreClaim: In the thermosphere air can hit 2,000 C yet feel freezing — the air is too thin to transfer heat.
buriedGem: Warmth depends on how many molecules hit you.

headline: All of Earth's Weather Fits in a Layer Thinner Than Apple Skin
coreClaim: Nearly all weather fits in a shell of air proportionally thinner than an apple's skin.
buriedGem: 99% of air mass near the ground.

headline: Earth's Spin Is Why Deserts Ring the Globe
coreClaim: Earth's spin organizes winds into belts — and is why deserts ring the globe near 30 degrees.

headline: One Lake Has Lightning Almost Every Night
coreClaim: Venezuela's Lake Maracaibo has lightning about 300 nights a year — once used as a navigation beacon.

headline: Lightning Fuses Sand Into Glass, and One Man Survived Seven Strikes
coreClaim: Lightning can fuse sand into glass tubes (fulgurites), and one ranger survived seven strikes.

headline: Lightning Really Does Strike the Same Place Twice
coreClaim: Lightning really does strike the same place twice — the Empire State Building ~25 times a year.
buriedGem: Franklin's rod offers a safe path down.

headline: A Single Hurricane Out-Powers Every Power Plant on Earth
coreClaim: A single mature hurricane releases far more energy each day than all the world's power plants combined.
buriedGem: Powered by warm sea and condensing rain.

headline: Inside a Hurricane's Eye, the Sky Turns Calm and Blue
coreClaim: A hurricane's eye can be calm with blue sky, ringed by the violent eyewall — even trapping birds.
buriedGem: Birds ride hundreds of miles inside.

headline: A Hurricane's Surge Caused America's Deadliest Disaster
coreClaim: Storm surge from the 1900 Galveston hurricane killed ~8,000 — the deadliest US disaster.
rationale: Concrete; water, not wind, does most killing.

headline: Most of the World's Tornadoes Hit One Country
coreClaim: The US gets ~75% of the world's tornadoes because cold Canadian and warm Gulf air collide.

headline: Storms Have Literally Rained Fish and Frogs
coreClaim: Waterspouts and tornadoes can suck up fish and frogs and rain them down miles away.
rationale: Concrete; the "rains of animals" are real.
buriedGem: Sounds like myth, but it happens.

headline: The Worst Tornadoes Can Drive Straw Into Wood
coreClaim: EF5 tornado winds top 300 mph — strong enough to drive straw into wood.

headline: The Post-it Glue Was a 'Failure' for Five Years
coreClaim: Post-it glue was an accidental "failed" weak adhesive its inventor pitched for five years before anyone wanted it.

headline: The Post-it Note Was Born in a Church Choir
coreClaim: The Post-it Note was born from a choir singer's slipping hymnal bookmark.
rationale: Concrete; matched a problem to an orphaned invention.

headline: Post-it Notes Are Yellow Purely by Accident
coreClaim: Post-it Notes are canary yellow only because the lab next door had scrap yellow paper.

headline: He Invented the Safety Pin in Hours to Pay a Debt
coreClaim: Walter Hunt invented the safety pin in a few hours from one wire to settle a $15 debt.

headline: A 3,000-Year-Old Idea Became a Punk Symbol
coreClaim: The safety pin's design is millennia old, and punk culture later made it a fashion symbol.
buriedGem: Ancient fibulae to punk to solidarity.

headline: The Safety Pin Inventor Also Gave Up a Sewing Machine Fortune
coreClaim: Walter Hunt also refused to patent his early sewing machine, fearing job losses — missing a fortune.
rationale: Concrete; "brilliant at inventing, terrible at profiting."

headline: A Journalist Invented the Ballpoint by Copying Newspaper Ink
coreClaim: Biro invented the ballpoint after noticing newspaper ink dried fast and never smudged.
buriedGem: A tiny ball rolled the gooey ink.

headline: The Air Force Made the Ballpoint Pen Take Off
coreClaim: The ballpoint took off when the RAF adopted it because it didn't leak at high altitude.
rationale: Concrete; a niche need proved it.

headline: People Once Mobbed Stores to Buy a $200 Pen
coreClaim: Ballpoint pens caused buying frenzies in 1945 — about $200 each in today's money.
buriedGem: Gimbels sold out $10,000 worth day one.

headline: The Barcode Was First Drawn in Beach Sand
coreClaim: The barcode was first drawn in beach sand — stretched Morse-code dots and dashes into lines.
buriedGem: A doodle became a global symbol.

headline: The Barcode Was Patented 20 Years Before Anyone Could Use It
coreClaim: The barcode was patented in 1952 but waited 20 years for cheap lasers and computers.
rationale: Concrete; a good idea ahead of its tech.

headline: The First Item Ever Scanned Was a Pack of Gum
coreClaim: The first product ever scanned by barcode was a pack of Wrigley's gum, now in the Smithsonian.

headline: Velcro Got Famous in Space, but NASA Didn't Invent It
coreClaim: NASA didn't invent Velcro but made it famous by anchoring floating gear in orbit.
buriedGem: A Swiss fastener, a space showcase.

headline: 'Velcro' Is a Brand That Begs You Not to Say It
coreClaim: "Velcro" is a trademark, and the company campaigns for people to say "hook-and-loop" instead.
buriedGem: A brand fighting its own fame.

headline: A 2,000-Year-Old Jar May Have Been a Battery
coreClaim: A ~2,000-year-old clay "Baghdad Battery" jar can actually produce a current.
buriedGem: Or it may have just stored scrolls.

headline: One Battery Has Been Ringing a Bell Since 1840
coreClaim: Oxford's Electric Bell has rung on a single sealed battery since ~1840 — its recipe still unknown.
buriedGem: Opening it would ruin the experiment.

headline: The First Battery Settled a Fight About Frog Legs
coreClaim: Volta built the first true battery to win an argument about twitching frog legs.

headline: A Pandemic Rescued the QR Code From Being a Joke
coreClaim: QR codes were mocked as a gimmick until the COVID pandemic pushed them into daily life.
rationale: Concrete revival story (distinct from the tech-category QR origin).

headline: A QR Code Still Works With a Chunk Missing
coreClaim: A QR code still scans with up to ~30% of it damaged or covered, thanks to error correction.
buriedGem: Why logos can sit in the middle.

headline: QR Codes Spread Because the Inventor Gave Them Away
coreClaim: QR codes spread worldwide because Denso Wave chose not to enforce its patent.
buriedGem: Free access from a factory tool.

headline: Almost All the Internet Travels Under the Ocean
coreClaim: About 99% of international internet data travels through cables on the ocean floor, not satellites.
rationale: Concrete assumption-gap (distinct from the tech-category internet fact).
buriedGem: The "cloud" is wires in the dark sea.

headline: There Are Enough Internet Addresses for Every Grain of Sand
coreClaim: IPv6 has enough addresses for every grain of sand on Earth, many times over.

headline: The First Webcam Was Invented to Watch a Coffee Pot
coreClaim: The world's first webcam was set up so Cambridge researchers could check the coffee pot.
buriedGem: Nobody wanted a wasted coffee trip.

headline: A Shot-Down Airliner Is Why GPS Is Free for Everyone
coreClaim: After a Korean airliner was shot down in 1983, Reagan opened military GPS to the world for free.
rationale: Concrete; a tragedy made it public.

headline: The Military Used to Blur Civilian GPS on Purpose
coreClaim: The military deliberately blurred civilian GPS until 2000 — switching it off made it ~10x sharper.

headline: GPS Is Secretly a Clock the World Depends On
coreClaim: GPS is secretly a clock the world depends on — power grids, banks, and networks sync to it.
buriedGem: If GPS failed, the chaos would be lost time.

headline: A 14-Year-Old Imagined TV While Plowing a Field
coreClaim: A 14-year-old imagined electronic TV's scan lines while plowing a field in rows.
buriedGem: The screen still paints in rows.

headline: Countless Early TV Shows Are Lost Forever
coreClaim: Countless early TV shows are lost forever — aired live or taped over to save money.
buriedGem: Gone into the air the instant broadcast.

headline: The First Televisions Were Mechanical, With a Spinning Disk
coreClaim: The first working TVs were mechanical, using a spinning perforated disk built from spare parts.
buriedGem: A hatbox, knitting needles, bicycle lights.

headline: The Supreme Court Said Tesla, Not Marconi, Invented Radio

headline: Radio Dramas Faked Horses With Coconut Shells
coreClaim: Radio dramas faked galloping horses with clapping coconut shells.
buriedGem: Cellophane for fire, metal for thunder.

headline: Edison Tested Thousands of Materials, and Bamboo Won
coreClaim: Edison's team tested ~6,000 filament materials — carbonized bamboo won, lasting 1,000+ hours.

headline: One Light Bulb Has Been Glowing Since 1901
coreClaim: California's "Centennial Light" bulb has glowed almost continuously since 1901.

headline: Edison Electrocuted Animals to Win the 'War of Currents'
coreClaim: To discredit rival AC power, Edison publicly electrocuted animals during the "War of the Currents."

buriedGem: He also promoted the first electric chair.
proposedAngle: Human glance on age-appropriateness; if it fails, re-hook the AC-vs-DC rivalry without the animal cruelty.

headline: Bell Beat a Rival to the Patent Office by Hours
coreClaim: Bell beat rival Elisha Gray to the patent office by mere hours on the same day in 1876.
buriedGem: A few hours decided the telephone.

headline: Bell Wanted Us to Answer the Phone With 'Ahoy'
coreClaim: Bell wanted people to answer the phone with "Ahoy" and refused to keep one in his study.

headline: A Telegraph Giant Dismissed the Phone as a 'Toy'
coreClaim: Western Union reportedly rejected buying the telephone patent, calling it a useless "toy."
buriedGem: You once asked an operator by name.

headline: The Printing Press Was Built From a Wine Press
coreClaim: Gutenberg built his printing press by adapting a wine/olive screw press.

headline: Gutenberg's Real Invention Was a Secret Recipe
coreClaim: Gutenberg's real breakthrough was reusable movable type — an alloy, a mold, and oily ink.
buriedGem: Melt the letters down and reuse them.

headline: 'Upper Case' and 'Cliché' Come From the Print Shop
coreClaim: "Uppercase," "lowercase," "stereotype," and "cliché" all come from the print shop.
buriedGem: Capitals in the upper case tray.

headline: The Hard Part of the Wheel Was the Axle, Not the Wheel
coreClaim: The hard part of the wheel was the precisely fitted axle, not the round wheel.

proposedAngle: Keep one "the axle was the hard part" fact across engineering and inventions; differentiate or trim the other.

headline: The Americas Had Wheeled Toys but No Wheeled Carts
coreClaim: Ancient Americans made wheeled toys but never used wheels for transport — no draft animals.
buriedGem: So the wheel stayed a toy.

headline: The One-Wheeled Wheelbarrow Was a Military Secret
coreClaim: The single-wheeled wheelbarrow appeared in China and was reportedly a military secret.

headline: Listerine Invented a Disease to Sell Mouthwash
coreClaim: Listerine invented a "disease" — halitosis — to sell mouthwash, and sales exploded.
rationale: Concrete; they sold the fear of a problem.

headline: One Ad Campaign Convinced the World to Buy Diamond Rings
coreClaim: The diamond engagement ring isn't old tradition — it's a 1947 De Beers ad slogan.

headline: The First Web Banner Ad Got Almost Half Its Viewers to Click
coreClaim: The internet's first banner ad (1994) got a 44% click rate — thousands of times today's average.

headline: Coca-Cola Could Burn Down and Rebuild on Its Name Alone
coreClaim: A Coca-Cola exec claimed the brand name alone could rebuild the whole company from ashes.
rationale: Concrete; the value of a name.
buriedGem: Valued in the tens of billions.

headline: People Prefer Pepsi in Blind Tests but Coke on the Shelf
coreClaim: People prefer Pepsi in blind tests but Coke on the shelf — the label overrides taste.
buriedGem: The brand lit up reward areas on scans.

headline: The Most Valuable Company Ever Existed 400 Years Ago
coreClaim: The most valuable company ever may be the 1602 Dutch East India Company — worth trillions.
buriedGem: It had its own army and money.

headline: One Share of This Company Costs Over Half a Million Dollars
coreClaim: One share of Berkshire Hathaway costs over half a million dollars — more than a house.

headline: The World's Oldest Company Ran for Over 1,400 Years
coreClaim: The world's oldest company, a Japanese temple builder, ran for over 1,400 years.

headline: He Built 5,127 Vacuums Before One Worked
coreClaim: James Dyson built 5,126 failed vacuum prototypes before the 5,127th worked.
rationale: Concrete; failure, not a flash of genius.
buriedGem: Deep in debt when it succeeded.

headline: Why So Many Giant Companies Started in a Garage
coreClaim: Apple, Amazon, Disney, and Google all began in garages.

headline: Capitalism Grows by Destroying Itself
coreClaim: Schumpeter argued capitalism advances through "creative destruction" — each innovation kills the old.
rationale: Concrete; the churn is the engine.

headline: The Game Monopoly Was Invented to Attack Monopolies
coreClaim: Monopoly began as an anti-monopoly teaching game — then became a tribute to bankrupting rivals.

headline: Breaking Up His Monopoly Made Rockefeller Richer
coreClaim: Breaking up Standard Oil into 34 companies made Rockefeller richer — he owned them all.
buriedGem: The breakup helped make him the richest man.

headline: You Used to Have to Rent Your Telephone
coreClaim: Under AT&T's monopoly, Americans rented their telephones and couldn't own one until 1984.

headline: In 2020, Oil Was Worth Less Than Nothing
coreClaim: In April 2020, US oil went negative — sellers paid buyers to haul it away.

headline: Some Products Sell Better When You Raise the Price
coreClaim: Some luxury "Veblen goods" sell better when the price goes up — the cost signals status.

headline: Capping Prices Can Empty the Shelves
coreClaim: Capping prices below market tends to empty the shelves, like the 1970s gas lines.
buriedGem: The price tag stays friendly; the product vanishes.

headline: Not a Single Person on Earth Can Make a Pencil
coreClaim: No single person on Earth knows how to make a pencil from scratch.

headline: Even If You're Better at Everything, You Should Still Specialize
coreClaim: Even if you're better at everything, you should still specialize (comparative advantage).

headline: A Simple Steel Box Built the Modern World
coreClaim: The plain steel shipping container cut freight costs 90%+ and quietly built globalization.

headline: The Word 'Brand' Comes From Burning Cattle
coreClaim: The word "brand" comes from searing an ownership mark onto cattle with a hot iron.
buriedGem: Modern branding does the same job, minus the fire.

headline: Why So Many Fast-Food Logos Are Red and Yellow
coreClaim: Fast-food logos flood red and yellow because the colors are thought to spark hunger and urgency.
buriedGem: Red for appetite, yellow to keep you moving.

headline: A New Logo Lasted Just Six Days
coreClaim: Gap's redesigned logo lasted six days; a Tropicana redesign cost millions before both reversed.

headline: McDonald's Makes More From Real Estate Than Burgers
coreClaim: McDonald's often makes more money as a landlord than from selling burgers.
buriedGem: It rents the land to franchisees.

headline: The McDonald's You Know Was Taken From Two Brothers
coreClaim: Ray Kroc franchised the McDonald brothers' restaurant, then bought them out and pushed them aside.

headline: The World's Biggest Chain Is Japanese-Owned 7-Eleven
coreClaim: 7-Eleven has more locations than any chain on Earth — and the Texas-born brand is now Japanese-owned.

headline: Most Startups Fail Because Nobody Wanted the Product
coreClaim: Most startups fail not from money or rivals but because nobody wanted the product.

headline: A 'Unicorn' Startup Was Named for Being Rare
coreClaim: "Unicorn" was coined in 2013 for rare billion-dollar startups — now there are over a thousand.

headline: Instagram Started as a Check-In App, YouTube as a Dating Site
coreClaim: Instagram began as a check-in app, YouTube as a dating site, Slack as a failed game's chat tool.
buriedGem: The real business hid in the wreckage.

headline: Wall Street Began Under a Tree in 1792
coreClaim: The New York Stock Exchange began in 1792 as 24 brokers signing a pact under a buttonwood tree.

headline: Machines Now Trade Stocks in Millionths of a Second
coreClaim: Machines now trade stocks in microseconds — one 2010 "Flash Crash" erased ~$1 trillion in minutes.

headline: A Single Tulip Bulb Once Cost More Than a House
coreClaim: In 1630s Holland, a single tulip bulb once cost more than a house, then crashed.

headline: Business Cards Are Over 500 Years Old
coreClaim: The business card traces back to 15th-century China.

headline: Folding a Calling Card's Corner Sent a Secret Message
coreClaim: Victorians folded a calling card's corners in coded ways to send silent messages.

headline: In Japan, You Receive a Business Card With Both Hands
coreClaim: In Japan, exchanging a business card (meishi) is a two-handed ceremony you can fumble.
buriedGem: The card as an extension of the person.

headline: Abraham Lincoln Is the Only US President With a Patent
coreClaim: Abraham Lincoln is the only US president to hold a patent — a boat-lifting device.
buriedGem: "The fuel of interest to the fire of genius."

headline: Someone Patented Swinging Sideways on a Swing
coreClaim: The US once patented swinging sideways on a swing — filed for a five-year-old.

headline: A Patent War Nearly Grounded American Aviation
coreClaim: The Wright brothers' patent battles stalled US aviation so badly the WWI government forced a patent pool.

headline: Coca-Cola's Recipe Sits in a Bank Vault
coreClaim: Coca-Cola never patented its formula — guarding it as a trade secret in an Atlanta vault.

headline: WD-40 Was Never Patented on Purpose
coreClaim: WD-40 was never patented on purpose, so it never had to publish its formula.

headline: KFC Splits Its Secret Recipe Between Two Companies
coreClaim: KFC splits its secret recipe between two companies, neither knowing the whole.

coreClaim: You can trademark a sound, a color, or even a smell — NBC's chimes, UPS brown, a scented toy.

headline: Aspirin and Escalator Were Once Brand Names

headline: Nestle Spent Years Failing to Trademark the KitKat Shape
coreClaim: Nestle spent years and lost trying to trademark the four-finger shape of a KitKat.

buriedGem: Courts ruled it not distinctive enough.

headline: Amelia Earhart Vanished Almost Within Reach of Land

headline: Some Domes Let You Hear a Whisper Across the Room
coreClaim: In a domed "whispering gallery" a whisper carries clearly across 30+ meters.

headline: A Glass Skyscraper Melted a Parked Car
coreClaim: London's concave "Walkie-Talkie" tower focused sunlight so intensely it melted parts of a parked car.

headline: Glass Towers Kill Up to a Billion Birds a Year
coreClaim: Birds can't see glass — collisions kill up to a billion of them a year in the US alone.

headline: An Architect Controlled Even the Building's Blinds
coreClaim: Mies van der Rohe designed the Seagram Building's blinds to lock in only three positions.

headline: Flying Buttresses Let Cathedral Walls Become Glass
coreClaim: Flying buttresses carried a cathedral's roof thrust outside, freeing the walls to become stained glass.
rationale: Concrete; the skeleton moved out for the light.
buriedGem: Glass can't hold a stone roof.

headline: A Cathedral Could Take Six Centuries to Finish
coreClaim: A Gothic cathedral could take six centuries to finish — Cologne's ran 1248 to 1880.
buriedGem: The masons never saw the spires.

headline: 'Gothic' Started as an Insult
coreClaim: "Gothic" started as an insult — Renaissance critics compared the style to barbarian Goths.

headline: The Great Pyramid Was the Tallest Building for 3,800 Years
coreClaim: The Great Pyramid was the tallest human-made structure for about 3,800 years.
buriedGem: No iron tools or wheels for hauling.

headline: The Great Pyramid Points Almost Perfectly North
coreClaim: The Great Pyramid's sides align to true north within a fraction of a degree — without compasses.

headline: The World's Largest Pyramid Is Hidden Under a Hill
coreClaim: The world's largest pyramid by volume is in Mexico — so overgrown that conquistadors built a church atop it.
buriedGem: Disguised as a hill for centuries.

headline: 'Skyscraper' Meant a Tall Hat Before a Tall Building
coreClaim: "Skyscraper" was slang for any tall thing — a high sail, a tall horse, even a towering hat — before buildings.

headline: The Chrysler Building Hid a Spire to Win a Race
coreClaim: The Chrysler Building hid a spire inside, then raised it to steal the "world's tallest" title.

headline: Elevators Made the Top Floor the Best Address
coreClaim: Before elevators the top floor was the worst — a long climb; elevators made it the prized penthouse.

headline: Paris's Grand Boulevards Were Designed to Stop Revolts
coreClaim: Paris's grand boulevards were partly designed to stop revolts — too wide to barricade.
rationale: Concrete; the City of Light, revolution-proofed.

headline: Car Companies Invented the Crime of Jaywalking
coreClaim: The 1920s auto industry invented the crime of "jaywalking" to claim the streets for cars.

headline: Brazil Built a Capital From Scratch in Under 4 Years
coreClaim: Brazil built its capital, Brasilia, from empty scrubland in under four years — laid out like an airplane.
buriedGem: A city designed as one diagram.

headline: Castle Staircases Spiral Clockwise for a Reason
coreClaim: Castle spiral staircases usually wind clockwise so a right-handed defender above had room to swing.
rationale: Concrete; the architecture fought for the defender.
buriedGem: The attacker's arm jammed the pillar.

headline: Castle Toilets Just Dropped Waste Down the Wall
coreClaim: A castle's "garderobe" toilet dumped waste down the outer wall — and clothes stored in its fumes gave us "wardrobe."

headline: The Classic Fairy-Tale Castle Is a Victorian Fake
coreClaim: Neuschwanstein, the fairy-tale castle that inspired Disney, is a 19th-century fantasy with running water.

headline: A Day on Venus Is Longer Than a Year
coreClaim: A day on Venus (243 Earth days) is longer than its year (225).

headline: Venus Spins Backward
coreClaim: Venus spins backward — its Sun rises in the west.
buriedGem: Maybe flipped by a giant crash.

headline: Mars Is Entirely Inhabited by Robots
coreClaim: Mars is entirely inhabited by robots — no human has ever stood there.

headline: Sunsets on Mars Are Blue
coreClaim: On Mars the daytime sky is butterscotch and the Sun sets in cool blue.

headline: A Rover Has Used AI to Drive on Another Planet
coreClaim: A rover (Perseverance) used AI to drive itself on another planet.

headline: Asteroids Are Older Than Earth
coreClaim: Asteroids are older than Earth — leftovers from before the planets formed.

headline: All the Asteroids Together Weigh Less Than the Moon
coreClaim: All the asteroids together weigh less than the Moon.
buriedGem: Most mass in a few big ones.

headline: Humans Have Successfully Moved an Asteroid
coreClaim: In 2022, humans crashed a spacecraft into an asteroid and actually moved it (DART).
buriedGem: First time we moved a space object.

headline: There Are More Planets Than Stars in Our Galaxy
coreClaim: There are more planets than stars in our galaxy.

headline: Some Planets Wander Through Space Alone
coreClaim: Some "rogue" planets wander space alone, with no star at all.

headline: Scientists Have Found Lava Worlds and Styrofoam Worlds
coreClaim: Known exoplanets include oceans of lava and one so light it's compared to Styrofoam.

headline: A Comet's Tail Always Points Away From the Sun
coreClaim: A comet's tail always points away from the Sun — even leading it on the way out.
buriedGem: Blown by sunlight and solar wind.

headline: A Comet Once Crashed Into Jupiter
coreClaim: In 1994 a comet crashed into Jupiter, leaving scars visible from Earth.

headline: 'Oumuamua Was Humanity's First Known Interstellar Visitor
coreClaim: ‘Oumuamua was the first known interstellar visitor to our solar system (2017).
buriedGem: Traveled millions of years to reach us.

headline: Only Five Dwarf Planets Are Official, but Many More Are Out There
coreClaim: Only five dwarf planets are official, but perhaps 100+ are still hiding past Neptune.

headline: Haumea Is One of the Fastest-Spinning Worlds Known
coreClaim: Haumea spins so fast it's been stretched into an egg shape.

headline: The Official Planet Definition Didn't Exist Until 2006
coreClaim: There was no official definition of "planet" until 2006.

headline: One Thousand Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter
coreClaim: About 1,000 Earths could fit inside Jupiter.
buriedGem: Made mostly of hydrogen and helium.

headline: Jupiter Has the Shortest Day in the Solar System
coreClaim: The biggest planet, Jupiter, has the shortest day — under 10 hours.

headline: Jupiter's Great Red Spot Is Slowly Disappearing
coreClaim: Jupiter's Great Red Spot — big enough to swallow Earth — is slowly shrinking.
buriedGem: Three Earths wide a century ago, now barely one.

headline: One of Saturn's Moons Has Lakes and Rain
coreClaim: Saturn's moon Titan has lakes, rivers, and rain — of liquid methane, not water.
buriedGem: The only other place with surface liquid.

headline: A Small Moon May Hide an Ocean Beneath Its Ice
coreClaim: Saturn's little moon Enceladus blasts its hidden ocean into space through geysers.
rationale: Concrete; a top place to search for life.

headline: Uranus Rotates on Its Side
coreClaim: Uranus is tipped ~98 degrees and rolls around the Sun on its side.
buriedGem: Probably knocked over by a crash.

headline: Neptune Sits Farther From the Sun, Yet Uranus Is Colder
coreClaim: Neptune sits farther out, yet Uranus is the coldest planet — and no one knows why it gives off no heat.

headline: The First New Planet in Human History Was Mistaken for a Comet
coreClaim: The first new planet in human history (Uranus, 1781) was logged as a comet.
rationale: Concrete; the solar system pushed outward.
buriedGem: Herschel didn't realize what he had.

headline: Pluto Has a Giant Heart on Its Surface
coreClaim: A spacecraft revealed a giant heart-shaped region on Pluto's surface.

headline: Pluto Has a Blue Sky
coreClaim: Pluto, billions of miles from the Sun, has a blue sky.

headline: Pluto and Its Moon Orbit a Point in Empty Space
coreClaim: Pluto's moon is so big they orbit a point in empty space between them.
buriedGem: Like two dancers spinning as a pair.

headline: One Trip Around the Galaxy Takes 230 Million Years
coreClaim: One trip around the galaxy takes ~230 million years — dinosaurs lived on the far side.

headline: Only 24 Humans Have Ever Traveled to the Moon
coreClaim: Only 24 humans have ever traveled to the Moon; just 12 walked on it.

headline: A Hidden Ninth Planet May Be Lurking Out There
coreClaim: A hidden "Planet Nine" may be herding distant icy worlds with its gravity.
buriedGem: No one has ever seen it.

headline: The Sunlight on Your Skin Is Thousands of Years Old
coreClaim: The sunlight on your skin may have taken tens of thousands of years to escape the Sun's core.
buriedGem: Born before the last Ice Age.

headline: The Northern Lights Are the Sun Crashing Into Our Air
coreClaim: The northern lights are really the Sun crashing into Earth's air.
rationale: Concrete; auroras start at the Sun.

headline: Neptune May Have Stopped a Planet From Forming
coreClaim: A whole planet may have failed to form because Neptune kept stirring the pieces apart.

headline: Pluto Crosses Neptune's Orbit but Avoids Neptune
coreClaim: Pluto crosses Neptune's orbit yet never gets near it, locked in a 3:2 timing resonance.

headline: A Spacecraft Visited a Red Snowman World
coreClaim: A spacecraft visited "Arrokoth," a tiny world shaped like a flattened red snowman.
rationale: Concrete; the most distant object explored.

headline: Voyager 1 Still Has 300 Years to Reach the Oort Cloud
coreClaim: Even Voyager 1 won't reach the Oort Cloud for about 300 years.

headline: Sunlight Takes Days to Reach the Oort Cloud
coreClaim: Sunlight reaches Earth in 8 minutes but takes days to reach the Oort Cloud.
buriedGem: Up to 1.5 years to cross it.

headline: Some Comets Last Visited Before Humans Existed
coreClaim: Some comets take so long to return that humans didn't exist the last time they visited.

headline: Meteor Showers Usually Don't Drop Meteorites
coreClaim: Meteor showers usually drop no meteorites — the comet debris burns up.

headline: About 48 Tons of Space Rock Hit Earth Every Day
coreClaim: About 48 tons of space rock hit Earth every day.

headline: Voyager 2 Launched Before Voyager 1
coreClaim: Voyager 2 actually launched before Voyager 1.

headline: Only One Spacecraft Has Ever Visited Uranus or Neptune
coreClaim: Only one spacecraft (Voyager 2) has ever visited Uranus or Neptune — in 1989.

headline: One Moon Has Hundreds of Active Volcanoes
coreClaim: Jupiter's moon Io has hundreds of active volcanoes, some erupting hundreds of miles into space.

headline: A Moon May Hide More Water Than Earth
coreClaim: Jupiter's moon Europa may hide more water than all of Earth's oceans under its ice.
rationale: Concrete; a top place to search for life.

headline: The Largest Moon Is Bigger Than Mercury
coreClaim: The largest moon (Ganymede) is bigger than the planet Mercury.
buriedGem: It even has a magnetic field.

headline: Guinness World Records Started From a Pub Argument
coreClaim: Guinness World Records began as a way to settle pub arguments.

headline: The Guinness Book Is One of the Most-Stolen Library Books
coreClaim: The Guinness Book of Records is itself one of the most-stolen library books.

headline: Alice in Wonderland Was Banned for Its Talking Animals
coreClaim: Alice in Wonderland was banned in 1930s China for letting animals talk like people.

headline: The Catholic Church Kept a Banned-Books List for 400 Years
coreClaim: The Catholic Church kept an official banned-books list (Galileo, Kant, Descartes) for 400 years.

headline: Putting a Book on Trial Made It a Bestseller
coreClaim: Winning its 1960 obscenity trial made Lady Chatterley's Lover sell ~2 million copies in weeks.

headline: A 10-Cent Comic Now Sells for Millions
coreClaim: A 1938 dime comic (Action Comics #1, Superman's debut) has sold for over $3 million.

headline: The Term 'Yellow Journalism' Came From a Comic Strip
coreClaim: "Yellow journalism" is named after a comic strip, the Yellow Kid.

headline: A Comic About the Holocaust Won a Pulitzer Prize
coreClaim: Maus, a Holocaust graphic novel, became the first comic to win a Pulitzer (1992).
rationale: Concrete; the "funny pages" as serious literature.
buriedGem: Jews as mice, Nazis as cats.

headline: The Detective Story Was Invented Before the Word Existed
coreClaim: Poe invented the detective story in 1841 — before "detective" was even a common word.
rationale: Concrete; a genre for a job that barely existed.
buriedGem: A crime cracked by pure reasoning.

headline: Readers Forced an Author to Resurrect Sherlock Holmes
coreClaim: When Conan Doyle killed Sherlock Holmes, outraged fans mourned until he resurrected him.

headline: The Queen of Mystery Vanished for 11 Days
coreClaim: In 1926 Agatha Christie vanished for 11 days, sparking a manhunt she never fully explained.

headline: The Oldest Epic Has a Flood Story Older Than Noah's
coreClaim: The 4,000-year-old Epic of Gilgamesh has a flood-and-ark tale strikingly like Noah's, written centuries earlier.
buriedGem: A bird released to find dry land.

headline: We Don't Know If Homer Ever Really Existed
coreClaim: We don't know if Homer ever existed — the epics grew from centuries of bards singing from memory.
buriedGem: Illiterate Balkan singers proved it possible.

headline: The Longest Poem Ever Runs to 1.8 Million Words
coreClaim: The longest poem ever, India's Mahabharata (~1.8M words), is ~10x the Iliad and Odyssey combined.

headline: The Grimms Rewrote Their Own Fairy Tales to Be Gentler
coreClaim: The Grimms rewrote their own fairy tales to be gentler edition after edition.
rationale: Concrete; the cheerful versions are the cleaned-up ones.

headline: Some Fairy Tales Are Thousands of Years Old
coreClaim: Some fairy tales trace back ~6,000 years — older than the Bible or Greek mythology.

headline: The Original Little Mermaid Dissolves Into Sea Foam
coreClaim: In Andersen's original, the Little Mermaid loses the prince and dissolves into sea foam.
buriedGem: Legs that felt like walking on knives.

headline: Atlantis Came From a Single Ancient Author
coreClaim: Every Atlantis legend traces to a single Plato passage (~360 BCE), likely a moral fable.
rationale: Overlaps the dedicated myths-legends/Atlantis topic; Atlantis is covered in two categories.
proposedAngle: Keep one home for Atlantis (the richer myths-legends topic) and use a different legend to illustrate "legends from one author" here.

headline: Dracula Was Based on a Real, Brutal Prince
coreClaim: Bram Stoker's Dracula draws his name and menace from a real, brutal 15th-century prince.
buriedGem: Vlad the Impaler; the Order of the Dragon.

headline: The Pied Piper May Record a Real Tragedy
coreClaim: A 1284 Hamelin record says 130 children were lost — hinting the Pied Piper recalls a real tragedy.
buriedGem: No rats or magic in the record.

headline: The World's Oldest Library Was Founded by a Woman
coreClaim: The world's oldest still-working library was founded by a woman, Fatima al-Fihri, in 859 CE.

headline: Alexandria Stole Books From Every Passing Ship
coreClaim: Alexandria seized books off docking ships to copy — and likely faded slowly, not in one fire.

headline: A Library Tried to Save Every Public Tweet
coreClaim: The Library of Congress set out to archive every public tweet in 2010, then gave up by 2017.

headline: Some Myths Remember the Coastline From 10,000 Years Ago
coreClaim: Aboriginal Australian myths accurately describe coastlines from up to 10,000 years ago.
rationale: Concrete; maybe Earth's oldest true stories.

headline: You Name Norse Gods Every Day of the Week
coreClaim: Tuesday through Friday are named for Norse gods, hidden in your weekly calendar.

headline: Star Wars Was Built on an Ancient Story Pattern
coreClaim: George Lucas built Star Wars on the "hero's journey," a pattern Campbell found in myths worldwide.

headline: The First Novel May Have Been Written by a Woman in 1000 CE
coreClaim: The first novel may have been written by a woman (Murasaki Shikibu) around 1000 CE.
buriedGem: The Tale of Genji, ~600 years before Cervantes.

headline: The Longest Novel Ever Runs to 1.2 Million Words
coreClaim: The longest novel ever, Proust's In Search of Lost Time, runs ~1.2 million words.
buriedGem: A cake in tea unleashes it all.

headline: A Novel Helped Push a Nation Toward War
coreClaim: Uncle Tom's Cabin so inflamed anti-slavery feeling that Lincoln reportedly called Stowe "the little woman who started this great war."

headline: The First Author in History Was a Woman Poet
coreClaim: The first author in history known by name was a woman poet, Enheduanna (~2300 BCE).

headline: A Great Poet Published Almost Nothing While Alive
coreClaim: Emily Dickinson wrote ~1,800 poems but published fewer than a dozen while alive.
buriedGem: Found in a drawer after her death.

headline: A 13th-Century Persian Poet Is a US Bestseller Today
coreClaim: A 13th-century Persian poet, Rumi, is among the best-selling poets in America today.

headline: Books Were Once So Precious They Were Chained Down
coreClaim: Before printing, hand-copied books were so precious that libraries chained them to the shelves.
buriedGem: A book cost as much as a small farm.

headline: Printing Made More Books in 50 Years Than the Past 1,000
coreClaim: In its first 50 years the printing press made ~20 million books — more than the prior millennium.

headline: Cheap 'Penny Dreadfuls' Sparked a Panic Over Trashy Books
coreClaim: Cheap Victorian "penny dreadfuls" created mass readers and the first panic over "trashy" fiction.
rationale: Concrete; the same panic later hit comics and games.
buriedGem: A penny for crime and horror.

headline: Shakespeare Left His Wife His 'Second-Best Bed'
coreClaim: Shakespeare's life is so murky he spelled his name several ways and willed his wife only his "second-best bed."

headline: You Quote Shakespeare Without Knowing It
coreClaim: Shakespeare coined or popularized 1,000+ words and phrases we still use — "eyeball," "break the ice."

headline: Half of Shakespeare's Plays Were Nearly Lost
coreClaim: Without the 1623 First Folio compiled by two friends, ~18 plays (including Macbeth) might be lost.
rationale: Concrete; two friends saved half his work.

headline: The Oldest Known Story Is Painted on a Cave Wall
coreClaim: The oldest known story is a 44,000-year-old hunting scene painted in an Indonesian cave.

headline: Scheherazade Told Stories to Stay Alive
coreClaim: In 1,001 Nights, Scheherazade survives by ending each night on a cliffhanger the king must hear resolved.

headline: When a Griot Dies, 'a Library Burns'
coreClaim: West African griots memorize centuries of history — "when a griot dies, a library burns."

headline: The Great Gatsby's Cover Was Painted Before the Book Was Done
coreClaim: The Great Gatsby's iconic eyes were painted before the book was finished — Fitzgerald wrote them in.
rationale: Concrete; the cover shaped the story.

headline: Paperbacks Once Cost the Same as a Pack of Cigarettes
coreClaim: Penguin's 1935 paperbacks cost about a pack of cigarettes and color-coded covers by genre.
buriedGem: Orange for fiction, green for crime.

headline: The Wrapper People Threw Away Is Now Worth a Fortune
coreClaim: Dust jackets were thrown away as trash — now an original can be worth more than the book itself.
buriedGem: The difference between hundreds and a fortune.

headline: J.K. Rowling Has Hidden Behind Two Pen Names
coreClaim: J.K. Rowling hid behind two pen names — initials to mask her gender, then "Robert Galbraith."

headline: 'Mark Twain' Is Really a Riverboat Command
coreClaim: "Mark Twain" is a riverboat call meaning the water was two fathoms deep and safe to pass.

headline: Nancy Drew's Author Never Actually Existed
coreClaim: Nancy Drew's author "Carolyn Keene" never existed — a publisher's name shared by ghostwriters.
buriedGem: The Hardy Boys used the same trick.

headline: A Real Volcanic Disaster May Be Behind Atlantis
coreClaim: A real volcanic disaster — the Bronze Age eruption of Thera that crippled the Minoans — may lie behind Atlantis.

headline: A US Congressman Launched the Modern Atlantis Craze
coreClaim: An 1882 bestseller by a US congressman launched the modern Atlantis-as-real-civilization craze.

headline: The Nazis Went Searching for Atlantis
coreClaim: Nazi occultists claimed Atlantis was a lost "Aryan" homeland and funded expeditions to find it.

headline: Eastern Dragons Are the Opposite of Western Ones
coreClaim: European dragons are evil monsters to slay; Chinese dragons are lucky, wise bringers of rain.

headline: Old Maps Almost Never Said 'Here Be Dragons'
coreClaim: The famous map warning "here be dragons" survives on essentially one object — a ~1510 globe.
rationale: Concrete; a phrase more myth than fact.

headline: There's a Real Dragon, and It's Venomous
coreClaim: There's a real dragon — the Komodo dragon, a venomous predator that can kill a water buffalo.

headline: Egyptian Embalmers Threw the Brain Away
coreClaim: Egyptian embalmers pulled the brain out through the nose and threw it away.
rationale: Concrete, striking; they thought the heart did the thinking.

headline: Your Heart Was Weighed Against a Feather After Death
coreClaim: Egyptians believed the dead's heart was weighed against a feather; too heavy, and a monster ate it.

headline: Egypt Made Millions of Animal Mummies
coreClaim: Ancient Egyptians mummified millions of animals as offerings — a huge religious industry.
buriedGem: Breeders raised ibises to be mummified.

headline: Two Girls Faked Fairy Photos and Fooled a Genius
coreClaim: The 1917 Cottingley fairy photos — really paper cutouts — fooled Sherlock Holmes's creator.

headline: People Called Fairies 'the Good Folk' Out of Fear
coreClaim: Fearful villagers flattered dangerous fairies as "the Good Folk" and blamed sick babies on "changelings."

headline: Fairy Rings Are Real, Just Not Magic
coreClaim: Fairy rings of mushrooms are real — a single fungus spreading outward, some centuries old.
rationale: Concrete; the magic circle is one organism.

headline: The Greek Gods Behaved Like a Bad Soap Opera
coreClaim: The Greek gods behaved like a bad soap opera — petty, jealous, and cruel.
buriedGem: A divine family feud, not scripture.

headline: You Speak Greek Mythology Every Day
coreClaim: You speak Greek mythology daily — panic, narcissism, echo, tantalize, nemesis.
buriedGem: The gods live in our vocabulary.

headline: Greek Monsters May Have Been Misread Fossils
coreClaim: Greek monsters like the Cyclops may have come from misread fossils — elephant skulls look one-eyed.

headline: One Medieval Writer Mostly Invented King Arthur
coreClaim: Much of the King Arthur legend was effectively invented by one 12th-century writer.

headline: Lancelot and Camelot Were French Additions
coreClaim: Lancelot, Camelot, and the Holy Grail were French additions to the British Arthur legend.

headline: Monks 'Found' King Arthur's Grave for Tourism
coreClaim: Cash-strapped Glastonbury monks "found" King Arthur's grave in 1191 — likely a tourism hoax.

headline: A Famous Scientist Listed the Kraken as Real

headline: Sailors Feared the Kraken Was as Big as an Island
coreClaim: Legend said the kraken was so vast sailors mistook it for an island — likely inspired by giant squid.
rationale: Concrete; fear stretched a big animal into a landmass.

headline: 'Release the Kraken!' Gave the Monster New Life
coreClaim: "Release the Kraken!" turned an old Norse legend into a pop icon — even a Seattle NHL team.
buriedGem: The kraken wasn't even in Greek myth.

headline: Nessie's Most Famous Photo Was a Toy Submarine
coreClaim: Nessie's most famous 1934 photo was a fake — a model head on a toy submarine.

headline: The Loch Ness Legend Is 1,400 Years Old
coreClaim: The Loch Ness legend is ~1,400 years old — a 565 AD account has Saint Columba repelling a "water beast."

headline: A DNA Survey Found No Monster, but Lots of Eels
coreClaim: A 2018 DNA survey of Loch Ness found no monster but lots of eel DNA.

headline: Columbus Saw 'Mermaids' That Were Really Manatees
coreClaim: Columbus logged seeing "mermaids" in 1493 that were really manatees — "not half as beautiful as painted."

headline: A Famous 'Mermaid' Was a Monkey Sewn to a Fish
coreClaim: P.T. Barnum's celebrated "FeeJee Mermaid" was a monkey's torso sewn onto a fish tail.

headline: The Original Sirens Were Bird-Women, Not Fish
coreClaim: The original Greek sirens were winged bird-women, not fish-tailed mermaids.

headline: 'Labyrinth' Comes From a Double-Headed Axe
coreClaim: "Labyrinth" likely derives from labrys, the sacred double-headed axe of Minoan Crete.

headline: Athens Sent Teenagers to Be Eaten by the Minotaur
coreClaim: In the myth, Athens had to send 14 young people into the labyrinth to be devoured as tribute.

headline: 'Ariadne's Thread' Became a Real Problem-Solving Term
coreClaim: "Ariadne's thread," from the Minotaur myth, is now a real problem-solving term.

headline: Nearly Every Culture Has a Phoenix
coreClaim: Nearly every culture has a phoenix — Egypt's Bennu, China's Fenghuang, Russia's Firebird.

headline: The Phoenix Builds Its Own Funeral Pyre
coreClaim: The phoenix builds its own fragrant funeral pyre, burns to ashes, and a new bird rises.
buriedGem: Destruction and rebirth in one act.

headline: Phoenix, Arizona Was Named for Rising From Ruins

buriedGem: Ancient canals, then a modern city.

headline: The FBI Once Tested Bigfoot Hair

headline: The Name 'Bigfoot' Came From a Prankster's Wooden Feet
coreClaim: The name "Bigfoot" came from 1958 footprints later revealed to be a prankster's carved wooden feet.

headline: One Shaky 1967 Film Still Divides People

headline: Narwhal Tusks Were Sold as Unicorn Horns
coreClaim: Prized medieval "unicorn horns" that sold for fortunes were really narwhal tusks.

headline: Scotland's National Animal Is the Unicorn
coreClaim: Scotland's official national animal is the mythical unicorn.

headline: Full Moons and Silver Bullets Came From Hollywood

headline: Tested 'Yeti' Remains Keep Turning Out to Be Bears

headline: 'Abominable Snowman' Is a Translation Mistake

headline: One 1951 Photo Launched the Yeti Craze

headline: Loki Gave Birth to Odin's Eight-Legged Horse
coreClaim: The trickster Loki turned into a mare and gave birth to Odin's eight-legged horse, Sleipnir.

headline: Most Norse Myths Were Written Down by a Christian
coreClaim: Most Norse mythology survives only because a Christian Icelander wrote it down around 1220.

headline: Valhalla's Warriors Train for a Battle They'll Lose
coreClaim: Norse warriors in Valhalla trained daily for Ragnarok — a final battle they were fated to lose.

headline: 'Nirvana' Literally Means 'to Blow Out'

headline: For Centuries, Buddhist Art Refused to Show the Buddha

headline: A Living Tree Grew From a Cutting Planted in 288 BCE
coreClaim: A living tree in Sri Lanka grew from a cutting of the Buddha's Bodhi tree planted in 288 BCE.

headline: The First Christian Symbol Was a Fish, Not a Cross
coreClaim: The first Christian symbol was a fish, not a cross — its Greek name an acronym for Jesus.

headline: The Bible's Table of Contents Took Centuries to Settle
coreClaim: The Bible's table of contents took centuries to settle — the 27 books weren't fixed until the 300s.
buriedGem: Gospels of Thomas and Mary left out.

headline: Christianity Went From Outlawed to Imperial in One Lifetime
coreClaim: Christianity went from outlawed to the Roman Empire's favored faith within one lifetime.

headline: 'Breakfast' Literally Means Breaking a Fast
coreClaim: "Breakfast" literally means breaking the overnight fast you keep while sleeping.
rationale: Concrete etymology hidden in a daily word.
buriedGem: The most universal fast of all.

headline: Ramadan Drifts Through Every Season
coreClaim: Ramadan follows the lunar calendar, drifting ~11 days earlier each year through every season.
rationale: Concrete; long summer fasts, then short winter ones.

headline: Gandhi Turned Refusing Food Into a Political Weapon
coreClaim: Gandhi turned refusing food into a political weapon — the hunger strike.
rationale: Concrete; the body as an argument.

headline: 'Hindu' Began as an Outsiders' Word for a River
coreClaim: "Hindu" began as an outsiders' word for the river Indus, not a religious label.

headline: Hindu Time Is Measured in Billions of Years
coreClaim: Hindu cosmology counts a single "day of Brahma" as billions of years — near Earth's real age.

headline: Hinduism's 'Millions of Gods' Are Often One Reality
coreClaim: Hinduism's "millions of gods" are often understood as faces of one reality, Brahman.

headline: Medieval Viewers Could 'Read' Paintings Without Words
coreClaim: Medieval viewers could "read" paintings without words — saints identified by signature objects.
rationale: Concrete; art as a visual language.

headline: The Swastika Was Sacred for 3,000 Years Before the Nazis
coreClaim: The swastika was a sacred good-fortune symbol for ~3,000 years before its 20th-century misuse.
buriedGem: From a Sanskrit word for well-being.

headline: The Glowing Halo Appeared in Many Cultures at Once
coreClaim: The glowing halo arose independently in Greek, Roman, Buddhist, and Christian art.
buriedGem: Surround a sacred head with light.

headline: Praying Toward Mecca Helped Advance Astronomy
coreClaim: Determining the qibla (prayer direction to Mecca) spurred medieval advances in astronomy.
buriedGem: A geometry puzzle on a curved planet.

headline: Millions of People Have Memorized the Entire Qurʾān
coreClaim: Millions of Muslims (hafiz) have memorized the entire Qurʾān word for word.
rationale: Concrete; the book lives in memory and sound.

headline: Two Million People Circle One Cube in the Same Few Days
coreClaim: The hajj gathers ~2 million people to circle one cube in the same few days.
rationale: Concrete; among the hardest logistics humans attempt.

headline: The Jewish Calendar Counts From the Creation of the World
coreClaim: The traditional Jewish calendar counts years from the creation of the world — now the 5780s.
buriedGem: A daily reminder of sacred history.

headline: Hebrew Came Back to Life After 1,700 Years
coreClaim: Hebrew came back to life after ~1,700 years — the only "dead" everyday language successfully revived.

headline: The Talmud Deliberately Records the Arguments It Rejected
coreClaim: The Talmud deliberately preserves the minority opinions that lost the debate.
rationale: Concrete; debate itself treated as sacred.

headline: One Holy Man Lived 37 Years on Top of a Pillar
coreClaim: The ascetic Simeon Stylites reportedly lived ~37 years atop a pillar.

headline: Monks Gave the World Champagne, Beer, and Genetics
coreClaim: Monks gave the world Champagne, famous beers, and — via Mendel's pea garden — genetics.

headline: Monks Hand-Copying Books Saved the Ancient World
coreClaim: Monks hand-copying manuscripts in scriptoria preserved much of the ancient world's knowledge.
buriedGem: Letter by letter through the "Dark Ages."

headline: An Egyptian Pharaoh May Have Been History's First Monotheist
coreClaim: An Egyptian pharaoh, Akhenaten (~1350 BCE), may have been history's first monotheist — then was erased.

headline: Belief in One God Often Grew Out of Many
coreClaim: Many ancient peoples were henotheists — worshipping one god while accepting others existed.

headline: Three Major Religions All Trace Back to Abraham
coreClaim: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam are all "Abrahamic," sharing Abraham and Jerusalem.
buriedGem: Jesus honored as a prophet in Islam.

headline: The Same Trickster Character Appears All Over the World
coreClaim: The clever "trickster" figure recurs across unconnected cultures — Anansi, Coyote, Hermes.
buriedGem: Separate peoples imagined the same troublemaker.

headline: Many Myths Exist to Explain a Ritual
coreClaim: Many myths ("etiological") exist to explain a ritual, making a custom feel ancient and authoritative.
buriedGem: The practice came first, the story after.

headline: Calling Something a 'Myth' Does Not Mean It Is False
coreClaim: In religious studies, "myth" means a meaningful sacred story — not necessarily a false one.
rationale: Concrete reframe of a common word.
buriedGem: About role and authority, not fact-checking.

headline: A Scallop Shell Marks Europe's Most Famous Pilgrimage
coreClaim: Camino de Santiago pilgrims have carried a scallop shell as their badge for over a thousand years.
buriedGem: A "pilgrim's passport," stamped along the way.

headline: The Largest Human Gathering on Earth Is a Pilgrimage
coreClaim: The largest human gathering on Earth is a pilgrimage — India's Kumbh Mela, visible from space.

headline: Medieval Pilgrimage Ran on a Booming Trade in Relics
coreClaim: Medieval pilgrimage ran on a relic trade so inflated critics joked the "True Cross" fragments could build a ship.

headline: The Word 'Bead' Originally Meant 'Prayer'
coreClaim: The word "bead" originally meant "prayer" — named for the prayers it counted.

headline: 'Rosary' Means 'Rose Garden'
coreClaim: "Rosary" means "rose garden" — prayers offered like a bouquet to the Virgin Mary.
buriedGem: Once counted on knotted cords or pebbles.

headline: Around the World, Prayer Beads Come in Sacred Numbers
coreClaim: Prayer beads use sacred counts — 108 (Hindu/Buddhist), 99 (Muslim), the rosary's groupings.

headline: The Word 'Religion' May Mean 'to Bind Together'
coreClaim: The word "religion" may come from the Latin religare, "to bind together."
rationale: Concrete etymology; fits its community role.
buriedGem: What binds people into a shared web.

headline: The Pretzel May Have Been Shaped Like Praying Arms
coreClaim: By tradition, monks shaped the pretzel to look like arms folded in prayer.
rationale: Concrete; religious symbolism in a snack.

headline: Hundreds of Thousands Once Declared Their Religion 'Jedi'
coreClaim: In several 2000s censuses, hundreds of thousands listed "Jedi" from Star Wars as their religion.
buriedGem: Sparked debate over what counts as a religion.

headline: Some Calendars Insert a Whole Extra Month
coreClaim: Lunisolar calendars (like the Hebrew) add a whole extra leap month 7 times every 19 years.
rationale: Concrete; grow a 13th month to stay seasonal.
buriedGem: Keeping sacred time and sky aligned.

headline: Right Now It Is Several Different Years at Once
coreClaim: Right now it's several different years at once — Gregorian 2020s, Jewish 5780s, Islamic 1440s.
rationale: Concrete; the year depends on your calendar.

headline: Fixing the Date of Easter Was a Major Medieval Science
coreClaim: Fixing the date of Easter was so hard that medieval scholars built a whole science, the computus.
rationale: Concrete; a single holy day drove astronomy.
buriedGem: Depends on both sun and moon.

headline: Rituals Calm the Mind Whether or Not You Believe
coreClaim: Structured rituals can measurably reduce anxiety and grief — even for nonbelievers.
buriedGem: Order and control when life feels chaotic.

headline: Every 'Rite of Passage' Shares the Same Three Stages
coreClaim: Rites of passage worldwide share three stages: separation, a "liminal" middle, and return.

headline: Doing a Ritual Together Synchronizes a Crowd
coreClaim: Acting in unison during ritual creates "collective effervescence," a bonding energy.
rationale: Concrete; ritual creates community, not just expresses it.

headline: A Shepherd's Stray Rock Uncovered 2,000-Year-Old Scriptures
coreClaim: A shepherd's stray rock uncovered the 2,000-year-old Dead Sea Scrolls in 1947.
rationale: Concrete, tellable; matched later copies closely.

headline: The Vedas Were Memorized Perfectly for 3,000 Years
coreClaim: India's Vedas were memorized near-perfectly for ~3,000 years before being written.
rationale: Concrete; chanted forwards, backwards, scrambled as error-checks.
buriedGem: A "book" that lived flawlessly in memory.

headline: Sikhs Treat Their Scripture as a Living Guru
coreClaim: Sikhs treat their scripture, the Guru Granth Sahib, as a living Guru — even giving it rest at night.
buriedGem: Placed on a throne by day.

headline: Gladiator Battles Came From Etruscan Traditions
coreClaim: Rome didn't invent gladiator games — it borrowed them from the older Etruscans.
buriedGem: Rome blew it up into Colosseum shows.

headline: You Can Still Walk on 2,000-Year-Old Roman Roads
coreClaim: Some Roman roads were built so well you can still walk on them 2,000 years later.
rationale: Overlaps human-civilization/roads-military-first (modern roads follow Roman routes).
proposedAngle: Keep one "Roman roads endure" fact across history and human-civilization.

headline: Viking Women Could Own Property and Divorce
coreClaim: Viking women could own property, seek divorce, and run the family farm.

headline: Ancient Athens Picked Officials by Lottery
coreClaim: Ancient Athens picked most officials by random lottery, not election.
buriedGem: Elections seemed less fair than chance.

headline: Wars Were Paused for the Ancient Olympics
coreClaim: Greek city-states paused their wars for a sacred truce so athletes could travel to the Olympics.

headline: Nobody Knew They Were Living in the Middle Ages
coreClaim: Nobody living in the Middle Ages knew they were in a "middle" age — the name came later.
rationale: Concrete; no era thinks itself the in-between.

headline: The Lecture and the Degree Were Invented in the Middle Ages
coreClaim: The lecture and the degree were invented in the "backward" Middle Ages.

headline: Beans Helped Trigger a Population Boom
coreClaim: Planting more beans helped trigger a medieval European population boom.

headline: It Took Years to Realize America Even Existed
coreClaim: It took years to realize America even existed — many still thought it was Asia.
buriedGem: Named for Vespucci, who argued it was new.

headline: Magellan Died Before His Voyage Circled the World
coreClaim: Magellan died midway through the first voyage around the world — one ship finished the loop.

headline: The Fall of Constantinople Helped Spark the Renaissance
coreClaim: The 1453 fall of Constantinople helped spark the Renaissance as Greek scholars fled west with ancient books.
buriedGem: One empire's fall fueled another's learning.

headline: Artists Invented Mathematical Perspective
coreClaim: Renaissance artists invented mathematical (linear) perspective to fake real depth on a flat wall.
buriedGem: Lines meeting at a vanishing point.

headline: The American Revolution Began as a Civil War
coreClaim: The American Revolution began as a civil war — Patriots vs Loyalists, neighbor against neighbor.
buriedGem: Colonists still saw themselves as British.

headline: A French Fleet Sealed Britain's Defeat at Yorktown
coreClaim: A French fleet, not a land battle, sealed Britain's defeat at Yorktown.
rationale: Concrete; control of the seas decided a land war.

headline: Paul Revere Didn't Ride Alone
coreClaim: Paul Revere didn't ride alone — and was actually stopped before finishing the famous ride.
buriedGem: A poem made him the star.

headline: Exporting Industrial Know-How Was Illegal
coreClaim: Britain made exporting its industrial machines and know-how illegal — one man rebuilt them from memory in America.
rationale: Concrete; hard to lock away an idea.

headline: The Word 'Luddite' Comes From Machine-Smashers
coreClaim: The word "Luddite" comes from real workers who smashed the machines they feared.

headline: China's First Emperor Was Buried With an Army
coreClaim: China's first emperor was buried with thousands of clay soldiers — each with a unique face.

headline: Gunpowder Began as a Chinese Discovery
coreClaim: Gunpowder began as a Chinese accident — alchemists hunting an immortality potion.
rationale: Cited in the topic-curation guide as a model; concrete, ironic.

headline: China Had One Female Emperor
coreClaim: China had one female emperor — Wu Zetian, who rose from concubine to declare her own dynasty.

headline: Some Indus Cities Had Sewage Systems
coreClaim: Some Indus Valley cities had private bathrooms and covered sewers 4,000 years ago.

headline: India Turned Zero From a Placeholder Into a Number
coreClaim: India turned zero from a placeholder into a true number you can calculate with.
proposedAngle: Keep one "India made zero a number" fact across history and mathematics.

headline: Chess Pieces Started as an Indian Army's Four Divisions
coreClaim: Chess pieces began as the four divisions of an ancient Indian army.

headline: Steppe Nomads Built History's Biggest Land Empire in a Few Generations
coreClaim: Steppe nomads built history's biggest connected land empire in just a few generations.

headline: The Mongols Didn't Need Huge Armies
coreClaim: The Mongols often won while outnumbered — with speed, discipline, and fake retreats.
rationale: Concrete; how an army fights beats its size.

headline: The Mongols Had a Massive Messenger Network
coreClaim: The Mongols' "yam" relay could carry news hundreds of miles in a day.
rationale: Concrete; one of the fastest pre-tech information systems.

headline: Nobody Who Traveled the Silk Road Ever Called It That
coreClaim: No one who traveled the Silk Road ever called it that — the name was coined in the 1800s.
buriedGem: A sprawling web, not one road.

headline: Buddhism Reached China Along the Silk Road
coreClaim: The Silk Road carried Buddhism from India to China, not just silk and spices.

headline: The Black Death May Have Traveled the Silk Road
coreClaim: The same Silk Road routes that moved silk may have carried the Black Death.
rationale: Concrete; links that enabled trade spread devastation.

headline: France Bankrupted Itself Helping America, Then Revolted
coreClaim: France bankrupted itself helping America beat Britain — then revolted.
rationale: Concrete; aiding one revolution triggered its own.

headline: France Stormed the Bastille to Free Just a Handful of Prisoners
coreClaim: France stormed the Bastille to free just a handful of prisoners — but it symbolized everything.

headline: Movable Type Existed Before Gutenberg
coreClaim: Movable metal type existed in Korea before Gutenberg was born.
buriedGem: His genius was a system to spread it.

headline: Gutenberg's Press First Appeared in a Lawsuit
coreClaim: One of the earliest records of Gutenberg's press is a lawsuit, not a book.
buriedGem: A money dispute preserved the history.

headline: Only About 40 Gutenberg Bibles Survive
coreClaim: Only about 40 Gutenberg Bibles survive — war, fire, and time destroyed the rest.

headline: George Orwell Coined 'Cold War'
coreClaim: George Orwell, author of 1984, coined the term "Cold War" in a 1945 essay.
rationale: Concrete; a novelist named the era.
buriedGem: A standoff never at peace, never open war.

headline: America's First Satellite Blew Up on Live TV
coreClaim: America's first satellite blew up on live TV, mocked as "Kaputnik."
rationale: Cited in the topic-curation guide as a model; concrete, tellable.
buriedGem: Rose four feet, then a fireball.

headline: One Apollo 11 Astronaut Never Walked on the Moon
coreClaim: One Apollo 11 astronaut never walked on the Moon — Michael Collins orbited alone, cut off from every human.

headline: Salem's Witch Hunt Turned on Its Own Respected Neighbors
coreClaim: Salem's witch hunt began with marginalized women, then turned on respected neighbors.
rationale: Concrete, if terse; how panic spreads.

headline: Salem Ended When "Ghost Evidence" Was Banned
coreClaim: Salem's trials collapsed when the court stopped accepting "spectral evidence" — claims about ghostly specters.
buriedGem: A new court barred the claims.

headline: Massachusetts Apologized Centuries Later
coreClaim: Massachusetts didn't fully exonerate the last convicted until 2001.
rationale: A dry procedural timeline of apology dates (1702, 1711, 1957, 2001); little "wait, really?"
proposedAngle: Re-hook the striking "300 years to apologize" angle up front, or trim as the weakest of three.

headline: Titanic Could Float With Four Compartments Flooded
coreClaim: Titanic could float with four compartments flooded — the iceberg breached five.
rationale: Concrete; the fatal margin by one.

headline: A Key Ice Warning Never Reached the Bridge
coreClaim: A key iceberg warning arrived hours before the crash but never reached Titanic's bridge.

headline: Titanic's First Lifeboat Left With 27 People in a Boat Built for 65
coreClaim: Titanic's first lifeboat left with 27 people in a boat built for 65.
rationale: Concrete; the seats existed and went empty.

headline: Samurai Became Powerful Enough to Run Japan
coreClaim: Japan's samurai became powerful enough to dominate the government for centuries.

headline: Many Samurai Became Bureaucrats During Peace
coreClaim: During 250 years of peace, many samurai became civil bureaucrats — and grew poorer on fixed stipends.

headline: Pirate Stories Were Already Exaggerated News
coreClaim: The fearsome pirate image was partly built by sensational 18th-century newspapers.

headline: Many Pirates Started Out With a Government License
coreClaim: Many pirates began with a government license (a letter of marque), then crossed into piracy.
rationale: Overlaps the pirates category's privateers facts (legal-raiders, blurred-line).
buriedGem: A thin line between raiding and piracy.
proposedAngle: Keep the privateering→piracy angle in one place (the dedicated pirates category).

headline: Black Bart Captured More Than 400 Vessels
coreClaim: Black Bart (Bartholomew Roberts) captured over 400 vessels before the navy caught him.
rationale: Concrete; one of the most successful pirates.

headline: Knights Started Training Around Age 7
coreClaim: A future knight could begin training as a page around age 7.
rationale: Concrete, if terse and somewhat known.
buriedGem: Dubbed a knight only after years of service.
